If the list is short, the section remains visually light with generous empty space at the bottom before the add-to-cart area, respecting the mockup’s airy editorial vibe. This entire block feeds naturally into the add-to-cart form below, making it clear that add-ons are optional but related to the current session configuration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left content column, following the ticket list. On smaller screens, it spans the full container width below the tickets, preserving the top-to-bottom reading and configuration flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price summary sits in the right column, directly beneath the inline-cart-summary, functioning as the lower portion of a single cohesive summary card. Together, they occupy about 35–40% of the content container’s width (roughly grid columns 9–12) and are aligned flush to the right edge of the central container. The price summary section begins with a subtle top-divider line or increased padding (16–24px) from the inline cart items above, then lists subtotals, taxes/fees (if any), and the final total. Lines of text are spaced 8–16px apart, with the final total given additional top spacing and slightly larger or bolder typography. Internal padding on all sides of this card area is generous (24–32px), mirroring the vertical image’s substantial presence in the mockup. There is clear negative space below the price summary (32–48px) before the bottom of the right column, so the card feels grounded and not cramped against the page edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the combined inline-cart-summary and price-summary card behaves as a sticky element within the right column: once the user scrolls past the hero bottom, the card remains in view, anchored about 80–96px from the top of the viewport, until the bottom of the content area is reached. On smaller screens, it loses sticky behavior and becomes an inline block positioned below the add-ons list (or below tickets if add-ons are absent).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form is the final section in the left column, positioned below the addons-list with a noticeable vertical gap of 40–56px to signal completion of configuration options. It spans the same width as the preceding left-column sections (about 60–65% of the container). Within this block, the primary 'Add to Cart' button is left-aligned but visually strong, with 16–24px of padding and enough horizontal space around it so it does not feel cramped. Any supplementary controls (e.g., quantity validation errors, helper text) appear above or immediately below the button with modest spacing (8–12px). The entire form area is relatively shallow compared to the lists above; it acts as a clean, decisive end-cap to the configuration flow. In relation to the right column, the button roughly lines up vertically with the total price figure in the price summary, encouraging a mental link between configuration (left) and outcome (right).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the left column on desktop. On smaller screens, a condensed version of the add-to-cart control (e.g., button plus key price) may optionally become a sticky footer for usability, but the core layout remains an inline section directly following the add-ons list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart feedback is displayed as a horizontal notification banner within the white content section, directly below the hero and above the back-to-tickets link and main two-column layout. It spans the full width of the central content container but is limited to a moderate height, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. The banner is centered horizontally, with text left-aligned inside and optional iconography (e.g., a small checkmark or warning symbol) on the left. There is at least 24–32px of whitespace above the banner (from the bottom edge of the hero) and another 24–32px below it before the back-to-tickets link, ensuring it is prominently visible without feeling intrusive. Its background color contrasts gently with the white section (e.g., soft green for success, pale red for error) but still harmonizes with the Christmas palette.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow. When displayed, it pushes the content below it downward; it does not overlay other components or remain fixed on scroll. On smaller screens, it remains full-width inside the content container with text wrapping gracefully.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline cart summary forms the upper portion of the right-hand card, aligned to the top of the right column in the white content section. It starts horizontally where the right column begins (about 35–40% of the container width from the left) and vertically aligns roughly with the top of the ticket-category-list in the left column, establishing a balanced two-column composition. This summary lists the user’s current selections—ticket types, quantities, and any chosen add-ons—in a vertical stack, each line separated by 8–12px. The entire summary sits within a card with a subtle drop shadow or border, sharing the same card container as the price-summary below. Internal padding on all sides is comfortable (24–32px), yielding a tall, visually substantial block reminiscent of the vertical historical image column in the mockup. Extra whitespace at the bottom of this summary area, before the price summary starts, keeps the content from feeling dense while enabling easy scanning of selected items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grouped with the price-summary as a single card that behaves as a sticky element in the right column on larger screens, remaining visible as the user scrolls down the left column. On smaller screens, it stacks below the configuration sections (tickets and add-ons), appearing above the add-to-cart form or immediately above the final CTA to keep context close to the decision point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-tickets link is positioned at the top-left of the white content section, directly below the hero (and any add-to-cart feedback banner, when present). It is aligned flush with the left edge of the central container, occupying only as much width as its text requires. There is approximately 32–40px of vertical space between the bottom of the hero and the link (or between the feedback banner and the link), ensuring it is distinct and easily discoverable without competing with the hero. A small left-pointing icon or chevron precedes the text, reinforcing its navigational function. Below this link, after a modest gap of 16–24px, the main two-column layout begins with the ticket-category-list heading appearing slightly indented but visually aligned with the link, creating a continuous left edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline at the top of the content section. It scrolls away with the rest of the page content and does not exhibit sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs (e.g., session not found or failed to load), the session-detail-error-state occupies the primary content region beneath the hero while maintaining the festive hero at the top. Inside the white content section, centered within the max-width container, a prominent error card appears, spanning roughly 60–70% of the container’s width and centered horizontally to create balanced whitespace on both sides. The card features generous padding (32–40px) and vertically stacks an icon or illustration at the top, a concise error heading beneath it, a short explanatory message, and a clear action (e.g., a button or link back to the ticket list). Vertical spacing between these elements is moderate (16–24px), maintaining the page’s editorial feel. There is ample whitespace above the card—about 64px from the bottom of the hero—and below it, giving the entire error state a calm, clear presence. When this state is active, the standard two-column layout (tickets, add-ons, summary, add-to-cart) is hidden so the user’s attention is focused entirely on resolving the error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content container, replacing the usual content. It scrolls normally and does not introduce overlays; the hero remains visible above to preserve the site’s structural continuity.

name: Ticket Type Details
title: Ticket Type Details
description: Displays full details for a selected ticket type (e.g., General Admission, VIP package), including what's included, perks, pricing, and a form to select quantities and add this ticket type to the shopping cart.

core_features: Read the `ticketTypeId` URL parameter and load the corresponding ticket type data using `getTicketTypeDetails`.
Display the ticket type's name, description, image (if provided), category, and availability status.
Show a structured 'What's Included' section using `formattedWhatsIncluded`, plus a list of perks and any indicators derived from `includesFreeDrink`.
Present clear adult and child pricing using `adultPrice` and `childPrice` from the `TicketType` data.
Provide quantity selectors for each applicable pricing category (adult, child) and preview the calculated line total before adding to cart.
Validate quantity selections and submit an add-to-cart request via `addTicketTypeToCart`, using the selected categories, quantities, and unit prices.
Display a confirmation area showing the newly added cart line (`addedItem`) and updated cart summary (`cartSummary`) after a successful add-to-cart operation.
Offer navigation back to the tickets overview page (`tickets.html`) for comparing with other ticket types, and navigation to the cart page (`cart.html`) once tickets have been added.
user_workflows:
View ticket type details and pricing:
1. User arrives at `ticket_type_detail.html` with a `ticketTypeId` URL parameter.
2. The page controller reads `ticketTypeId` and calls `getTicketTypeDetails(ticketTypeId)`.
3. While loading, the page shows a loading state instead of ticket content.
4. On success, the page displays the ticket type's name, description, image (if any), 'What's Included' content, perks, and pricing for adults and children (where available).
5. The user can review this information to compare value against what they remember from the Tickets & Pricing page.

Select ticket quantities and add to cart:
1. After reviewing details, the user scrolls to the quantity selection form.
2. The form shows entry fields for each available category (adult, and child if `childPrice` is provided by `TicketType`).
3. As the user adjusts quantities, the form recalculates a prospective line total based on the relevant unit prices from `TicketType`.
4. The user clicks the 'Add to Cart' action; the form validates that at least one quantity is greater than zero and that the ticket type is active.
5. The form constructs a `ticketSelections` array matching the API shape (each entry includes category, quantity, and unitPrice drawn from `TicketType`).
6. The page calls `addTicketTypeToCart(ticketTypeId, ticketSelections)`.
7. While the request is in progress, the form shows a submitting/disabled state.
8. On success, the page displays confirmation based on `addedItem.description`, `addedItem.lineTotal`, and `cartSummary`. It also surfaces any return `message`.
9. On error, the page shows a non-blocking error message and leaves the current quantity selections intact.

Navigate to compare ticket types and view cart:
1. If the user wants to compare with other ticket types, they click 'Back to Tickets & Pricing', which navigates to `tickets.html` without altering the current URL parameters.
2. After successfully adding tickets to the cart, the confirmation area surfaces a 'View Cart' control.
3. When the user clicks 'View Cart', the browser navigates to `cart.html` (no additional parameters required), where they can review all items and proceed with checkout.
4. The user can also use the browser Back button to return to the tickets list or other pages without losing server-side cart state.
interactions: Page load with `ticketTypeId` present: triggers `getTicketTypeDetails(ticketTypeId)`. UI enters a loading state until the request resolves.

Page load without `ticketTypeId` or with an invalid value: the controller detects the missing or unusable parameter and shows an error state with a link back to `tickets.html` instead of attempting API calls.
Successful `getTicketTypeDetails` response: populates all detail components (header, description, 'What's Included', perks, and pricing summary). Any prior error state is cleared.

Failed `getTicketTypeDetails` response (e.g., network error, ticket type inactive): the page shows an error message and a link back to `tickets.html`. Ticket-selection and add-to-cart components are disabled in this state.

Quantity field change (adult or child): updates the local `ticketSelections` draft state in the add-to-cart form. The form recalculates an estimated line total using the corresponding `adultPrice` or `childPrice` values from the loaded `TicketType`.
Click 'Reset' quantities (if provided): sets all quantity fields back to zero and clears any calculated line total preview, without calling any interface.

Click 'Add to Cart': validates that at least one quantity is non-zero and that the `TicketType.status` indicates it is active. If validation passes, calls `addTicketTypeToCart` with the current `ticketSelections`. If validation fails, shows inline validation messaging and aborts the request.

Successful `addTicketTypeToCart` response: shows a confirmation section using `addedItem.description` and `addedItem.lineTotal`, and renders an updated cart summary using `cartSummary` (itemsCount, subtotal, taxes, fees, total). The add-to-cart button returns to its normal state.
Failed `addTicketTypeToCart` response: displays `message` from the API if provided, or a generic failure message. The form remains editable so the user can adjust quantities and retry.
Click 'Back to Tickets & Pricing': navigates to `tickets.html` via a standard link (no API calls).
Click 'View Cart' in the confirmation/cart summary area: navigates to `cart.html` via a standard link (no additional parameters or API calls from this page).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is driven by the `ticketTypeId` URL parameter and the results of the two assigned interfaces. On initial load, the page controller reads `ticketTypeId` from the query string (for example, `ticket_type_detail.html?ticketTypeId=<value>`). If `ticketTypeId` is present, the page calls `getTicketTypeDetails(ticketTypeId)` and uses the response to populate `TicketType`-bound components. If `ticketTypeId` is empty or missing, the page does not call the interface and instead displays an error with navigation back to `tickets.html`. When a user changes quantities and submits the add-to-cart form, the page constructs the `ticketSelections` payload from the current form state and calls `addTicketTypeToCart` with the same `ticketTypeId` as in the URL, ensuring that the cart operation is always tied to the visible ticket type. The response from `addTicketTypeToCart` is stored in transient in-memory state (not persisted to URL parameters or localStorage) and used to render confirmation and cart summary; it does not change the URL. Navigating away (e.g., via 'Back to Tickets & Pricing' or 'View Cart') changes the page and may provide different `ticketTypeId` values upon return, which will cause a fresh call to `getTicketTypeDetails` using the new parameter value. No client-side state is persisted beyond the lifetime of the current page view except what is maintained server-side in the cart via `addTicketTypeToCart`.

components.functionality: Coordinates reading the `ticketTypeId` from the URL, calls `getTicketTypeDetails`, and manages global loading and error states for the Ticket Type Details page. Distributes loaded `TicketType` data and `formattedWhatsIncluded` to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read `ticketTypeId` from URL and decide whether to call `getTicketTypeDetails` or show a missing-parameter error.
onParamChange: If the page is re-used with a different `ticketTypeId` in the URL, re-trigger `getTicketTypeDetails` and reset child component state.
onLoadSuccess: Receive the `TicketType` and `formattedWhatsIncluded` from `getTicketTypeDetails` and propagate them to bound components.
onLoadError: Handle failures from `getTicketTypeDetails` by setting an error state, disabling the add-to-cart form, and showing navigation back to `tickets.html`.

components.functionality: Provides simple navigation controls for moving back to the tickets overview page and, after a successful add-to-cart, to the cart page.
data_binding: cartSummary (from addTicketTypeToCart.cartSummary, used to decide whether to emphasize 'View Cart')
event_handlers: onBackToTicketsClick: Navigate the browser to `tickets.html` without modifying the cart or other data.
onViewCartClick: Navigate the browser to `cart.html` so the user can see all cart contents.

components.functionality: Displays key ticket type information: name, optional category label, status (only if relevant to purchase availability), primary image, and description. Helps the user confirm they are looking at the correct ticket product.
event_handlers: onTicketTypeChange: Re-render header content whenever the `TicketType` object from `getTicketTypeDetails` changes (e.g., when `ticketTypeId` changes in the URL).

components.functionality: Renders the 'What's Included' details for the ticket type, including formatted descriptive content and a list of individual perks. Optionally highlights if a free drink is included based on `includesFreeDrink`.
event_handlers: onTicketTypeChange: Update the rendered 'What's Included' section whenever `TicketType` or `formattedWhatsIncluded` is updated by the page controller.

components.functionality: Displays the base prices for the ticket type by audience category, focusing on adult and (if available) child pricing. Used by the user to understand cost per person and to support manual comparison against other ticket types on the tickets overview page.
event_handlers: onTicketTypeChange: Refresh the pricing rows (including whether a child price row is shown) whenever the loaded `TicketType` changes.

components.functionality: Allows the user to specify how many tickets to purchase for each applicable category (adult and, if a child price exists, child). Maintains local draft `ticketSelections` based on user input and previews a calculated line total using the loaded prices.
event_handlers: onQuantityChange: For each category field change, update the internal draft `ticketSelections` and recompute an estimated line total as the sum of (quantity * unitPrice) across all categories.
onResetClick: Clear all quantity inputs back to zero and reset the estimated line total without issuing any API call.

onSubmitRequest: Package the current draft `ticketSelections` into the shape required by `addTicketTypeToCart` (categories, quantities, unit prices) and pass them to the parent add-to-cart component for submission.

components.functionality: Wraps the quantity selector, performs validation, and sends the add-to-cart request via `addTicketTypeToCart`. Manages submission state and exposes success or error results to downstream components.
event_handlers: onSubmit: Validate that the ticket type is active and that the aggregated quantity across all categories is greater than zero. If validation passes, call `addTicketTypeToCart` with `ticketTypeId` (from URL or `TicketType.id`) and the constructed `ticketSelections`, then set a submitting/loading state.

onAddSuccess: Receive the `addedItem`, `cartSummary`, and `message` from `addTicketTypeToCart`. Clear the submitting state, optionally clear or preserve quantities based on design choice, and propagate the results to the inline cart summary component.
onAddError: Clear the submitting state, keep the existing quantities, and display error messaging derived from the API's `message` field or a generic fallback.

components.functionality: Shows a brief confirmation and summarized cart information after a successful add-to-cart operation. Communicates what was just added and provides a quick overview of the cart totals to encourage checkout or continued shopping.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When new `addedItem` and `cartSummary` values arrive from `addTicketTypeToCart`, update the confirmation text and totals displayed.
onViewCartClick: Trigger navigation to `cart.html` so the user can review the complete cart and proceed to checkout.

components.functionality: Supports user comparison of ticket types by emphasizing the key value elements for this ticket (included perks and prices) and directing users back to the tickets overview page to see other ticket types side-by-side.
event_handlers: onBackToTicketsClick: Navigate to `tickets.html` to allow the user to compare this ticket's highlights with other ticket types presented on the overview page.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width, fixed-height hero at the top, followed by a white content area structured as a two-column layout. The hero carries the selected ticket type’s main title and key meta details, visually mirroring the 'Our History' hero treatment from the mockup. Directly below, the main content is arranged in a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) split into a wider left column for informational content ('What’s Included', explanations, comparison helper) and a narrower right column for transactional elements (pricing summary, quantity selection, add-to-cart, inline cart summary). Breadcrumbs sit near the transition between hero and content, aligned with the left column, maintaining the editorial, linear reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width, fixed-height hero at the top, followed by a white content area structured as a two-column layout. The hero carries the selected ticket type’s main title and key meta details, visually mirroring the 'Our History' hero treatment from the mockup. Directly below, the main content is arranged in a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) split into a wider left column for informational content ('What’s Included', explanations, comparison helper) and a narrower right column for transactional elements (pricing summary, quantity selection, add-to-cart, inline cart summary). Breadcrumbs sit near the transition between hero and content, aligned with the left column, maintaining the editorial, linear reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding (about 64px top and bottom) around the ticket title to give it strong prominence against the red bokeh background. The global content container beneath the hero is centered with ample side margins, constrained to about 70–80% of the viewport width up to a max of ~1120px. Within this, the left column takes about 60–65% of the content width for comfortable long-form reading, while the right column occupies 35–40% for the pricing and purchase module. Vertical gaps between major content blocks (hero to content, content sections) are around 48–64px; internal spacing between headings, text, and form elements follows the 8px baseline, typically 16–24px intervals.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low to medium, optimized for clarity and decision-making. Text blocks are not crowded and use a readable line length (~55–65 characters). The left informational column is airy, with clear separation between the 'What’s Included' content and the comparison helper. The right purchase column is visually denser but still padded, grouping pricing, quantity, and CTA into a single card-like block with consistent 16–24px spacing between controls to avoid clutter. White space above, below, and between sections reinforces an editorial, premium feel aligned with the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the top-left logo within the navigation (managed by the page controller) and then tracks horizontally across the top navigation. From there, attention is pulled downward to the large, centered ticket title in the hero. As the user scrolls or looks down, the breadcrumbs at the left edge of the content area subtly reinforce context, leading directly into the left-aligned 'What’s Included' heading. The reading flow moves down the left column through descriptive text, then naturally shifts right to the prominent pricing and add-to-cart panel, forming a mild Z-pattern similar to the mockup: nav → centered hero title → left informational content → right pricing/CTA panel. The comparison helper appears after the main information on the left, acting as a secondary decision aid once the user understands the ticket’s core value.

layout.overall_description: The Ticket Type Details page opens with a dramatic, full-width hero: a deep red, softly glowing bokeh Christmas background stretching across the top third to almost half of the viewport. A slim, semi-transparent navigation bar sits over the hero at the very top, with the logo on the left and navigation links aligned to the right. Centered vertically and horizontally within the hero, the selected ticket type’s name appears as a bold, elegant headline, possibly with a short subtitle beneath it, both set in clean, editorial typography. Below the hero, the background switches sharply to clean white. Aligned to the left edge of a centered max-width content container, a small breadcrumb trail appears with modest spacing above and below, acting as a understated anchor before the main content. The main body of the page is a two-column layout: the left column, slightly wider, contains a 'What’s Included' section with a left-aligned heading, a short descriptive lead, and detailed bullet points or paragraphs spaced generously for readability. Beneath this, within the same left column, a lighter comparison helper component offers guidance on how this ticket compares to other options. The right column begins at the same vertical starting point as the 'What’s Included' heading and forms a single stacked card with a soft border or subtle shadow: at the top, a concise pricing summary; directly below, a quantity selector; followed by a full-width, high-contrast add-to-cart button; and finally a more subdued inline cart summary. The two columns maintain a comfortable gutter between them, framed by wide white margins on either side, creating a balanced, editorial composition that echoes the mockup’s history page while adapting it for a product-detail, ticket-purchase experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the full-page structure. It places a full-width hero section at the very top of the page, spanning the entire viewport width with a fixed-height band occupying roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height. Inside this hero, content is horizontally centered within a max-width container (~1100–1200px), aligned to a 12-column grid. The navigation bar sits at the top edge of the hero, overlaying the background, with the logo fixed to the top-left and navigation links aligned to the top-right. Directly beneath the hero, the controller introduces a white background main content area that is also centered and constrained to the same max-width. Within this main content area, it establishes a two-column grid: a wider left column (approximately 60–65% of the container width) and a narrower right column (approximately 35–40%), separated by a comfortable gutter (~32px). All other components (breadcrumbs, informational content, pricing and forms) are positioned relative to this structure, maintaining consistent side margins and vertical section spacing of about 48–64px between major sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the structural wrapper for the hero and main content; the navigation may remain sticky at the top on scroll, but the controller itself follows normal document flow and manages the central 12-column grid and max-width constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs are positioned just below the hero, aligned with the left edge of the main content container, creating a visual bridge between the dramatic hero and the calmer white content area. They sit on the white background, not inside the hero, with approximately 24–32px of vertical padding above and below, so that there is clear breathing room between the bottom edge of the hero and the start of the two-column layout. Horizontally, the breadcrumb text occupies only a small portion of the left column width (roughly the first 25–30%), leaving ample white space to the right. This placement gently anchors the user’s sense of location before they encounter the larger headings and detailed content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, aligned to the left column of the main content; scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main details component is the visual centerpiece inside the hero. It is vertically and horizontally centered within the hero’s max-width inner container. The ticket type name appears as a large, bold, centrally aligned headline spanning roughly 60–70% of the container width, with each line kept to a comfortable width so it doesn’t stretch edge to edge. Below the main title, a short line of supporting meta information (for example, season dates or a short descriptor like 'Evening General Admission') appears in smaller type, also center-aligned and spaced about 16–24px beneath the main heading. There is generous vertical padding above and below this block (around 48–64px), giving the hero a tall, spacious look and ensuring that the title remains the dominant focal point against the rich red bokeh background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in position relative to the hero section, centered; it scrolls off the screen as the user moves downward with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'What’s Included' section sits at the very top of the left column in the white content area, starting roughly 16–24px below the breadcrumbs. It is left-aligned within the left column’s width (approximately 60–65% of the overall content container). A section heading ('What’s Included' or ticket-specific variant) appears at the top, bold and slightly larger than body text, with about 8–16px of space separating it from the breadcrumb area above. Directly beneath, an optional short lead-in or summary sentence may appear, spaced 8–16px below the heading, followed by a more detailed list or paragraphs. Bullet lists or paragraphs are arranged in a single column with a line length around 55–65 characters, ensuring comfortable reading. Vertical spacing between list items or paragraphs is around 8–16px, maintaining an airy editorial feel. The overall block can extend vertically as needed, forming the main narrative spine of the page on the left, with its top edge aligning horizontally with the top of the pricing panel in the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, forming the top of the main text flow. As the user scrolls, this content scrolls normally while the right-hand purchase panel may remain partially visible if sticky behavior is implemented.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing summary is positioned at the top of the right column, aligned horizontally with the 'What’s Included' heading in the left column. It sits inside a visually unified card that fills the full width of the right column (roughly 35–40% of the content container). Within this card, the pricing summary occupies the upper portion: a short title or label (e.g., 'Ticket Price') at the top, followed by the main price in a larger, bold type, and any secondary pricing details (tax notes, per-person descriptors) beneath in smaller text. The content is left-aligned inside the card with generous internal padding (about 24–32px on all sides), giving the panel a solid, contained appearance. There is clear visual separation (e.g., extra spacing or a subtle rule) between the pricing summary and the form elements below it (quantity selector and add-to-cart), though all belong to the same column card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed within a right-column panel that may use sticky positioning on larger viewports: the top of the panel remains pinned with a small offset from the top of the viewport while the user scrolls through the taller left-column content, but on smaller screens it behaves as a normal block stacking beneath the informational content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity selector resides directly below the pricing summary within the same right-column card. It is left-aligned and spans most of the card’s inner width, with approximately 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the bottom of the pricing summary. Label text (e.g., 'Quantity') sits above the input control with about 8px of spacing, and the actual selector (e.g., stepper, dropdown, or number field with plus/minus controls) stretches horizontally, but still respects the card’s internal padding, leaving consistent inset margins on the left and right. There is another 16–24px gap between the quantity selector and the add-to-cart button below, keeping the controls visually distinct yet tightly grouped as a purchase flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right-column card, stacking vertically between the pricing summary above and the add-to-cart form below; it moves together with the card if sticky behavior is applied at the panel level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form is the focal call-to-action area in the right column, positioned directly below the quantity selector within the same card. It begins approximately 16–24px beneath the quantity controls. The primary add-to-cart button spans nearly the full inner width of the card (subtracting padding), running from left inset to right inset, and is centered vertically within its own sub-block. The button height is generous (around 48px), with clear, high-contrast text and a festive accent color that harmonizes with the red theme but stands out on the white card background. Any supporting text (e.g., 'Secure checkout', 'No fees added at checkout') appears in smaller font beneath the button with about 8–12px spacing, aligned left. The entirety of this sub-section feels compact but uncrowded, clearly tied to the pricing and quantity controls above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and tightly coupled to the pricing and quantity sections within the right-column card. It moves with the card if the panel is sticky, remaining visible while the user scrolls through left-column content on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline cart summary sits at the bottom of the right-column card, underneath the add-to-cart form. It is separated from the button area by a subtle top border or a slightly increased vertical gap of about 16–24px, creating a visual distinction between the primary action and informational status. The summary text is smaller and lighter in weight than the main body copy in the card, perhaps occupying one to three short lines, aligned left and spanning the card’s width within the internal padding. It might display a brief status such as 'You have 2 items in your cart' or 'Cart total: $XX.XX'. The space around it remains generous enough that it feels like a footer to the card rather than a crowded add-on.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the bottom of the right-column card. It scrolls with the card and remains within the sticky panel’s view if sticky behavior is implemented for the whole right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison helper is positioned in the left column, directly below the main 'What’s Included' body content. After the last paragraph or bullet list in the informational section, a vertical gap of about 32–48px introduces this helper as a distinct but related block. It spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the overall content container) and appears as a subtle bordered or lightly shaded panel to differentiate it from the plain text above. Inside, a short heading (e.g., 'Not sure if this is the right ticket?') appears at the top with about 8–16px of space above the explanatory text or links. Below, a concise body copy line and perhaps a small set of horizontally arranged comparison links or buttons are aligned to the left. The helper’s visual weight is intentionally lighter than the hero and purchase panel so that it reads as an optional aid, not a competing focal point. On taller screens, the bottom of this helper may visually align roughly with or slightly below the bottom of the right-column card, giving the overall layout a balanced baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column. It scrolls normally and has no sticky behavior; on narrower screens it will stack below the primary informational content and either above or below the right-column purchase panel depending on the responsive stacking logic.

name: Shopping Cart
title: Shopping Cart
description: Page that shows all items currently in the visitor’s cart, allows updating ticket quantities or removing items, and displays an up-to-date pricing summary before proceeding to checkout.

core_features: Retrieve and display the current cart contents, including each item’s description, ticket selections, add-on summaries, line totals, and overall pricing (subtotal, taxes, fees, total) using getCartSummary.
Allow users to adjust ticket quantities for each cart item by editing the ticketSelections array and submitting updates via updateCartItemTickets, then refresh the full cart view.
Allow users to remove individual cart items via removeCartItem, then refresh the cart to reflect the updated contents and totals.
Show a clear empty-cart state when there are no items, with navigation options back to home and to the tickets listing.
Provide clear navigation to continue browsing tickets (to tickets.html) and a pathway into the checkout flow (destination managed by the host application).
Reflect navigation-related state through URL parameters (e.g., highlighting a specific item, redirecting when the cart is empty) without using localStorage.
user_workflows: View current cart contents: 1) User navigates to cart.html (e.g., via header Cart link or a link after adding tickets). 2) On page load, cart-page-controller calls getCartSummary with no parameters. 3) The response data is bound to ShoppingCart and CartItem-based components. 4) cart-items-list renders each cart-item-row with description, ticketSelections, addOnSummaries, and lineTotal. 5) cart-totals-summary shows subtotal, taxes, fees, and total from the same response.

Update ticket quantities for a cart item: 1) User locates the desired item in cart-items-list. 2) In cart-item-row, user changes one or more quantity inputs associated with ticketSelections (e.g., for specific categories). 3) User triggers the update (e.g., by leaving the field, pressing an Update button, or similar event in cart-item-row). 4) cart-item-row aggregates the updated ticketSelections array and calls updateCartItemTickets with parameters {cartItemId, ticketSelections}. 5) On success, cart-page-controller optionally reads cartSummary totals from the response, then immediately calls getCartSummary again to refresh the full items list and line-level totals. 6) cart-notifications displays any message returned from updateCartItemTickets, and the updated cart items and totals appear.

Remove a cart item: 1) User clicks a Remove or similar control on a specific cart-item-row. 2) cart-item-row optionally asks for confirmation (client-side). 3) On confirmation, cart-item-row calls removeCartItem with parameter {cartItemId}. 4) On success, cart-page-controller reads the returned cartSummary (including itemsCount and pricing), then calls getCartSummary to fetch the updated detailed items. 5) The removed item disappears from cart-items-list; totals in cart-totals-summary update. 6) If the resulting itemsCount is zero, cart-items-list hides and cart-empty-state is displayed instead. 7) cart-notifications can show any message returned from removeCartItem.

Continue browsing tickets from the cart: 1) From any non-empty or empty state, user clicks the Continue Browsing Tickets control. 2) cart-navigation-actions or cart-empty-state reads current URL parameters (such as source or highlightItem) if needed and constructs a navigation URL to tickets.html, optionally appending context parameters (e.g., returnTo=cart). 3) Browser navigates to tickets.html; the cart state remains on the server/session and can be viewed again later via cart.html.

Handle empty-cart state and navigation to home: 1) User navigates to cart.html. 2) getCartSummary returns an empty items array. 3) cart-page-controller sets state to empty; cart-empty-state component is rendered instead of cart-items-list. 4) cart-empty-state shows a message that the cart has no items and exposes a Back to Home action. 5) On Back to Home click, the component navigates to index.html, optionally preserving or cleaning URL parameters.

Proceed from cart to checkout: 1) User reviews cart-items-list and cart-totals-summary. 2) User clicks a Proceed to Checkout control exposed by cart-totals-summary or cart-navigation-actions. 3) The page triggers navigation into the checkout flow (for example, to a checkout.html route or equivalent), optionally including URL parameters such as cartId (from getCartSummary) or returnFrom=cart. 4) No new interfaces are called from cart.html for checkout itself; subsequent steps are handled on the checkout page.
interactions: On page load, call getCartSummary with no parameters to retrieve the current ShoppingCart contents; render either the list view or empty state depending on whether items are present.

When a quantity input for a ticket selection changes in cart-item-row, update the local representation of the ticketSelections array for that item; when the user confirms the change, call updateCartItemTickets(cartItemId, ticketSelections).

After a successful updateCartItemTickets response, trigger a refresh by calling getCartSummary again so that all item descriptions, line totals, and cart totals are consistent with server-side calculations.
When the Remove control is activated for a cart item, call removeCartItem(cartItemId); on a successful response, call getCartSummary again to repopulate the list and totals with the updated state.
Display success or error messages from updateCartItemTickets and removeCartItem via cart-notifications, and clear or replace messages as new actions occur.
Respond to URL parameter highlightItem by automatically focusing or scrolling to the corresponding cart-item-row after getCartSummary has loaded and items are rendered.

Respond to URL parameter redirectWhenEmpty by automatically redirecting the user to tickets.html or index.html if the cart is empty after getCartSummary, instead of or in addition to displaying the empty state.
When Continue Browsing Tickets is clicked, navigate to tickets.html (optionally including returnTo=cart or source=cart in the URL query string) without modifying the cart via interfaces.
When Back to Home is clicked from the empty state, navigate to index.html (optionally preserving or cleaning URL parameters) without calling any cart interfaces.

When Proceed to Checkout is clicked, navigate to the checkout route with optional URL parameters (such as cartId) taken from getCartSummary; no additional cart interface calls are required at that moment.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary server-side cart identity is implicit in getCartSummary and is not controlled by URL parameters. URL parameters on cart.html are used purely for UI and navigation state and never for storing cart data. Example parameters: 1) highlightItem={cartItemId}: On initial load (or when the URL changes), cart-page-controller reads this parameter. After getCartSummary returns and cart-items-list is rendered, it checks whether any CartItem.id matches highlightItem; if so, it instructs cart-items-list to scroll that cart-item-row into view and optionally apply a temporary visual highlight or focus for accessibility. 2) redirectWhenEmpty={home|tickets}: After getCartSummary resolves, if the returned items array is empty and redirectWhenEmpty is present, cart-page-controller automatically redirects the user to index.html (when value is home) or tickets.html (when value is tickets) instead of rendering the empty state; if redirectWhenEmpty is absent, the empty state is shown normally. 3) source={string}: When present, this parameter indicates where the user came from (for example, tickets or session), and may be used to adjust navigation decisions (e.g., deciding which page to use for the Continue action) or to slightly adjust explanatory text; it does not affect any interface calls. 4) focusSection={items|totals}: When present, cart-page-controller passes this to the appropriate component to move visual focus to either the cart-items-list or the cart-totals-summary area after load, improving accessibility. URL parameters are read on initial load and whenever the browser’s history state changes (e.g., via back/forward navigation); no state is stored in localStorage or other client-side persistence. All cart content and pricing data comes exclusively from getCartSummary, updateCartItemTickets, and removeCartItem.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching and page-level state for the Shopping Cart page. On initial load and after any mutation (updateCartItemTickets or removeCartItem), it calls getCartSummary to obtain the full ShoppingCart contents, binds the response to child components, interprets URL parameters (highlightItem, redirectWhenEmpty, source, focusSection), decides whether to show the empty state or items list, and triggers focus or navigation behaviors as needed.

Renders the collection of cart items returned in getCartSummary.items. For each array element, it instantiates a cart-item-row with the provided cartItemId, itemType, description, ticketSelections, addOnSummaries, and lineTotal. If the items array is empty, it does not render item rows and instead signals cart-page-controller to show cart-empty-state.

Represents a single CartItem in the cart. It displays the item’s description as supplied by getCartSummary, an editable list of ticketSelections (each with category, quantity, unitPrice), a read-only list of addOnSummaries, and the lineTotal. Users can change quantities for one or more ticketSelections; when they confirm the change, the component aggregates the current ticketSelections array and calls updateCartItemTickets for this cartItemId via the controller. It also exposes a Remove control that triggers removeCartItem for the current cartItemId. After success responses, it relies on cart-page-controller to refresh the entire cart via getCartSummary.

Displays when getCartSummary returns a ShoppingCart with no items. Shows a simple explanatory message that the cart is empty and presents navigation options Back to Home (to index.html) and Continue Browsing Tickets (to tickets.html). It does not call any cart interfaces itself; it only reads the current state and URL parameters (for example, source or redirectWhenEmpty) to adjust messaging or behavior.

Shows the pricing summary for the cart using subtotal, taxes, fees, and total fields returned by getCartSummary. It updates whenever cart-page-controller refreshes the cart. It also exposes a Proceed to Checkout action that initiates navigation into the checkout flow, optionally including the cartId from getCartSummary in the destination URL. It does not modify the cart itself.

Provides persistent navigation controls for users reviewing their cart, typically rendered alongside or below the cart items and totals when the cart is not empty. It includes a Continue Browsing Tickets action that navigates to tickets.html (optionally adding URL parameters such as returnTo=cart or source=cart) and may also contain a Proceed to Checkout action that delegates to the same handler used in cart-totals-summary. It does not perform any cart interface calls.

Displays user-facing feedback messages resulting from cart operations. When updateCartItemTickets or removeCartItem returns a message field or indicates failure via success=false, cart-page-controller passes the message and severity to cart-notifications to display above or near the cart content. It can show success confirmations (e.g., that quantities were updated) or error messages (e.g., if the update fails). Messages are cleared or replaced when new operations occur or when the user dismisses them.

Reads and interprets URL parameters related to the cart page (highlightItem, redirectWhenEmpty, source, focusSection) and informs other components how to respond. After getCartSummary returns, it helps cart-page-controller decide whether to scroll to a specific cart-item-row, automatically redirect when the cart is empty, or move focus to cart-items-list or cart-totals-summary. It does not call any interfaces or modify cart data and never writes to localStorage; all state adjustments are done via URL parsing and in-memory state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s hero-then-content pattern. At the top, a full-width Christmas-themed hero band contains the global site navigation overlaid at the top and a large, centered 'Your Cart' title. Below the hero, the page transitions into a white-background main section with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px). Inside this container, content is split into a two-column layout: the left, wider column is dedicated to the cart-items-list (and, when applicable, the cart-empty-state); the right, narrower column holds the cart-totals-summary. Above these columns, the cart-notifications component spans the container width. Below the columns, a full-width cart-navigation-actions bar provides cart-specific navigation and primary CTAs.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s hero-then-content pattern. At the top, a full-width Christmas-themed hero band contains the global site navigation overlaid at the top and a large, centered 'Your Cart' title. Below the hero, the page transitions into a white-background main section with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px). Inside this container, content is split into a two-column layout: the left, wider column is dedicated to the cart-items-list (and, when applicable, the cart-empty-state); the right, narrower column holds the cart-totals-summary. Above these columns, the cart-notifications component spans the container width. Below the columns, a full-width cart-navigation-actions bar provides cart-specific navigation and primary CTAs.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding around the 'Your Cart' heading and minimal vertical height for the nav overlay. The main content container sits on a white background with large left/right margins and a max width of about 1100–1200px, centered in the viewport. Inside this container, the left column for items takes approximately 60–65% of the container width, and the right column for the totals summary takes about 35–40%. Vertical spacing between the hero and the main content section, between the notifications and the columns, and between the columns and the bottom navigation actions is generous (48–64px). Within each column, individual elements (headings, lists, rows, buttons) use moderate vertical spacing (8–24px) to keep the layout open and readable.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a low-to-medium content density, mirroring an editorial article feel. Text blocks (item titles, pricing details, instructions) respect comfortable line lengths, and each cart-item-row uses ample padding so rows feel distinct, not crowded. The right-column summary is compact but not cramped: key totals are clearly separated by subtle spacing. Notifications and empty states are given breathing room so they read like clear, standalone messages. Overall, the design favors clarity and ease of scanning over packing in maximum information per viewport.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts with the site logo in the top-left of the hero’s navigation, then moves right along the nav links. From there it is drawn down to the large, centered 'Your Cart' hero title. Scrolling slightly brings the user into the white main content area: first encountering any cart-notifications spanning the top of the content container, then naturally moving down into the left column where the cart items (or empty state) live. As the user reads down through the item list, the right-column totals summary sits in peripheral view, drawing secondary attention and supporting a soft Z-shaped flow: hero center → down-left items → across to right totals → downwards into the bottom cart-navigation-actions bar.

layout.overall_description: The Shopping Cart page opens on a full-width, deep red bokeh Christmas hero, with the global navigation sitting as a slim overlay at the very top. Centered within this hero, the 'Your Cart' title is given generous vertical padding so it feels like a festive chapter heading in an editorial spread. As the user scrolls, the hero fades into a clean white content section where a centered, max-width container holds all cart functionality. At the top of this container, any cart notifications appear as a full-width strip of lightly styled messages. Below, the content splits into two columns: on the left, a wide, text-priority column lists all cart items as a vertical stack of spacious rows; on the right, a narrower column presents a tidy, card-like totals summary aligned to the top of the first row of items. The surrounding whitespace keeps both columns feeling calm and readable, with comfortable gaps between rows. At the bottom of the content container, a full-width cart navigation bar provides the main actions—continuing shopping or proceeding to checkout—grounding the page with a clear next step while preserving the airy, editorial feel inspired by the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-controller is a non-visual, behind-the-scenes component. It does not render any UI or occupy physical space in the layout. Functionally, it wraps the entire cart page, orchestrating data loading and feeding state into all visual components (cart-items-list, cart-empty-state, cart-totals-summary, cart-notifications, and cart-navigation-actions) that live within the hero and main content sections. In the visual hierarchy, it acts like an invisible outer frame around the page rather than a visible element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic component with no spatial footprint; participates only in data and state orchestration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-items-list sits in the left column of the white main content section, directly below any cart-notifications. It is aligned to the top of the right-column totals summary so their top edges visually line up. The list’s column occupies roughly 60–65% of the centered content container width and stretches from near the top of the main section downwards, growing vertically as more items are added. Each side of the column has generous horizontal padding (24–32px) from the container edge, and there is ample whitespace between the list and the right column (a gutter of about 24–32px). Inside this column, the items list flows as a vertical stack of cart-item-row components with consistent gaps between rows, giving the left column a comfortable, editorial rhythm rather than a dense table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal document flow; expands vertically to fit the number of items and pushes subsequent content (like the bottom navigation bar) downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row appears as a horizontally oriented card within the cart-items-list’s left column. It spans the full width of that column, with internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides and a subtle divider or soft card edge separating it from adjacent rows. The row’s interior is divided into a content-first layout: the left portion (roughly 60–70% of the row width) includes the ticket or product name, visit date/time, and any descriptive details, stacked vertically with modest spacing. The right portion (about 30–40% of the row width) aligns quantity controls, per-item price, subtotal, and a remove action, typically right-aligned to form a clean edge. Vertically, rows are tall enough (around 80–120px depending on content) to preserve readability and touch targets, with 8–16px of vertical space between consecutive rows to echo the airy editorial style rather than a cramped list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the cart-items-list as a repeating stacked element; no fixed or sticky behavior. Row height expands slightly as needed to accommodate longer labels while maintaining consistent left and right alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the cart-empty-state occupies the same left-column area where the cart-items-list would normally appear. It is positioned directly beneath any cart-notifications and aligned with the top of where the first item row would be. The empty state content is centered within this left column—both horizontally and with generous vertical padding (at least 64–96px above and below the core message)—to create a calm, open space. A small festive illustration or icon can sit above a clear explanatory message ('Your sleigh is empty'), followed by a primary action encouraging the visitor to browse or purchase tickets. The right column may either hide or display a minimized totals summary; if shown, the empty state visually dominates the central area, with the right side reading as supporting context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the cart-items-list in the left column; occupies its space in normal flow and pushes the bottom cart-navigation-actions downward. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-totals-summary is placed in the right column of the main content section, directly to the right of the cart-items-list or cart-empty-state, with a consistent horizontal gutter of about 24–32px between columns. Its column occupies roughly 35–40% of the centered content container width. The card is top-aligned so that its top edge matches the top of the first cart-item-row (or the top of the empty state), reinforcing a cohesive two-column grid. Within the card, content is stacked vertically: a 'Order Summary' heading at the top, followed by line items (subtotal, taxes, fees, discounts), and a prominent 'Total' line near the bottom. Each line has clear separation (8–16px of vertical spacing) and the card uses ample internal padding (24–32px) to keep the summary readable and substantial. The card height is visually shorter than the full height of the items list, creating a clear primary/secondary relationship but remaining highly visible in the right peripheral view as the user scans the items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the right column within normal document flow, aligned to the top of the items column. On longer carts, it scrolls away with the page content rather than sticking to the viewport, maintaining the simple editorial pattern of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-navigation-actions component appears as a full-width bar within the white main content section, positioned directly below the two-column area that contains the cart-items-list/cart-empty-state and the cart-totals-summary. It is horizontally centered within the main content container and spans nearly the entire container width, with consistent left and right padding matching the container (24–32px). A generous vertical gap (about 48–64px) separates it from the content above, making it feel like a distinct conclusion to the page. Inside the bar, actions are laid out on a horizontal axis: secondary actions (like 'Continue Shopping' or 'Back to Tickets') align toward the left, while the primary action (such as 'Proceed to Checkout') aligns to the right, creating a clear visual emphasis. The bar itself can be subtly distinguished with a very light background tint or top border, staying consistent with the restrained editorial style rather than a heavy, app-like footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block anchored at the bottom of the main content section. It moves up or down based on content height and does not stick to the viewport, preserving the scroll-based narrative layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-notifications component is placed at the top of the white main content section, directly below the hero and above the two-column layout. It spans the full width of the centered content container, maintaining the same left/right margins and padding (24–32px) as the columns below. When active, one or more notification banners stack vertically here, each styled as a low-height strip or pill with clear but understated background colors (green for success, red for errors) that harmonize with the warm, festive palette. There is moderate vertical spacing (8–12px) between multiple notifications and around the entire notification block so it feels visually separate but closely associated with the cart content. The presence of notifications gently pushes the items and totals sections down, preserving the generous spacing and editorial flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow component that appears and disappears at the top of the main content. When hidden, it collapses completely, allowing the two-column layout to move upward without leaving empty gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-url-state-handler is purely functional and does not render visual UI. It sits logically alongside the cart-page-controller at the page level, managing synchronization between the browser’s URL (e.g., query parameters, steps, or coupon codes) and the cart state. It has no visual presence and does not influence spacing or alignment of the visible components; instead, it ensures that interactions like updating quantities or applying codes are reflected correctly in the URL and vice versa.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic component with no spatial presence; operates entirely in the background to sync state with the URL.

name: Schedule & Activities
title: Schedule & Activities
description: Page to select a visit date, apply filters, and browse scheduled activities, with options to view details and add activities to a personal "My Night" itinerary.

core_features: Initialize schedule filters and time-range presets by calling getScheduleFilterOptions and binding the results to filter controls.
Read URL parameters (date, audience, timeStart, timeEnd, category, locationType) on load to pre-select the visit date and filters.
Provide a date picker that controls the schedule date and triggers a fresh searchActivities call whenever the selected date changes.
Provide filter controls (audience, time range, category, location type) that map directly to searchActivities parameters and are reflected in the page URL.
Call searchActivities with the current date and filter values to retrieve a list of activities and display them as cards with key details and duration.
Allow users to click an activity card to navigate to activity_detail.html with the corresponding activityId.
Allow users to add an activity to their "My Night" itinerary via addActivityToItinerary, updating an inline itinerary summary for the selected date.
Provide a "View My Night" navigation action that sends users to itinerary.html with the currently selected schedule date as a URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Landing on the Schedule with pre-selected filters:
1. User arrives on schedule.html, possibly with URL parameters from another page.
2. The page controller reads date, audience, timeStart, timeEnd, category, and locationType from the URL query string.
3. The page calls getScheduleFilterOptions and populates the date picker and filter controls.
4. The controller normalizes the read URL parameters against the available filter options (e.g., matches audience/category IDs if present).
5. The date picker is set to the date URL parameter if present; otherwise, an implementation-defined default date is used.
6. The filter controls are set according to their corresponding URL parameters if values are valid.
7. The page calls searchActivities using the resolved date and filter values.
8. The returned activities are rendered as a list/grid of activity cards; the user sees what is happening on that date with the chosen filters.

Workflow 2 – Adjusting date and filters to refine activities:
1. User changes the date using the date picker.
2. The controller updates the internal state for the selected date.
3. The page updates the URL query string to include the new date and preserve current filter values, using history.replaceState or a similar mechanism.
4. The page calls searchActivities with the new date and existing filter values.
5. The results list re-renders with activities for the newly selected date.
6. Similarly, when the user changes a filter (audience, time range, category, or location type), the filter state and URL query string are updated.
7. Each change triggers a new searchActivities call and updates the activity list accordingly.

Workflow 3 – Viewing activity details:
1. User scans the list of activity cards returned from searchActivities.
2. The user clicks on a card (or a dedicated "View details" control on that card).
3. The page reads the selected activity's id from the bound Activity model.
4. The browser navigates to activity_detail.html with activityId set in the query string (e.g., activity_detail.html?activityId={id}).

Workflow 4 – Building "My Night" itinerary from the schedule:
1. User finds an activity of interest in the list returned by searchActivities.
2. If canAddToItinerary is true for that activity, the "Add to My Night" control is enabled on its card.
3. The user clicks "Add to My Night" on that activity card.
4. The page calls addActivityToItinerary with the corresponding activityId.
5. When the call returns, the page reads the success flag and itinerary data (including items and any conflict flags).
6. The inline itinerary summary component is updated to reflect the returned itinerary items for that date (e.g., count of activities, conflict indicators) without persisting anything beyond the current page session.
7. If conflicts are reported for the newly added item, the component highlights the conflict status (e.g., via text or icon), using the hasTimeConflict and conflictsWithActivityIds values.
8. The "View My Night" link becomes enabled or visually emphasized when at least one activity is in the itinerary for the selected date.
9. When the user is ready, they click "View My Night" to navigate to itinerary.html?date={selectedDate}.

Workflow 5 – Returning from Itinerary or other pages with a date:
1. User comes from itinerary.html, index.html, about.html, or faq.html using a link that includes one or more of the supported URL parameters.
2. The schedule page again reads and applies the incoming parameters to re-construct the same schedule state (date and filters).
3. The page re-executes getScheduleFilterOptions (for options) and searchActivities (for activities) to show consistent results for the given context.
interactions:
Date selection:
- Interaction: User selects a date via the date picker.
- Behavior: onDateChange handler updates internal selected date state, updates the date parameter in the URL, and calls searchActivities with the new date and current filters.

Audience filter change:
- Interaction: User selects or clears an audience option from an audience filter control (e.g., dropdown or pills).
- Behavior: onAudienceChange handler updates internal audience state (either to the selected audience ID or to null), reflects this in the URL audience parameter (adding, changing, or removing it), then calls searchActivities with the updated audience value.

Time range selection:
- Interaction: User selects a time-range preset or manually adjusts start and end times.
- Behavior: onTimeRangeChange handler maps UI selection to timeStart and timeEnd values, updates the URL timeStart/timeEnd parameters, and calls searchActivities with timeRangeStart and timeRangeEnd mapped from these values.

Category filter change:
- Interaction: User selects or clears an activity category.
- Behavior: onCategoryChange handler updates the internal category state and URL category parameter, then triggers a new searchActivities call.

Location type filter change:
- Interaction: User selects indoor, outdoor, or other location-type options.
- Behavior: onLocationTypeChange handler updates the internal locationType state, updates the URL parameter, and triggers searchActivities.

List refresh / reset filters:
- Interaction: User uses a "Clear filters" or similar control.
- Behavior: onClearFilters handler resets audience, time range, category, and locationType to their default (unfiltered) state, removes those parameters from the URL, and calls searchActivities with only the date parameter (and any reset defaults as appropriate).

Activity card click (details navigation):
- Interaction: User clicks anywhere on the activity card or a dedicated detail link.
- Behavior: onActivityCardClick handler reads the bound Activity.id and performs a navigation to activity_detail.html?activityId={Activity.id}.

Add to My Night:
- Interaction: User clicks the "Add to My Night" action on an activity card for which canAddToItinerary is true.
- Behavior: onAddToItineraryClick handler invokes addActivityToItinerary with activityId, receives the itinerary object, updates the inline itinerary summary component with the returned items, and may show a transient status message based on the message field and success flag.

Conflict indication within itinerary summary:
- Interaction: User opens or hovers over the itinerary summary to inspect items.
- Behavior: The component uses hasTimeConflict and conflictsWithActivityIds from itinerary.items to label conflicting entries; no additional API calls are made here.

View My Night navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks a "View My Night" link or button.
- Behavior: onViewMyNightClick handler reads the currently selected date from state and navigates to itinerary.html?date={selectedDate}, without additional API calls from this page.

Pagination or incremental loading (if implemented):
- Interaction: User clicks "Load more" or pagination controls if the activity list is segmented.
- Behavior: onPageChange or onLoadMore handlers update pagination-related state (if supported by searchActivities configuration or by additional query parameters) and call searchActivities with the same filters and new pagination parameters (if supported by the implementation).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Schedule & Activities page is fully driven by URL parameters for shareable, restorable state, supplemented by in-memory UI state for transient interaction.

URL parameter → State mapping:
- date: Represents the selected visit date in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format.
  - On load: If present and valid, it is used as the selected date; if absent or invalid, the implementation derives a default date (e.g., current date) without encoding this value in code.
  - On change: Whenever the user selects a new date, this value is written to the URL (date={selectedDate}) via history.replaceState or equivalent.
  - On every searchActivities call: Mapped directly to the date parameter for the interface.

- audience: Represents the selected audience filter.
  - On load: If present, its value is matched against the audience IDs returned by getScheduleFilterOptions.audiences; if it does not match any allowed option, it is ignored.
  - On change: When the user changes the audience filter, the parameter is updated (or removed when cleared) in the URL and mapped to the searchActivities audience parameter.

- timeStart and timeEnd: Represent the selected time range in 'HH:MM' 24-hour format.
  - On load: If present, these are used to initialize the time-range filter controls. If only one is present or they are invalid/unordered, the implementation can normalize or ignore them according to business rules.
  - Mapping: timeStart → searchActivities.timeRangeStart, timeEnd → searchActivities.timeRangeEnd.
  - On change: When the user selects a time-range preset, these parameters are populated from the preset's startTime and endTime; manual adjustments update them directly. They are then used in subsequent searchActivities calls.

- category: Represents the selected activity category.
  - On load: If present, its value is matched against getScheduleFilterOptions.categories IDs; invalid values are ignored.
  - On change: Updated or removed in the URL when the filter control changes and passed as the category parameter to searchActivities.

- locationType: Represents the selected activity location setting.
  - On load: If present, its value is matched against getScheduleFilterOptions.locationTypes IDs; invalid values are ignored.
  - On change: Updated or removed in the URL and passed as the locationType parameter to searchActivities.

Derived in-page state:
- currentDate: The resolved selected date based on date parameter or default.
- currentFilters: An object holding the current audience, timeStart, timeEnd, category, and locationType values, all synchronized with URL parameters.
- scheduleResults: The most recent array returned from searchActivities, bound to the activities list component.
- itineraryPreview: The last itinerary object returned from addActivityToItinerary for the date corresponding to the added activity; stored in memory for the session of this page. This is not persisted to URL parameters, but its date should normally match currentDate when activities are added via this page.

Navigation-driven state refresh:
- On any direct navigation to schedule.html (including back/forward), the controller re-parses the URL query string, re-applies state (currentDate and currentFilters), re-fetches filter options and activities, and re-renders the UI so that the shared link or browser navigation restores the same logical state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, state management for date and filters, and orchestrates calls to getScheduleFilterOptions, searchActivities, and addActivityToItinerary.
Allows the user to select the visit date for which activities should be displayed, synchronizing the selected date with the URL and triggering a new searchActivities call.

Displays and manages all filter controls (audience, time range, category, location type) based on options from getScheduleFilterOptions, synchronizing selections with URL parameters and triggering searchActivities.

Specific filter control for selecting time ranges, supporting both presets from getScheduleFilterOptions.timeRangePresets and manual start/end time inputs, and exposing normalized timeStart/timeEnd values to the controller.

Shows a summary of the current search state, such as the selected date and applied filters, and may display the count of activities returned by the last searchActivities call or an empty-state message when no results are found.
Renders the array of results returned from searchActivities as a list or grid of activity cards, with each card showing key details and actions.

Represents a single scheduled activity, binding to a single Activity object plus durationMinutes and canAddToItinerary flags from the searchActivities results; exposes actions to view details and add the activity to the itinerary.

Invokes addActivityToItinerary when triggered for a given activity, and passes the resulting itinerary data back to the controller for displaying the itinerary summary and alerting the user to conflicts.

Displays a compact summary of the user's "My Night" itinerary for the relevant date using the itinerary object returned from addActivityToItinerary, including conflict indicators based on hasTimeConflict and conflictsWithActivityIds.
Navigates the user to itinerary.html with the current selected date as the date query parameter, enabling users to view and manage their full "My Night" itinerary.

Optionally manages pagination or incremental loading for the activities list if searchActivities is configured to support it, by tracking the current page or offset and requesting additional results while keeping the same date and filters.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width, red bokeh hero with the global navigation overlaid at the very top and a centered 'Schedule & Activities' title. Immediately below, a white content section holds a centered, max-width container (~1120px) that uses a two-column layout. The left column behaves like the editorial text column in the mockup: it’s slightly wider and contains the functional content in a top-to-bottom flow (section heading, date picker, filters, results summary, activities list). The right column mirrors the tall hero image column: a narrower, vertically oriented space dedicated to the itinerary inline summary and the 'View My Night' link. Pagination/load more sits at the bottom of the left column list. All components are arranged in a linear reading flow within their respective column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width, red bokeh hero with the global navigation overlaid at the very top and a centered 'Schedule & Activities' title. Immediately below, a white content section holds a centered, max-width container (~1120px) that uses a two-column layout. The left column behaves like the editorial text column in the mockup: it’s slightly wider and contains the functional content in a top-to-bottom flow (section heading, date picker, filters, results summary, activities list). The right column mirrors the tall hero image column: a narrower, vertically oriented space dedicated to the itinerary inline summary and the 'View My Night' link. Pagination/load more sits at the bottom of the left column list. All components are arranged in a linear reading flow within their respective column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 40% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding around the central title.
