Below, the white content section has about 56–64px of top padding and similar bottom padding. The main container is centered and constrained to ~1100–1200px, with 24–32px of horizontal padding inside. Within this container, the two-column grid gives the left column about 60–65% of the width and the right column about 35–40%, separated by a 24–32px gutter. Vertical spacing between stacked elements (date picker, filters, summary, list) follows a consistent rhythm of 12–24px, while larger section transitions (hero to content, content to footer) use 48–64px spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium, mirroring an editorial article layout. Each control block and card has enough breathing room: generous padding (16–24px) inside components and clear separation (16–24px) between them. The activities list is scannable rather than packed, with each activity card occupying most of the left column width and separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. The right column itinerary panel is visually substantial but not crowded, with whitespace around its edges and between its internal elements. Overall, the page feels spacious and calm, prioritizing clarity and ease of reading over raw information density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye path mimics the mockup’s Z-pattern: first the logo at the top-left, then across the navigation in the hero, then to the large, centered hero title. As the user moves down, the gaze lands on the left-aligned section heading and date selector in the main white section, then moves down through filters and results summary into the activities list. The tall itinerary panel on the right column creates a secondary focal area, drawing the eye across from the left column controls to this vertical panel, then back down to the scrolling list. Within each activity card, the user reads from the top-left title and time across to the add-to-itinerary action on the right, reinforcing a consistent left-to-right reading direction.

layout.overall_description: Below the festive, full-width hero with its dramatic red bokeh background and centered 'Schedule & Activities' heading, the page transitions into a calm, white content area. A centered, max-width container creates a framed, editorial feel. Inside, a two-column layout organizes the planning experience: on the left, a slightly wider column stacks a left-aligned section heading, the date picker, filters, and a concise results summary, followed by a vertical list of generously spaced activity cards. On the right, a narrower column houses a tall itinerary summary panel that aligns to the top of the content, echoing the mockup’s vertical hero image and providing an always-visible snapshot of 'My Night.' The 'View My Night' link is integrated near the top of this right column, inviting deeper engagement with the itinerary. As users scroll, their attention alternates between fine-tuning filters on the left and glancing at their evolving itinerary on the right, all within a spacious, holiday-themed layout that feels both festive and orderly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-page-controller sits directly below the hero section, occupying the full width of the white background area but constraining its inner content to a centered container about 1100–1200px wide. This container has generous top padding of approximately 56–64px to separate it from the hero, and similar bottom padding to separate it from the footer. Inside the controller, a two-column grid is established: the left column spans roughly 60–65% of the container width, starting from the left edge, and the right column occupies the remaining 35–40% on the right, with a 24–32px gutter between them. A left-aligned section heading such as 'Plan Your Night' or 'Tonight’s Activities' (part of this controller) sits at the very top-left of the main content, with 8–16px of space below before the date-picker and other controls. The controller is responsible for stacking the left-column components (date-picker, schedule-filters, time-range-selector, schedule-results-summary, activities-list, pagination) from top to bottom, while the right column holds the itinerary-inline-summary and the view-my-night-link, aligned to the top of the left column’s control block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container defining the main two-column grid; scrolls with the page as a standard content section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-picker is placed in the left column near the top of the content area, directly below the section heading and aligned flush with the left edge of the main content column. It spans most of the left column width—about 80–90%—leaving a small margin of 16–24px from the column’s right edge for visual breathing room. There is approximately 12–16px of vertical space between the heading above and the date-picker, maintaining the moderate spacing seen between text blocks in the mockup. The date-picker itself appears as a horizontally oriented control (e.g., calendar input plus label) with 16–20px internal padding and enough height to feel substantial but not bulky, visually anchoring the start of the interaction flow. Below it, with another 12–16px gap, the schedule-filters component begins, forming a cohesive filter block that reads as a continuous, top-down segment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left column; remains at the top of the scrollable content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-filters component sits in the left column, directly beneath the date-picker, separated by roughly 12–16px of vertical space. It is left-aligned and spans nearly the full width of the left column, matching or slightly exceeding the width of the date-picker for visual alignment. The filter bar reads as a single, cohesive horizontal band, with individual filter chips or dropdowns arranged from left to right. On wide screens, the filters occupy about 70–90% of the left column width; any overflow wraps to a second line, maintaining at least 8px vertical spacing between rows. There is a clear 12–16px gap between the bottom of the filter bar and the time-range-selector below, keeping the controls visually segmented but closely related. The overall height of this block is modest, ensuring it does not dominate the column but still feels like a key interaction area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with other left-column controls; behaves as a responsive horizontal bar that may wrap its contents on smaller widths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-range-selector is positioned immediately beneath the schedule-filters in the left column, maintaining a vertical gap of about 8–12px to suggest a sub-filter relationship. On larger screens, it aligns left and can either span the full left column width or occupy the left two-thirds, leaving a bit of empty space to the right to keep the layout airy. It presents as a compact horizontal control—such as a slider or segmented toggle—with approximately 12–16px of internal padding and a total height that keeps it visually lighter than the main filter bar. Together with the filters and date-picker above, it forms a stacked control cluster occupying roughly the top quarter of the left column content area, before the results summary and list begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, subordinate control that sits inline in the vertical stack and scales responsively with the left column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-results-summary appears in the left column directly beneath the time-range-selector, separated by roughly 16px of vertical space to signal a shift from controls to results. It is left-aligned and typically spans the full width of the left column, but its content is concise—a single line or short block of text indicating the number of activities and current filter state. The text sits in the upper-left corner of the left column, with 0–8px of margin from the top of the block, and may be paired with a subtle icon. There is about 16–20px of vertical space between this summary and the first activity-card in the activities-list below, matching the rhythm used in the editorial mockup between intro and body text. This component visually acts like the italic lead-in paragraph in the mockup: a quick, slightly emphasized bridge between controls and the main content body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline text block; scrolls away as the user goes down the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-list occupies the majority of the left column below the schedule-results-summary, forming the main scrollable content area. It is left-aligned, spanning roughly 95–100% of the left column width, with consistent left and right padding inside each card to maintain a clean edge. The list is a vertical stack of activity-card components, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space, mirroring the well-spaced paragraphs in the mockup. The list extends downward as needed, potentially exceeding the viewport height, while the right column itinerary panel remains visible alongside it. At the very bottom of the list, below the final activity-card, the pagination-or-load-more component appears, separated by a 24–32px gap to clearly indicate the end of the visible results batch.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical list within the left column; the list itself is not fixed, and content flows naturally as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each activity-card is a horizontally oriented card within the activities-list, occupying most of the left column width (about 100% of the list’s inner width minus 16–24px side padding). Cards are left-aligned with a subtle shadow or border and 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, echoing the comfortable spacing of the article text block in the mockup. Within the card, content is arranged in a vertical hierarchy: the activity title sits at the top-left, followed immediately by the time, location, and a brief description, all aligned to the left. The add-to-itinerary-action is positioned within the card towards the right side, typically aligned either with the card’s vertical center or the bottom-right corner, creating a subtle horizontal flow from left content to right action, similar to reading across to the image in the original mockup. The cards have 16–24px of vertical space between them, ensuring none feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level items within the list; they expand vertically with content but maintain a consistent max width and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-itinerary-action is embedded within each activity-card as a prominent button or icon-button. It is visually anchored to the right side of the card, either in the upper-right or lower-right corner, depending on the card’s internal layout, with 12–16px of padding between the button and the card edges. Horizontally, it balances the left-aligned text, mimicking the mockup’s left text / right image split on a smaller scale: the user reads the activity details on the left, then their eye naturally moves to the action on the right. Vertically, it aligns with key information—such as the time or title—so it feels directly associated with the activity. It does not break the overall spacing between cards; instead, it sits comfortably within the card’s interior whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within each card; it does not affect the card’s layout flow beyond occupying its designated corner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The itinerary-inline-summary occupies the top of the right column, aligning its top edge with the vertical level of the date-picker and filters in the left column to create a visually balanced row, similar to the text-and-image alignment in the original mockup. It spans most of the right column width (about 90–100%), with 16–24px of internal padding and a card-like appearance that echoes the activity cards but with slightly more visual emphasis (e.g., a subtle accent border or background tint). The panel is vertically oriented and can extend downward to accommodate a short list of selected activities, creating a tall, prominent block similar in proportion to the historical image in the mockup. Inside, content flows from a small heading at the top, to a summary of selected items, to a compact list of itinerary entries. There is a modest gap of 8–12px above any internal 'View My Night' link if it is also shown inside this panel. On longer pages, as the user scrolls down the activities-list, this panel remains visually anchored on the right, providing a continuous reference to the user’s current plans.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right column on larger screens: once the top of the panel reaches a defined offset from the viewport top (e.g., 80–96px below the nav), it remains fixed in place while the left column content continues to scroll. On smaller screens, it becomes a standard block that stacks above or below the activities-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-my-night-link is positioned in close proximity to the itinerary-inline-summary, reinforcing its connection to the itinerary. On desktop, it appears either as a small, right-aligned text link or button at the top-right corner of the itinerary panel, or directly beneath the panel’s heading inside the card, aligned to the right. In either case, it sits within the right column’s horizontal boundaries and does not exceed the width of the itinerary panel. There is a small 8–12px gap between this link and adjacent text or panel edges to maintain clarity. The link is visually lighter than primary buttons but clearly distinguishable, serving as a tertiary call-to-action that invites users to jump to a dedicated itinerary page or overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column and typically within or just adjacent to the itinerary panel; it moves as part of that block’s sticky behavior on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-or-load-more component is placed at the very bottom of the activities-list in the left column, centered horizontally within the column. It is separated from the last activity-card by a noticeably larger vertical gap of about 24–32px, signaling a boundary between the current results and the control to fetch more. If implemented as numeric pagination, the control’s buttons or page numbers are arranged in a horizontal row with equal spacing; if implemented as a single 'Load More' button, it appears as a centered button roughly 40–48px tall and taking up about 30–40% of the left column width. This control marks the visual end of the main content flow in the left column before any footer content begins, mirroring the clear section breaks in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the end of the list; it moves naturally with the scroll and does not stick or float.

name: Activity Details
title: Activity Details
description: Displays full details for a selected activity and lets users add it to their 'My Night' itinerary, with navigation back to the schedule or to their itinerary.

core_features: Load and display full details for a specific activity using the activityId URL parameter and getActivityDetails.
Show activity timing, duration, location, category, audience suitability, and accessibility notes based on Activity data.
Provide an action to add the activity to the user's itinerary for that date using addActivityToItinerary.
Display feedback after adding to itinerary, including messages and any time conflicts returned by addActivityToItinerary.
Offer navigation back to the schedule for the activity date with appropriate URL parameters.
Offer navigation to the 'My Night' itinerary for the activity date after an activity has been added.
user_workflows:
View activity details from schedule:
1. User clicks an activity on schedule.html, which navigates to activity_detail.html with a URL parameter activityId.
2. Activity Details page reads activityId from the URL.
3. Page calls getActivityDetails(activityId).
4. When data returns, the page shows the activity title, formatted description, start/end times, derived duration, location, category, audience, and accessibility notes.
5. User reviews suitability and timing information.

Add activity to 'My Night' and handle conflicts:
1. On Activity Details, user clicks the 'Add to My Night' button.
2. Page calls addActivityToItinerary(activityId).
3. If success is true, page displays a confirmation message and updates the itinerary feedback component with itinerary.id, itinerary.date, and items.
4. If any items in the returned itinerary have hasTimeConflict set to true, the page highlights conflicts and lists which existing activities (by conflictsWithActivityIds) overlap.
5. User can choose to navigate to itinerary.html with date set to itinerary.date to review and adjust their night.

Return to schedule for the same date:
1. User clicks 'Back to Schedule'.
2. Page derives the activity date (date-only portion) from activity.startDateTime loaded via getActivityDetails.
3. Page navigates to schedule.html with a date URL parameter set to that derived date.
4. On schedule.html, activities for that date can be shown.

View itinerary for selected date after adding:
1. After successfully adding an activity, user clicks 'View My Night'.
2. Page reads itinerary.date from the last addActivityToItinerary response.
3. Page navigates to itinerary.html with a date URL parameter equal to itinerary.date.
4. On itinerary.html, user sees their activities for that date.

Handle missing or invalid activityId:
1. User attempts to access activity_detail.html without a valid activityId in the URL.
2. Page detects that activityId is missing or empty.
3. Page displays an error state explaining that the activity could not be loaded and hides dependent controls like 'Add to My Night'.
4. Optionally, if a date URL parameter is present, a 'Back to Schedule' link can still be shown using that date.
interactions:
Page load with activityId:
- Trigger: Browser loads activity_detail.html with ?activityId=...
- Behavior: Page parses activityId from the URL query string and calls getActivityDetails(activityId). While loading, a loading state is shown; on success, detail components bind to the returned activity and related fields; on error, an error state is shown.

Click 'Add to My Night':
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Add to My Night' button.
- Behavior: Button is disabled while the request is in progress. Page calls addActivityToItinerary(activityId). After response, button is re-enabled. The itinerary feedback component updates with success, message, and the returned itinerary data, including items and conflict flags.

Click 'View My Night':
- Trigger: User clicks the 'View My Night' link or button displayed after a successful add to itinerary.
- Behavior: Page reads itinerary.date from the last addActivityToItinerary response and navigates to itinerary.html with ?date=<itinerary.date>. The current activityId does not need to be passed to itinerary.html unless required by that page's design.

Click 'Back to Schedule':
- Trigger: User clicks a 'Back to Schedule' link or button.
- Behavior: Page derives the date portion (YYYY-MM-DD) from the currently loaded activity.startDateTime and navigates to schedule.html with ?date=<derived_date>.

Conflict item interaction (read-only in this page):
- Trigger: After adding to itinerary, conflicts are displayed if present.
- Behavior: User can see which activities conflict with the one just added through a list that references each itinerary item and its conflict status. Items are not editable on this page; users are guided to 'View My Night' for changes.

Error retry:
- Trigger: An error occurs when calling getActivityDetails or addActivityToItinerary, and user clicks a 'Retry' control.
- Behavior: For details load, page re-calls getActivityDetails(activityId). For add-to-itinerary errors, page re-calls addActivityToItinerary(activityId). Error messages are cleared or updated based on the new response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is based on URL parameters and the latest responses from getActivityDetails and addActivityToItinerary. On initial load, the page reads the activityId query parameter from the URL (e.g., activity_detail.html?activityId=<id>). If activityId is present, it calls getActivityDetails(activityId); if not, it shows an error and disables actions that depend on an activity. The successfully loaded Activity object becomes the source of truth for date and display data; when the user clicks 'Back to Schedule', the page converts activity.startDateTime to a date-only string (YYYY-MM-DD) and uses that value in the navigation to schedule.html?date=<derived_date>. When the user adds the activity to their itinerary, the page calls addActivityToItinerary(activityId) without changing the URL; instead, it stores the returned itinerary object in page memory for the current session. If the user then chooses 'View My Night', the page navigates to itinerary.html?date=<itinerary.date> using the date from the most recent addActivityToItinerary response. No persistent client-side storage is used; all state is either in the current page memory (activity details and the most recent itinerary response) or derived from URL parameters. Any reload of activity_detail.html uses only the URL parameters to restore state by re-calling getActivityDetails and, if needed, can optionally refetch itinerary-related info via addActivityToItinerary when the user interacts again.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the activityId from the URL query string and fetching full activity details via getActivityDetails. Manages loading and error states for downstream components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: parse activityId from URL; if present, call getActivityDetails(activityId); if missing, set error state.
onGetActivityDetailsSuccess: store returned activity object, durationMinutes, and formattedDescription in page state; clear errors; notify child components that data is ready.
onGetActivityDetailsError: set error state and notify child components that no activity is available.
onRetryClick: re-call getActivityDetails with the current activityId when a retry control is activated.

components.functionality: Displays the activity title and key temporal information (date, start time, end time, and duration) once getActivityDetails has returned data.
event_handlers: onActivityDataChange: update displayed title and time information whenever the activity object from activity-detail-loader changes.

components.functionality: Shows the detailed description of the activity using the formattedDescription field if available, with a fallback to the raw Activity.description.
event_handlers: onActivityDataChange: decide whether to render formattedDescription or Activity.description based on which is populated.

components.functionality: Displays metadata about the activity including category, audience, location, and indoor/outdoor type for suitability and planning.
event_handlers: onActivityDataChange: update category label, audience label, location text, and indoor/outdoor indicator when activity data changes.

components.functionality: Displays accessibility-related information for the activity using Activity.accessibilityNotes. If there are no notes, the component can remain hidden or show a neutral message based on configuration.
event_handlers: onActivityDataChange: show or hide the accessibility section based on whether accessibilityNotes is present; update the displayed notes when they change.

components.functionality: Provides an 'Add to My Night' / 'Add to Itinerary' control that calls addActivityToItinerary with the current activityId and triggers itinerary feedback and conflict handling.
event_handlers: onClick: if Activity.id is available and no add operation is currently in progress, call addActivityToItinerary(Activity.id); set an in-progress state to disable repeated clicks.
onAddActivityToItinerarySuccess: update itinerary feedback and conflict list components with the returned itinerary; clear any previous error associated with adding.
onAddActivityToItineraryError: display an error message in itinerary feedback; reset in-progress state.
onActivityDataChange: enable or disable the button based on whether Activity.id is present and valid.

components.functionality: Shows feedback after an attempt to add the activity to the itinerary, including success or error status and the message returned by addActivityToItinerary. If successful, exposes a 'View My Night' navigation control.
event_handlers: onAddActivityToItineraryResponse: display the banner with appropriate styling based on success or failure; show the message from the response.
onViewMyNightClick: if addActivityToItinerary.itinerary.date is available, navigate to itinerary.html with ?date=<itinerary.date>.

components.functionality: Lists itinerary items returned by addActivityToItinerary that have scheduling conflicts, based on hasTimeConflict and conflictsWithActivityIds. Provides read-only visibility into overlaps with other activities on the same itinerary.
event_handlers: onAddActivityToItinerarySuccess: iterate through itinerary.items and render items with hasTimeConflict set to true; for each, display its associated activity fields such as Activity.title, Activity.startDateTime, and Activity.endDateTime.
onViewMyNightClick: pass-through to itinerary-feedback-banner's navigation handler so users can adjust conflicts on itinerary.html.
onClearConflictsView: hide or collapse the conflict list if the user dismisses it, without altering underlying data.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the schedule for the activity date and, when available, to the user's itinerary for that date.
event_handlers: onBackToScheduleClick: derive a date-only value from Activity.startDateTime; navigate to schedule.html with ?date=<derived_date> when Activity.startDateTime is available.
onViewMyNightClick: if addActivityToItinerary.itinerary.date exists, navigate to itinerary.html with ?date=<itinerary.date>.
onActivityDataChange: enable or disable 'Back to Schedule' based on whether Activity.startDateTime has been loaded.

components.functionality: Centralizes display of loading indicators and error messages related to getActivityDetails and addActivityToItinerary calls, ensuring that dependent components know when data is not yet available or has failed.
event_handlers: onGetActivityDetailsStart: show a page-level loading indicator and hide activity-dependent components.
onGetActivityDetailsSuccess: hide loading indicator, clear any load error, and show activity-dependent components.

onGetActivityDetailsError: hide loading indicator, show an error message indicating that the activity could not be loaded, and disable add-to-itinerary and navigation controls that require activity data.
onAddActivityToItineraryStart: optionally show a local loading indicator on the add-to-itinerary control.
onAddActivityToItineraryError: show an inline error message associated with the add action without removing the already-loaded activity details.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup's hero-then-article pattern: a full-width, red, festive hero at the top containing the activity header, followed by a white background content area constrained to a centered max-width container. Inside the content area, a two-column layout is used on desktop: the left column is text-first (description and accessibility notes), while the right column is utility-first (key activity info, add-to-itinerary control, and any conflicts). Global or site-wide navigation is assumed above the hero; page-specific navigation (back to schedule / to itinerary) sits at the bottom of the content flow as a horizontal bar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup's hero-then-article pattern: a full-width, red, festive hero at the top containing the activity header, followed by a white background content area constrained to a centered max-width container. Inside the content area, a two-column layout is used on desktop: the left column is text-first (description and accessibility notes), while the right column is utility-first (key activity info, add-to-itinerary control, and any conflicts). Global or site-wide navigation is assumed above the hero; page-specific navigation (back to schedule / to itinerary) sits at the bottom of the content flow as a horizontal bar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height with generous vertical padding around the activity title and key metadata. Below, the main content container sits on a white background, centered with large side margins. The text column on the left uses approximately 60–65% of the container width, with comfortable measure for reading (around 55–65 characters per line), and the right column takes the remaining 35–40%. Vertical spacing between stacked sections (description, accessibility notes, navigation bar) stays around 48–64px, while spacing between related elements within a section (e.g., between heading and body text, or between the info panel and the add-to-itinerary button) is tighter but consistent (16–32px).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a low-to-medium content density reminiscent of an editorial article: generous margins around the hero and main content, well-spaced paragraphs, and clear breathing room between panels. Functional controls like the add-to-itinerary button and conflict list are visually grouped but not cramped, using subtle card-like containers and consistent vertical rhythm. Status banners and error messages are slim and clearly separated from primary reading content to avoid visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches any site logo and global nav at the very top, then is drawn to the center-aligned activity title in the festive hero. From there, attention moves down into the main white content section, starting at the top-left where the activity description heading and text live. As users read down the left column, their peripheral vision picks up the right column: the key info panel, the add-to-itinerary control, and any conflict information, forming a mild Z-pattern from hero center → left text heading → across to right info panel and CTA. The feedback banner and bottom navigation controls guide the eye further down, providing closure and next-step options at the end of the scroll.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The Activity Details page feels like a festive, editorial article about a single Christmas attraction. At the top, a full-width, red bokeh hero fills the upper third of the viewport, with a softly overlaid activity title and key details centered within a constrained inner container. Below this dramatic header, the page transitions to a calm white content area, with the main content centered and framed by generous side margins. Here, the layout splits into two columns: a wider left strip devoted to narrative content—section titles, descriptive paragraphs, and accessibility notes—and a narrower right strip hosting a compact information card, a prominent add-to-itinerary button, and any conflict or status messaging. The rhythm of spacing keeps everything airy and easy to scan, while the visual hierarchy leads users from festive storytelling in the hero down into practical planning tools. At the bottom, a simple horizontal navigation bar offers clear routes back to the event schedule or onward to their 'My Night' itinerary, completing the journey.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width, red, festive hero that takes up roughly the top third of the viewport, showcasing the activity name and key details in a centered, editorial-style block. Just below, the background shifts to white, and the content is drawn into a centered container with generous side margins. Within this container, a two-column layout emerges: the left column—wider and text-first—holds the activity description and accessibility notes as well-spaced, readable blocks. The right column—slightly narrower—stacked from top to bottom, contains a compact info panel summarizing time, location, and other essentials, followed by a strong add-to-itinerary control and any conflict indications. Status feedback appears as a subtle banner integrated into the flow near the planning controls. A loading overlay can temporarily sit above everything when data is fetched, and error states appear where the main content would be, maintaining the same centered, airy feel. At the very bottom of the white content area, a horizontal navigation bar stretches across the main container, offering clear, left/right-aligned navigation back to the schedule or into the user’s itinerary, rounding out a layout that feels both festive and highly readable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that covers the entire visible area of the Activity Details page—from the top of the hero down past the bottom of the main content—when data is being fetched. It sits on top of all other components, dimming the festive hero and white content sections slightly, with a centered spinner or seasonal loading indicator placed in the middle of the viewport. The overlay aligns to the viewport edges, not just the content container, giving the sense that the whole page is temporarily paused. When inactive, this component is hidden and does not affect layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position full-page overlay that spans 100% of the viewport width and height when active, sitting above all other components with a translucent background; does not occupy layout space when hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity header sits at the top of the page within a full-width hero section that uses the dramatic red bokeh/sparkle background. The hero occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding (around 64px top and bottom) inside. Within this hero, an inner content container is centered horizontally and constrained to about 1100–1200px maximum width. The activity title is centered within this inner container, both horizontally and approximately vertically within the hero, rendered large and readable. Beneath the title, a single line or two of key meta information (time, location, duration) may appear, still center-aligned but with tighter spacing (around 16–24px) from the title. The header content avoids the very edges, leaving ample padding on all sides to keep it feeling airy against the rich background. It sits directly below any global site navigation and directly above the main white content section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero section at the top of the page; scrolls normally with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity description panel is the top-left anchor of the white content section directly beneath the hero. It sits inside the centered main content container, starting approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero. On desktop, it occupies about 60–65% of the container width, forming the left column of a two-column layout. The panel begins with a left-aligned section heading (for example, the activity name repeated in a smaller style or 'About this activity') with about 16–24px of space below it, followed by an author or source line if applicable, then an optional short italic lead-in sentence, and finally the main descriptive paragraphs. Line length is kept comfortable (about 55–65 characters per line), and paragraphs have 16–24px vertical spacing between them. There is a clear 32–48px gap below the last paragraph before the next panel (accessibility notes) begins, preserving the editorial, airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main flowing two-column layout; on narrower viewports it stacks above the right column components, expanding to nearly full width while maintaining generous side margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity info panel sits in the right column of the main content area, aligned to the top edge of the activity description panel. It occupies the remaining 35–40% of the content container width and is visually treated as a compact card: a white or slightly tinted background, subtle border or soft shadow, and internal padding of about 24–32px. The panel’s content is vertically stacked: a small heading like 'Activity Details' at the top, followed by key-value rows for time, location, duration, age suitability, etc., each with 8–16px vertical spacing. The card’s height is determined by its content, and it leaves generous whitespace around itself: at least 24–32px gap to the main container’s right edge and around 24px gap below before the add-to-itinerary control is introduced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element occupying the top of the right column; scrolls with the page and reflows below the description panel as a full-width card on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The accessibility notes panel is positioned directly beneath the activity description panel in the left column of the white content area. It shares the same width as the description (about 60–65% of the container) and begins after a generous vertical gap of 32–48px to clearly separate it as its own section. A left-aligned section heading such as 'Accessibility' or 'Accessibility Notes' sits at the top, with about 16–24px of space before the explanatory text. The content is arranged in short paragraphs or bullet points with 12–16px spacing between items, keeping it easy to scan. The panel’s bottom edge ends with an additional 48–64px of whitespace before the bottom navigation controls, maintaining a spacious, editorial rhythm on the left side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the left column, flowing directly after the description; on narrow screens it appears below the description and below the info/CTA stack as part of a single column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-itinerary control is placed in the right column, immediately beneath the activity info panel, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px to visually separate the utility card from the call-to-action. It is presented as a prominent, full-width button within the right column’s content width, spanning nearly the entire width of that column (minus internal padding). The button is horizontally centered within the right column, with at least 16px padding above and below its label and 24–32px padding left and right. It visually stands out through festive but modern styling (e.g., solid red or accent color, high contrast text) while keeping the shape clean and simple, consistent with the semi-modern aesthetic. There is a small 16–24px gap beneath this button before any conflict indicators appear, so the button feels like the primary interactive element in that stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow call-to-action within the right column; on narrow viewports it appears just below the info panel in the single-column stack, maintaining full-width button behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The itinerary feedback banner appears within the white content area, spanning the full width of the main content container but not the entire viewport. It is positioned just below the hero and above the two-column layout, so that when it appears (for example, after successfully adding an activity to 'My Night' or encountering a planning issue), it is clearly visible near the top of the content. The banner is a low-profile horizontal strip with about 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, running edge-to-edge within the container (not touching the browser edges). There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of the banner and the start of the activity description/info columns, ensuring it feels like a transient status layer without crowding the main reading content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: In-flow, full-width element within the main content container; appears and disappears above the two-column content without overlaying other elements, pushing content down when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The itinerary conflict list is nested visually in the right column, appearing directly beneath the add-to-itinerary control. It begins after a modest 16–24px vertical gap from the bottom of the button, signaling that it is related feedback to the action above. The list is contained within the same right column width, optionally framed as a slim, lightly bordered panel or simply a text block with a small warning icon or colored label at the top (e.g., 'Conflicts in your night'). Individual conflicts are displayed as short lines of text with 8–12px spacing between them, keeping the overall height compact. The entire block maintains at least 24–32px space to the bottom of the main content area before the page-level navigation controls begin, so that the right column content does not feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow element in the right column stacked immediately after the add-to-itinerary control; on narrow screens it appears beneath the button in the single-column stack, preserving its relative relationship.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls form a horizontal bar at the bottom of the main white content area, inside the same centered container as the two-column content. They sit approximately 48–64px below the last main content elements (accessibility notes on the left and conflict list or button stack on the right), creating a clear visual break between activity details and next-step navigation. The bar spans the full width of the content container and uses a minimal height, with about 16–24px vertical padding. Within this bar, a 'Back to schedule' text link or button is aligned to the left, while a 'View My Night' or similar itinerary link/button is aligned to the right, creating a balanced left-right tension in line with the gentle Z-pattern. The bar’s background remains white or slightly tinted, with perhaps a subtle top border to distinguish it from the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow footer-style navigation within the content container; always appears after all detail sections and scrolls out of view normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-loading-state handler occupies the same general spatial footprint as the main content beneath the hero. When an error occurs (such as failing to load activity details), the handler suppresses the normal two-column layout and instead presents a centered error message within the main content container, starting roughly 48–64px below the hero. The error block is a compact card or text block with a clear heading ('Something went wrong'), a short explanation, and optionally a retry control, all centered within the container’s width but left-aligned within the card itself for readability. Vertical spacing around this block mirrors the spacing that would normally separate sections, maintaining the page’s airy, editorial feel. In loading states, this handler triggers the full-page loader overlay rather than altering the layout content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical state manager that conditionally replaces the main content area with an error block or triggers the full-page loader; when showing errors, it uses the normal in-flow content space beneath the hero without changing the hero’s position.

name: My Night / Itinerary
title: My Night Itinerary
description: View and manage a personalized list of activities for a selected date, ordered by time, with conflict and gap indicators and options to print or export the itinerary.

core_features: Load the user's itinerary for a specific date (or the most recently used date if no date parameter is provided) using the getItinerary interface.
Display all itinerary items in chronological order, including activity times, titles, and locations, based on the Activity data returned inside the itinerary items.
Allow users to remove individual activities from the itinerary through the removeItineraryItem interface and immediately refresh the displayed itinerary.
Highlight activities that have time conflicts using the hasTimeConflict and conflictsWithActivityIds values returned from getItinerary.
Identify and display gaps between consecutive activities (based on startDateTime and endDateTime) to help users see free time in their night.
Provide navigation from each activity entry to the detailed activity page (activity_detail.html) using the activityId URL parameter.
Provide navigation to the schedule page (schedule.html) to add more activities, passing the currently selected itinerary date as the date URL parameter.

Offer itinerary export options: print via browser printing, download a structured file generated on the client from the itinerary data, and copy a shareable URL so users can save or share their current itinerary state.
user_workflows: View itinerary for a chosen date: 1) User opens itinerary.html optionally with a date URL parameter; 2) Page controller reads the date parameter; 3) Page controller calls getItinerary with the date (or without if no parameter is present); 4) While loading, a loading state is shown; 5) On success, the itinerary date, summary, and ordered list of items are displayed; 6) If no items are present, an empty state with an option to add activities is shown.

Change itinerary date: 1) User opens the date selector in the header; 2) User selects a different date; 3) Component updates the browser location to itinerary.html with the new date URL parameter; 4) Page reloads and the controller calls getItinerary with the new date; 5) The itinerary list and summary update to reflect the selected date.

Remove an activity from the itinerary: 1) User clicks the remove action on an itinerary item; 2) A confirmation modal appears showing basic information from that item; 3) User confirms removal; 4) Page controller calls removeItineraryItem with the itineraryItemId; 5) On success, the updated itinerary from the response replaces the current in-memory itinerary and the list re-renders; 6) Conflict and gap indicators are recalculated based on the updated items; 7) Any message returned from the interface may be displayed in a transient status area.

Inspect and resolve time conflicts: 1) Page displays any items with hasTimeConflict set to true with a clear conflict indicator; 2) User clicks on a conflicting activity title; 3) Browser navigates to activity_detail.html with the activityId of the selected item; 4) After reviewing details on the activity detail page, user may remove or adjust activities there (via that page’s own features) and then return to itinerary.html (which will call getItinerary again on load).

Identify and use free time gaps: 1) After loading the itinerary, the list component compares the endDateTime of each activity to the startDateTime of the next activity; 2) Where there is a gap, a gap row is displayed between items showing the duration of free time; 3) User can click an adjacent control (for example, an 'Add more activities' action near the gap) which navigates to schedule.html with the itinerary date in the URL so the user can fill that time from the schedule page.

Navigate to activity details: 1) User clicks on the title (or dedicated details link) of an itinerary item; 2) Browser navigates to activity_detail.html with the activityId URL parameter set to the Activity.id from that itinerary item; 3) User reviews details and may choose to add related activities or make other changes on that page.

Add more activities to the itinerary: 1) User clicks the 'Add More Activities' control from the itinerary page (from header actions, empty state, or near gaps); 2) Browser navigates to schedule.html with the date URL parameter set to the current itinerary date; 3) On schedule.html the user adds activities to the itinerary; 4) When schedule.html links back to itinerary.html, the date is preserved in the URL so the updated itinerary for that date is shown.

Print or export itinerary: 1) User opens the export actions area; 2) To print, user clicks the print control and the component triggers browser printing of the current itinerary view; 3) To download, user clicks the download control, which generates a structured file (for example, text or calendar format) from the currently loaded itinerary object on the client side and prompts the user to download it; 4) To save/share the current itinerary, user clicks the copy-link control, which copies the current page URL (including the date parameter) to the clipboard so it can be saved or shared.
interactions: On page load, read the date URL parameter (if present) and call getItinerary with that date; if no parameter is present, call getItinerary without a date parameter so that the server selects the most appropriate itinerary date.
While getItinerary is in progress, show a loading indicator and disable itinerary interactions (remove, print, download, navigation via buttons) to prevent inconsistent states.

If getItinerary returns successfully with an itinerary object, bind the itinerary date, name, and list of items to the respective components; if the itinerary has no items, show the empty-itinerary component instead of the list.
If getItinerary fails or returns an error state, show an error banner with a generic message and a control that retries the call by invoking getItinerary again with the same date parameter.

When the user interacts with the date selector and picks a different date, update the browser URL to itinerary.html with the new date parameter and let the normal page-load flow call getItinerary for that date.

When the user clicks the remove button on an itinerary item row, open a confirmation modal that displays basic activity information from that row; if the user confirms, call removeItineraryItem with the associated itineraryItemId and, on success, replace the current itinerary data with the itinerary object returned from the interface.

After a successful removeItineraryItem call, re-evaluate which items have hasTimeConflict set, recompute gap indicators based on the new chronological list, and update any conflict summary counts or messaging shown on the page.
When the user clicks an activity title or dedicated details link in a row, navigate the browser to activity_detail.html with the activityId parameter set from the Activity.id in that itinerary item.

When the user clicks any 'Add More Activities' control, navigate the browser to schedule.html with the date parameter set to the currently loaded itinerary.date value, preserving the itinerary date in the next view.
When the user clicks the print itinerary control, invoke the browser’s print dialog for the current page so that the itinerary view can be printed or saved to a device-specific format.

When the user clicks the download itinerary control, generate a file on the client side using the currently loaded itinerary data (including date and items) and prompt the user to download it; no additional server-side interfaces are used for this export.
When the user clicks the copy-link or save-link control, construct the current itinerary URL, including the date parameter if present, and copy it to the clipboard so the user can paste it elsewhere.

Conflict indicators are interactive: hovering or focusing on a conflict indicator associated with an itinerary item highlights that item and any other items whose Activity.id appears in its conflictsWithActivityIds list, helping the user understand overlapping choices.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the date URL parameter on itinerary.html. On every page load, the controller reads location.search and extracts the date parameter (if present). If a valid date parameter is present, it is passed directly to getItinerary(date). If no date parameter is present, getItinerary is called without a date so that the server selects the most appropriate itinerary date (such as the most recently used). The response itinerary.date is then treated as the canonical date for this view and is used when constructing outgoing navigation URLs (to schedule.html and in any copied/sharing links). When the user changes the date via the page’s date selector, the component updates the browser URL to itinerary.html with the new date query parameter and allows the page to reload, which triggers getItinerary with the new date. After any successful removeItineraryItem call, the returned itinerary object replaces the current itinerary state in memory without changing the URL; the date in the URL remains the same since the date of the itinerary itself does not change. All other UI state (expanded rows, selected conflict filters, open modals) is managed in memory for the current page lifetime only and is reset on each navigation or reload; no localStorage or other persistent client storage is used.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading and updating of the itinerary based on the date URL parameter, manages loading and error states, and passes itinerary data into child components.
Displays a loading indicator while the itinerary data is being fetched or updated, and disables interactions that depend on the itinerary.
Shows error messages when getItinerary or removeItineraryItem fail, with an option to retry loading the itinerary.
Shows the selected itinerary date (from itinerary.date or the URL parameter) and allows the user to select a different date, which reloads the page with the new date parameter.

Displays a high-level summary of the itinerary, such as the number of activities, earliest start time, latest end time, and the count of conflicting activities, using only the currently loaded itinerary data.
Provides page-level actions for the itinerary including printing, downloading a file representation, copying a shareable link, and navigating to schedule.html to add more activities.
Explains the meaning of conflict indicators used in the itinerary list and optionally toggles the visibility or emphasis of conflicting items.
Renders itinerary items in chronological order for the current date, inserting gap rows where free time exists between consecutive activities.
Displays a single itinerary item including time range, activity title, location, audience or category details, and conflict status, with controls to view details or remove the item.
Represents a period of free time between two consecutive activities, calculated purely from their start and end timestamps, and can provide an action to add more activities.
Displays when an itinerary is loaded but has no items, offering guidance and a direct link to schedule.html to start adding activities for the current date.
Asks the user to confirm removal of a selected itinerary item before calling removeItineraryItem, and handles success or error feedback.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, hero-then-content pattern as the mockup. A full-width, fixed-height festive hero at the top (within the page controller) introduces the 'My Night' / Itinerary page with a centered title over a red bokeh background and global nav overlaid at the very top. Below the hero, a white content section uses a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: the broader left column holds the chronological itinerary (date header + list), while the narrower right column holds supporting information (summary panel, conflict legend, and secondary actions where appropriate). Error and loading states appear just under the hero and over the content container, respectively, without disrupting the overall editorial flow. The confirmation modal vertically and horizontally centers above everything when triggered.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom, hero-then-content pattern as the mockup. A full-width, fixed-height festive hero at the top (within the page controller) introduces the 'My Night' / Itinerary page with a centered title over a red bokeh background and global nav overlaid at the very top. Below the hero, a white content section uses a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: the broader left column holds the chronological itinerary (date header + list), while the narrower right column holds supporting information (summary panel, conflict legend, and secondary actions where appropriate). Error and loading states appear just under the hero and over the content container, respectively, without disrupting the overall editorial flow. The confirmation modal vertically and horizontally centers above everything when triggered.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding around the main 'My Night' heading and plenty of negative space so the background imagery feels dramatic. The main content container is constrained to about 1100–1200px and centered with comfortable side margins. Inside, the left column takes about 60–65% of the container width for readable text and list items; the right column takes about 35–40% for summary and legend. Vertical spacing between key blocks (date header, summary panel, legend, list sections) ranges around 32–48px, and between smaller text elements around 8–16px, mirroring the mockup’s 8px-based, spacious spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains low-to-medium, optimized for comfortable reading and scanning. The itinerary list is the densest area but still uses generous row padding and clear dividers or whitespace between items and gap rows. The summary panel and legend are visually light, with sufficient padding and whitespace so they don’t compete with the main list. Empty and error states use open space and centered messaging to feel calm and uncluttered, consistent with the editorial tone rather than a dashboard-heavy layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s mild Z-pattern. The eye first catches the logo and navigation at the top-left and top-right over the hero, then moves inward to the large centered hero title. As users scroll down past the hero, attention is drawn to the left-aligned date header at the top of the white content section, then flows downward through the itinerary items in chronological order. From there, the eye naturally shifts to the right column where the summary panel and conflict legend provide context, similar to how the historical image in the mockup draws secondary attention. Actions like Print/Export live in the upper-right region of the content, echoing the top-right emphasis of the navigation, but remain visually secondary to the hero title and date header.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width, festive red bokeh hero at the top, taking up roughly the upper third of the viewport. The global navigation sits as a slim bar along the very top edge, overlaying the hero. Centered within this hero, the 'My Night' title (and optionally a short subtitle) is given ample breathing room above and below, making the hero feel airy and celebratory. As you scroll past the hero, the background shifts to a clean white content area, with a centered, max-width container framed by generous left and right margins. At the top of this container, the selected date and itinerary heading are left-aligned, while key actions like Print and Export align to the right on the same horizontal row, creating a balanced header band. Directly beneath, the layout resolves into a two-column structure: a wider left column presents the main chronological itinerary list—rows of activities interspersed with clearly labeled gaps—while a narrower right column stacks a tidy summary panel above a compact conflict legend. Ample vertical spacing separates these blocks, giving the page a calm, editorial feel despite having interactive features. Overlays for loading, errors, and the remove-confirmation modal appear centered and layered above this structure without disturbing the underlying alignment, preserving the overall clean, seasonal, and slightly editorial aesthetic drawn from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller spans the full browser width and height, acting as the structural shell for both the hero and the main content. At the top, it reserves a hero region that is full-width and approximately 35–40% of the initial viewport height, with a dramatic red bokeh/sparkle background. The global navigation bar sits flush at the top edge of this hero, overlayed and stretching the full width but relatively shallow in height. Centered within the hero, the 'My Night' / Itinerary page title is vertically and horizontally centered in the remaining hero space, with generous vertical padding (roughly 64px above and below) so the text feels prominent yet airy. Beneath the hero, the controller transitions to a white background main content section. Inside this section, a centered max-width container (~1120px) applies consistent horizontal padding (24–32px) and vertical padding (48–64px from the hero bottom). Within that container, the controller defines a two-column grid: the left column occupies about 60–65% of the container width, the right column about 35–40%. This grid aligns all subsequent components (date header, actions, summary, legend, and list) so the page reads like an editorial article with a strong primary column and supporting sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-page wrapper that scrolls normally. The hero height is fixed relative to viewport (e.g., min-height ~40vh), while the white content area grows vertically with the itinerary content. The controller also establishes a positioning context for overlays like loading and modals.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loading state appears as a semi-opaque overlay covering the main white content section within the page controller, starting just below the hero and extending to the bottom of the viewport (or content height, whichever is taller). It matches the width of the central content container but visually dims everything under it with a light translucent layer, keeping the hero title still visible above. At the center of this overlay—both horizontally and roughly vertically centered within the visible content area—a circular spinner or festive loading indicator appears, with a short loading message beneath it. The spinner and text together form a compact vertical stack with about 8–16px spacing, and ample whitespace surrounds them on all sides, echoing the spacious editorial feel. The underlying date header, summary, legend, and list are perceptible but inactive beneath the overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay anchored within the page-controller’s main content region, covering the content container while leaving the hero and nav visually intact above. It blocks interaction with all underlying content while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner sits at the very top of the white content section, immediately below the hero’s bottom edge and above the date header. It spans the full width of the centered content container, respecting the left and right padding, and appears as a horizontal strip with 16–24px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding. The message text is left-aligned within this strip, with any close icon or secondary link aligned to the far right on the same line. The banner height is modest—roughly the height of a single text line plus padding—so it feels noticeable but not overwhelming. A warm red-tinted background and subtle border differentiate it from the white content area, aligning with the festive palette while maintaining visual clarity. There is a comfortable 16–24px gap between the bottom of the error banner and the start of the date header below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the normal document flow, pushing content (date header, actions, columns) downward when present. It does not stick; it scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date header is positioned at the top-left of the white content container, forming the left side of the first horizontal row beneath the hero (and any error banner). It occupies roughly 60–65% of the container width, aligning to the left column of the implied grid. The main date title (e.g., 'My Night – December 18') is left-aligned and given prominence with a heading style similar in visual weight to the content section headline from the mockup. Below the main heading, any subtext such as the selected date range, short description, or a small date-picker control is stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing. The date header’s bottom edge aligns roughly with the bottom of the actions bar on the right, forming a tidy top row. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the date header and the beginning of the itinerary list below, maintaining a clear separation between page-level context and the list content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the left within the main content container. It forms the starting point of the two-column region; below it, the left column continues with the itinerary list. On narrower screens, it stacks above the actions bar and other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary panel resides in the right column of the main content area, acting like the supporting image in the original mockup. It is aligned to the top-right of the centered container, occupying roughly 35–40% of the container width and aligning with the right edge. Vertically, it sits just below the actions bar (if present) or roughly aligned with the top of the itinerary list in the left column, creating a cohesive two-column block. The panel has a card-like appearance: a light background or subtle border, rounded corners, and generous internal padding (24–32px), giving it a self-contained, airy feel. Inside, key metrics (e.g., total activities, total time, first/last start times) are arranged in a vertical stack or simple grid with 8–16px spacing, leaving ample whitespace between text elements. The panel’s height is moderate, extending down to roughly the upper third of the itinerary list, so it feels substantial but not as tall as the full list column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar element fixed within the right column of the grid. It scrolls with the page and does not overlap or float; its top aligns with the start of the main content section so that it is visible alongside the first screenful of the itinerary list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The actions bar is positioned on the right side of the top content row, horizontally aligned with the date header. It occupies the portion of the right column nearest the top, spanning roughly 35–40% of the container width but with a relatively shallow height. The bar is right-aligned within the container: its right edge aligns with the right edge of the summary panel below. Within the bar, primary actions like 'Print Itinerary' and 'Export' are arranged from right to left, typically as buttons or icon-text controls, with 8–16px horizontal spacing between them. There is generous whitespace above and below the buttons (about 8–12px), letting the bar feel integrated into the header row rather than heavy. A subtle bottom margin of around 16–24px separates the actions bar from the summary panel, so the two elements feel related but distinct. Visually, the actions bar echoes the restrained, editorial navigation of the mockup: minimal in height, visually tidy, and placed in the upper-right of the content zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the top-right within the main content container. It does not stick on scroll, maintaining the calm editorial feel. On smaller screens, it stacks below the date header and above the summary panel and list, centered or right-aligned depending on breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The conflict legend sits directly below the summary panel within the right column, using the same width (about 35–40% of the container) and aligning its left and right edges exactly with the summary panel for a clean vertical column. There is a comfortable gap of 24–32px between the bottom of the summary panel and the top of the legend, mirroring the vertical spacing between text blocks in the mockup. The legend itself is compact: small swatches or icons representing different states (e.g., conflict, warning, open gap) are laid out in a simple vertical list or two-column grid, each with a short label to the right. Each legend item has 4–8px of vertical spacing, and the entire component has modest internal padding (16–24px), so it reads as a light explanatory block rather than a dense key. Its overall height is shorter than the summary panel, and together they form a vertical sidebar that runs alongside the upper half or so of the itinerary list to the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the right column, stacked immediately below the summary panel. It scrolls with the page and never overlaps the itinerary list. On narrow viewports, it stacks underneath the summary panel and list as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The itinerary list occupies the main left column beneath the date header, stretching from just below the date header down through the content area. It uses about 60–65% of the container width and is left-aligned with the date header, creating a strong vertical column of content. There is about 24–32px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the date header and the first row in the list, establishing a clear transition from header to content. Each itinerary item and gap row spans the full width of this left column, with internal padding (16–24px vertically, 16–24px horizontally) to keep the layout airy. The list flows from top to bottom in chronological order, with clear separation between rows via subtle dividers, background stripes, or comfortable whitespace (8–16px between rows). As the user scrolls, this left column remains visually dominant, with the right column visible beside it in the initial viewport, then progressively scrolling off as the list extends downward. If the list is short, the white space below maintains the spacious, editorial feeling rather than trying to fill every pixel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable column within the main content area. It extends vertically as needed, with no internal scroll bars; the browser scroll handles the entire page. On smaller screens, it stacks above the summary and legend, still occupying the full width of the container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each itinerary item row is a full-width block within the left column of the list. The row has a comfortable height, driven by vertical padding of about 16–24px, ensuring the text and any icons never feel cramped. Content inside the row follows a left-to-right arrangement: start time and duration near the left edge, then the activity title and details slightly indented, with any status badges or conflict indicators placed near the right side. The text aligns to a consistent baseline across rows, giving the list an organized, editorial column feel. A subtle bottom border or a 8–12px gap of whitespace separates each row from the next. If an item is in conflict or has a special status, a slim colored bar (2–4px wide) may appear along the left edge of the row or a small color-coded pill next to the time, echoing the legend’s colors without overwhelming the overall calm layout. When scanned from top to bottom, these rows form a clear vertical narrative of the night, matching the reading-oriented emphasis of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level elements within the itinerary-list column. They expand vertically if activity details wrap onto multiple lines but always respect the same left and right padding, maintaining alignment with other rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gap row appears between itinerary item rows wherever there is a notable time gap. It spans the full width of the left column, but is visually lighter and slightly slimmer than an item row. Vertical padding is modest (8–12px), and the interior often features a horizontal, dashed or faint line running across most of the width, with a small label like '1 hr 30 min gap' centered or left-aligned along that line. The typography is smaller and more subdued than that of the main item titles, signaling that this is meta-information rather than a primary activity. Additional vertical spacing (4–8px) separates the gap row slightly from the adjacent item rows, creating a gentle visual breathing space between blocks of activities.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static elements that sit in-line within the itinerary-list column, always between two item rows (or between an item row and the end of the list). They scroll naturally with the list and never overlap other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no activities for the selected date, the empty state replaces the usual item and gap rows within the left column. It is centered horizontally within the left column and occupies roughly 60–70% of that column’s width, leaving generous side margins for a calm, open feel. Vertically, it is positioned with ample top margin (approximately 48–64px) below the date header, so it appears clearly separated from the header but above the fold where possible. The empty state usually appears as a card or light panel containing a small festive illustration or icon at the top, followed by a short headline (e.g., 'No activities yet for this night') and a brief instruction line, each separated by 8–16px. The card has comfortable padding (24–32px) and perhaps a subtle border or soft background tint. The right column (summary panel and legend) may still appear, reflecting zeros or 'no data' states, but their visual weight is secondary to the central empty-state card drawing attention in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that occupies the list area when there are no items. It scrolls with the rest of the content and disappears once the itinerary-list gains real item rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove confirmation modal is displayed above the entire page, dimming both the hero and main content beneath. A semi-transparent dark overlay covers the full viewport, uniformly darkening the background while still allowing the underlying page structure to be faintly visible. Centered in the viewport, the modal card sits with a width of roughly 400–480px (around 35–45% of the page width on desktop) and auto height. It has a clean, white background, subtle drop shadow, and rounded corners that echo the card styling of the summary panel. Inside, the modal’s content is arranged in a vertical stack: a concise title at the top, then one or two lines of explanatory copy, and finally a horizontal row of confirm/cancel buttons aligned to the bottom-right. Vertical spacing between these elements is consistent (8–16px), and generous padding (24–32px) surrounds the content. The overall impression is calm and deliberate, giving users a clear, focused moment to make a decision without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, with a full-screen dimming backdrop. It remains fixed in place regardless of underlying page scroll and blocks interaction with all underlying components until dismissed.

name: Accessibility
title: Accessibility
description: Provides detailed accessibility information for the attraction, including entrances, viewing areas, and a dedicated accessibility contact form.

core_features: Display an accessibility overview using getAccessibilityOverview, covering wheelchair access, sensory-friendly information, parking, restrooms, and other services.
List all entrances and indicate which are accessible using listAccessibleEntrances, with selection of an entrance to view detailed information via getEntranceDetails.
Show a map area with pins for entrances and accessible viewing areas, synchronized with the entrance and viewing area lists.
List all accessible viewing areas using listAccessibleViewingAreas, with selection of a viewing area to load details via getAccessibleViewingAreaDetails.
Support deep linking and pre-selection of an entrance or viewing area using URL parameters entranceId and viewingAreaId.
Provide a dedicated accessibility contact form that submits inquiries via submitAccessibilityInquiry with topic, name, email, message, and relatedContext.
Offer a "Contact Accessibility Team" call-to-action that scrolls to or reveals the contact form and, when relevant, pre-associates the selected entrance or viewing area.
Include a secondary navigation link to contact.html with prefilled topic and context parameters for users who want more help outside the dedicated accessibility form.
user_workflows:
View general accessibility information:
1. User navigates to accessibility.html.
2. Page controller component calls getAccessibilityOverview.
3. Accessibility overview component renders wheelchair access, sensory-friendly, parking, restrooms, and other services information.
4. User optionally clicks a secondary link below the overview to open contact.html with topic=accessibility_mobility and context derived from the currently selected entrance or viewing area, if any.

Find an accessible entrance and view its details:
1. User navigates to accessibility.html (optionally with entranceId in the URL).
2. Page controller calls listAccessibleEntrances to populate the entrance list and map pins.
3. If entranceId is present in the URL, controller calls getEntranceDetails(entranceId) and opens the entrance details panel and highlights the corresponding map pin.
4. User clicks an entrance item in the list or a map pin; controller updates the URL parameter entranceId and invokes getEntranceDetails with the selected id.
5. Entrance details panel shows description, wheelchair accessibility flag, directions, notes, and nearbyAccessibleViewingAreas.
6. From within the entrance details, user can click a nearby accessible viewing area; controller updates viewingAreaId in the URL, calls getAccessibleViewingAreaDetails, and opens the viewing area details panel.

Find an accessible viewing area and view its details:
1. User navigates to accessibility.html (optionally with viewingAreaId in the URL).
2. Page controller calls listAccessibleViewingAreas to build the viewing areas list and map pins.
3. If viewingAreaId is present in the URL, controller calls getAccessibleViewingAreaDetails(viewingAreaId) and opens the viewing area details panel and highlights the corresponding map pin.
