On successful getGroupSessionDetails response: Store or bind groupSessionOption, session (if present), and priceEstimate to the relevant components. Initialize the group size input and pricing summary from priceEstimate.groupSize and priceEstimate.totalPrice.

On group size input change: Validate the input against groupSessionOption.minGroupSize and groupSessionOption.maxGroupSize (if defined). If invalid, show validation feedback and do not call getGroupSessionDetails. If valid and different from the currently loaded group size, update the groupSize URL parameter and re-call getGroupSessionDetails to refresh priceEstimate.

On payment method selection: Update the selectedPaymentMethod state based on the user’s choice, limited to values present in groupSessionOption.paymentMethodsAvailable. If a previously selected method becomes invalid due to a refreshed groupSessionOption response, clear the selection and prompt the user to choose again.
On contact info input: Update local form state for contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail, and perform basic client-side validation (e.g., required fields, basic email format) before submission.

On booking form submit: Prevent default form submission, ensure that groupSessionOptionId, current groupSize, selected paymentMethod, contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail are all present and valid. If validation passes, call createGroupBooking. Disable the submit button and show a loading indicator while waiting.

On successful createGroupBooking response (success=true): Display the booking-confirmation component with details from groupBooking, including groupBooking.id, groupBooking.groupSize, groupBooking.paymentMethod, and contact details. Optionally update the URL with a non-PII parameter (such as bookingStatus) to indicate that the booking is confirmed, without re-calling createGroupBooking.

On failed createGroupBooking response (success=false or error): Display an error message in a persistent error banner, re-enable the submit button, and keep form values intact for correction and resubmission.
On click of Back to Group Times link/button: Navigate to groups.html without preserving the groupSessionOptionId or groupSize in the URL.

On browser back/forward navigation: Re-read groupSessionOptionId and groupSize from the URL. If they differ from the current in-memory state, re-call getGroupSessionDetails to ensure that displayed information matches the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL query parameters and API responses. The required URL parameter groupSessionOptionId identifies the group session option to display; without it, the page cannot function and must show an error with a link back to groups.html. The groupSize URL parameter seeds the initial requested group size. On load, if groupSize can be parsed as a positive number, it is passed to getGroupSessionDetails; otherwise, the page treats the state as invalid and prompts the user to return to groups.html. The getGroupSessionDetails response provides groupSessionOption, an optional session, and priceEstimate. These outputs become the canonical state for the current view and are not hardcoded: the group size displayed in the UI is bound to priceEstimate.groupSize rather than directly to the URL, so that any server-side adjustments to group size (for example, clamping to allowed minima or maxima) are reflected. When the user changes the group size, the page first validates the input against groupSessionOption.minGroupSize and groupSessionOption.maxGroupSize (if present). If acceptable, the page updates the groupSize parameter in the URL (e.g., by mutating the existing query using history APIs without a full reload), then calls getGroupSessionDetails again with the new groupSize. This keeps the URL in sync with the visible state, enabling refresh and deep-linking with a specific group size. Booking state is primarily kept in memory via the groupBooking object returned by createGroupBooking. To avoid storing personal data in the URL, the page does not propagate contact details or booking IDs into query parameters. Instead, an optional non-identifying query parameter (such as bookingStatus) can be set on successful submission, allowing the page to infer that a booking was recently completed if reloaded immediately. However, because there is no API on this page to retrieve existing bookings by ID, the confirmation details shown rely solely on the response of the most recent createGroupBooking call in the current page lifecycle. All state persistence across reloads for session choice and group size relies on URL parameters; no localStorage or other client-side storage is used.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading groupSessionOptionId and groupSize from the URL, validating them, and loading group session details via getGroupSessionDetails. Manages global loading and error states for initial data fetch.

Displays the core details of the selected group session based on groupSessionOption and optional session data, including date/time, name, and any badges indicating discounts. Shows an error message if the group session is inactive or could not be loaded.

Allows the user to confirm or adjust the group size. Initializes from priceEstimate.groupSize and enforces validation rules using groupSessionOption.minGroupSize and groupSessionOption.maxGroupSize (if defined). When the user changes the group size and the new value is valid, updates the groupSize URL parameter and triggers a fresh getGroupSessionDetails call to update pricing.

Shows a live pricing summary for the current group size using priceEstimate from getGroupSessionDetails. Displays priceEstimate.pricePerPerson, priceEstimate.groupSize, priceEstimate.totalPrice, and priceEstimate.currency. Updates whenever a new getGroupSessionDetails response is received after a group size change.

Presents the available payment methods for the selected group session based on groupSessionOption.paymentMethodsAvailable. Ensures that only the methods provided by the API (such as pay_on_arrival, invoice_later, or pay_online) can be selected. The selected payment method is required before booking submission and is passed to createGroupBooking.

Captures group contact information including contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail, which are required for createGroupBooking. Provides inline validation for required fields and basic formatting checks (e.g., email structure). Exposes validated values to the booking submission component.

Wraps the group-size-form, payment-method-selector, and contact-information-form into a single submittable form. On submit, verifies that groupSessionOptionId and a valid groupSize are available, ensures that a payment method has been selected, and that contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail are valid. If all checks pass, calls createGroupBooking with groupSessionOptionId, current group size, chosen paymentMethod, and contact fields. Manages submit button disabled state and loading indicators, and routes success or error responses to the booking-confirmation-panel or error messaging.

Displays confirmation details after a successful createGroupBooking call, including a reference identifier from groupBooking.id, groupBooking.groupSize, groupBooking.paymentMethod, and echoing back the contactEmail and contactName. Optionally references the most recent priceEstimate for informational total cost display. Shows contextual instructions based on paymentMethod (for example, distinguishing between pay_on_arrival and invoice_later) without initiating any payment.

Provides a simple navigation control to return the user to the group sessions list page at groups.html. Does not carry any state forward in the URL, allowing the groups.html page to manage its own search or filter state.

Displays non-field-specific error messages, including failures from getGroupSessionDetails (e.g., invalid groupSessionOptionId, inactive session) or createGroupBooking (e.g., server-side validation errors). Provides feedback with no hardcoded content, using the message returned from createGroupBooking or generic messages when none is available.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup: a full-width festive hero at the top with the global navigation overlaid, then a white content area in a centered container (~1100–1200px max width). Within the white area, the layout uses a two-column structure on desktop: the left column (about 60–65% width) holds the main booking flow (group size, payment choice, contact details, submit), while the right column (about 35–40% width) holds contextual information (session details, price summary, and later the confirmation summary). On smaller screens, these columns stack into a single column, with the booking flow above and the summaries below. Utility elements (global error banner, back link) sit at the top of the white content area before the columns, aligning left to maintain a reading-first feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom editorial flow as the mockup: a full-width festive hero at the top with the global navigation overlaid, then a white content area in a centered container (~1100–1200px max width). Within the white area, the layout uses a two-column structure on desktop: the left column (about 60–65% width) holds the main booking flow (group size, payment choice, contact details, submit), while the right column (about 35–40% width) holds contextual information (session details, price summary, and later the confirmation summary). On smaller screens, these columns stack into a single column, with the booking flow above and the summaries below. Utility elements (global error banner, back link) sit at the top of the white content area before the columns, aligning left to maintain a reading-first feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 35–40% of the initial viewport, with generous vertical padding around the page title to mirror the 'Our History' treatment. The navigation remains slim along the top edge, overlaying the background image. Below the hero, the main content section starts with comfortable top padding (48–64px) before the forms. Within the left column, each form block is treated like an editorial section with clear separation: 24–32px vertical spacing between subsections (group size, payment method, contact info, submit area) and 16–24px between related controls within a subsection. The right column stacks panels (session details, price summary, confirmation) with 24px vertical gaps, and each panel uses 16–24px internal padding. There is generous horizontal margin inside the main container, leaving whitespace flanking both columns.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is kept low-to-medium, optimized for clarity and confidence in a transactional context. Text lines in labels and helper copy are kept to an approximate 55–65 character width by constraining the left column. Form sections avoid crowding: fields group logically with subtle separation, and no panels abut each other without whitespace. The right-hand panels use card-like blocks with ample padding and whitespace, echoing the editorial spacing of the mockup’s text and image. Overall, the layout breathes and feels calm despite being form-heavy, matching the spacious, editorial style.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the top-left logo and top-right navigation in the hero, then moves to the centered hero heading 'Group Session Booking'. From there, the eye transitions down into the white content area, first hitting the left-aligned back link, then any global error banner just below it if present, and then the primary left-column booking content: starting at the top with the session summary headline (within the booking context) and group size controls, followed by payment method, contact information, and the booking submission area. In parallel, the right-hand column panels act as a secondary attraction much like the image in the mockup: as users scan down the left column, their attention is periodically pulled to the right to review session and price details, creating a mild Z-pattern across the top of the content area and then down the left. After submission, the confirmation panel becomes the main focal point near the top of the white content area, while the right column still supports as a secondary reference.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width hero featuring a rich red bokeh Christmas background and the global navigation sitting lightly along the top edge. Centered within this hero, a large, elegant 'Group Session Booking' title floats with generous vertical padding above and below, echoing the dramatic yet minimal hero from the mockup. As the user scrolls into the white content section, a centered content container appears, framed by wide left and right margins. At the top-left of this container is a subtle 'Back to group times' link, followed by a slim, full-width error banner area that only appears when needed, maintaining a clean default state. Below this, the layout splits into two columns: the broader left column, dedicated to the booking journey, reads like an editorial article broken into clear segments—group size first, then payment method, then contact details, and finally the booking action area—all separated by consistent vertical spacing and aligned to a comfortable text width. The narrower right column stacks the group session details and price summary in card-like panels, each aligned to the top of the left content and extending downward, similar to the tall image column in the mockup. On completion, a confirmation panel emerges prominently in the main content area, while the right-hand panels remain available as a secondary reference. The combination of festive hero, clean white content, restrained typography, and ample whitespace produces a modern, calm, and seasonal booking experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component owns the hero region at the very top of the page. It spans the full browser width and approximately 35–40% of the initial viewport height, using the deep red bokeh/sparkle Christmas background. The global navigation is anchored along the top edge of this hero, stretching horizontally from left to right with a modest height so it feels light and overlayed. The 'Group Session Booking' page title sits centered both horizontally and approximately in the vertical middle of the hero, with generous padding above and below (48–64px from the hero’s edges) to give it strong prominence. Any optional supporting subtitle or short description sits directly beneath the main title, center-aligned, with moderate spacing (16–24px) while still maintaining an airy feel. The bottom edge of this component transitions smoothly into the white background of the main content section, either via a soft drop shadow or a clean straight edge, consistent with the mockup’s top hero treatment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in position at the top of the page as the initial section; scrolls away naturally as the user moves down (not sticky). Background image covers the hero area using a full-bleed cover behavior, while inner text and navigation remain constrained within the centered max-width content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group session details panel sits in the right-hand column of the main white content area, aligned to the top of the left booking column. Inside the centered max-width container, the overall width is split so that this right column occupies about 35–40% of the container width on desktop. The panel is the first card in this right column stack, positioned at the top with its top edge aligned roughly with the top of the first major block in the left column (typically the group size section), creating a cohesive horizontal starting line. The panel itself is rendered as a white card with subtle border or shadow, 16–24px internal padding on all sides, and rounded corners consistent with the site’s style. It contains a concise heading (e.g., 'Selected Session') at the top left of the card, followed by structured details (date, time, location, session type) stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing. The card does not stretch full height; instead, it has a natural height determined by its content and ends with comfortable bottom padding, leaving at least 24px of whitespace padding to the next panel below (the price summary).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls normally with the page. On narrow viewports, it reflows below the main booking form, becoming a full-width card with the same padding and spacing but stacking vertically in the single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group-size-form occupies the top portion of the left column in the white content area. It is positioned just below the global error banner (if shown) and back link, with 32–48px of vertical spacing separating it from those utilities to establish it as the main entry point into the booking flow. Within the centered container, the left column spans roughly 60–65% of the width, and the group size section uses this full left-column width, resulting in a comfortable reading line length. The section starts with a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Group details' or 'Group size') at the top, using the same editorial hierarchy as the mockup’s section titles. Immediately below the heading, there may be a short explanatory line or helper text, followed by the actual input controls (e.g., number steppers, dropdowns, or segmented controls) aligned in a vertical flow. Vertical spacing between the heading, helper text, and controls is moderate (12–16px), and fields within the section are spaced 8–16px apart to avoid crowding. The bottom of this component has 24–32px of whitespace before the next form section (payment method), visually reading like distinct paragraphs in an article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column and part of the overall booking form flow. On smaller screens, it becomes a full-width block at the top of the single column, maintaining the same vertical structure and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price summary panel is positioned directly below the group session details panel in the right-hand column, maintaining a vertical stack of informational cards. It uses the same column width (35–40% of the main container) and visual style: a white card with subtle border or shadow, 16–24px internal padding, and rounded corners. The top of the price summary card sits about 24px below the bottom of the session details panel, preserving clear separation. Within the card, a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Price Summary') appears at the top with 8–12px of spacing before a breakdown list (per person price, number of attendees, any discounts, and total). Totals are emphasized via typographic hierarchy but still within the clean, editorial style. The card’s bottom edge leaves comfortable breathing room (24–32px) above any subsequent right-column content, such as a confirmation snippet if present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the right column. On small screens, it appears immediately under the session details card within the single-column flow, expanding to full container width while preserving internal spacing and hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-method-selector appears in the left column immediately beneath the group-size-form, separated from it by 24–32px of vertical whitespace so it reads as the next logical step in the booking flow. Occupying the full width of the left column, it maintains the same text-width comfort as the surrounding sections. The component begins with a left-aligned label or subheading (e.g., 'Payment method') aligned to the same vertical grid as the group size heading, ensuring a clear typographic rhythm. Below this label, the available payment options (e.g., 'Pay on arrival', 'Invoice the organization') are presented as either radio-card options or a concise list of radio buttons, stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing between each option. Each option has sufficient padding around its label so the area feels tappable and uncluttered, mirroring the mockup’s spacious spacing between textual blocks. A small helper line or note about no online payment, if present, appears below the options, separated by 8–12px. The bottom of this component leaves 24–32px before the contact-information-form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the left column, flowing naturally as part of the booking form. On smaller screens, it remains in the same relative order, stacked beneath the group size section and above the contact information section in the single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-information-form sits in the left column below the payment-method-selector, again separated by 24–32px of vertical space to maintain readability. It spans the full width of the left column and is one of the taller sections, reflecting its multi-field nature. The section starts with a left-aligned heading like 'Contact details', placed on the same typographic grid as earlier section headings. Below the heading, fields such as name, email, phone, and organization are organized in a primarily vertical layout; any side-by-side fields (e.g., first/last name) are used sparingly and respect the base grid, with 16–24px horizontal gaps when used. Vertical spacing between individual fields is 12–16px, keeping the section open and easy to scan. Labels sit directly above their inputs, aligned left, with 4–8px spacing between label and field. Optional helper text or error messages align beneath each relevant field with 4–8px spacing, maintaining a tidy and editorial feel. The bottom of the contact-information-form leaves 24–32px before the group-booking-form’s submit area begins, visually grouping all contact-related inputs together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; scrolls with the rest of the page. On mobile, it becomes a full-width section within the single-column flow, maintaining the same vertical order and spacing, with any side-by-side fields stacking vertically if needed to preserve legibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group-booking-form acts as the overarching container for the left-column booking sections: it wraps the group-size-form, payment-method-selector, contact-information-form, and the final booking action area. Visually, the form’s boundaries are implied by alignment rather than a heavy border: all sections share the same left alignment and column width, creating a cohesive vertical block starting a short distance (32–48px) below the bottom of the hero and utilities. The final part of this component is the submission area, placed at the bottom of the left column after the contact-information-form. This submission area includes the primary 'Submit booking' button aligned to the left, with sufficient whitespace around it (at least 24px above from the last input, 16–24px below) to give it breathing room. Any legal notices, confirmation of 'no payment will be taken online', or small-print text are positioned beneath the button, left-aligned and constrained within the same text width, spaced 8–12px below the button. The entire vertical stack of this form reads like a sequence of well-separated editorial sections, with a clear top-to-bottom narrative from selecting group size, through payment mode, to providing contact info, and finally submitting the booking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, dominating the left column of the main content. On larger screens it maintains its column alignment next to the informational panels. On smaller screens, the entire form becomes a single, full-width stack above the right-column panels, preserving the internal section order and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-panel appears in the white content area after a successful submission and becomes the new primary focal point. It is positioned at the top of the main container, just below the hero and any persistent utilities, and can either replace or appear in place of the left-column form content. On desktop, it typically spans the full width of the content container or at least aligns with the left column but can visually read as a prominent card centered within the container. The panel has generous padding (24–32px) and a clear heading (e.g., 'Booking confirmed') aligned to the left, followed by a concise confirmation message and key booking details (date, time, group size) laid out in a vertical stack with 8–16px spacing between lines. There is abundant whitespace around this panel: 32–48px of vertical gap above and below, isolating it from the remaining right-column informational panels or any residual content. A secondary action, such as a link to print or review details, sits near the bottom of the panel aligned to the left or right, but still within the card’s padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered: hidden until a booking is successfully submitted. When visible, it can either push the booking form content below (if both are shown) or replace the booking form in that region, keeping the right column panels visible as secondary reference. On small screens, it appears at the top of the single-column flow, spanning full width and pushing other content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-group-times-link is placed near the top of the main white content area, directly below the hero and within the centered content container. It is left-aligned, sitting on the same vertical grid as the left column content. There is generous space (24–32px) between the bottom of the hero and this link, allowing it to be easily discoverable without feeling cramped. The link may include a left-pointing chevron icon followed by the text (e.g., 'Back to group times'), with a small 4–8px gap between icon and text. The link itself is not enclosed in a card; it floats on the white background with ample whitespace above and below—8–16px below it before any global error banner or the first form section—so that it reads as a lightweight, tertiary navigation control similar to a breadcrumb in an editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content container, always appearing above the two-column layout. On smaller screens, it remains at the top of the single-column flow, directly under the hero, ensuring a consistent navigation anchor for users.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-error-banner occupies a reserved strip directly beneath the back-to-group-times-link and above the main booking content within the white section. It spans the full width of the centered content container (matching the combined width of the two columns) but maintains a modest height determined by its text content. When visible, it appears as a horizontally oriented band with a subtle but noticeable background color (e.g., soft red) and 12–16px of vertical padding inside, with the error message text left-aligned and constrained to the same text width as the left column. There is 8–12px of whitespace between the back link and the top of the banner, and 16–24px between the bottom of the banner and the start of the left-column form section, ensuring it feels connected but not cramped. When no global error is present, this area collapses entirely, preserving the spaciousness of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally; when present, it pushes the rest of the content downward. It scrolls with the page (not sticky), preserving the page’s natural reading flow. On smaller screens, it remains full-width within the single-column layout, appearing immediately below the back link and above the form stack.

name: Holiday Newsletter Signup
title: Holiday Newsletter Signup
description: Page where visitors can subscribe to the holiday newsletter, choose their interests and preferred email frequency, and receive a confirmation of their subscription.

core_features: Fetch available newsletter interest categories and frequency options using getNewsletterSignupOptions and expose them to the signup form.
Render a newsletter signup form that collects subscriber details (email, first name, postal/ZIP code) and allows selecting one or more interest categories from the dynamically loaded list.
Render a frequency selector using only the dynamically loaded frequency options, ensuring that the option with id "weekly" (if returned) can be selected by the user.
Perform client-side validation for required fields (email, at least one interest, frequency; optionally first name/postal code if business rules require) before calling createNewsletterSubscription.
Submit the subscription request via createNewsletterSubscription with the user’s details, selected interests, and selected frequency.

Handle createNewsletterSubscription responses by showing success or error messages based on the returned success flag and message, and by optionally surfacing details from the returned NewsletterSubscription object.
Display a dedicated post-submission confirmation state on successful subscription that includes a clear acknowledgment and a "Return to Home" navigation control leading back to index.html.
Honor the incoming URL parameter "source" (if present) by capturing it at load and using it to adjust non-visual behavior such as default selections or tracking; avoid storing it in localStorage.
Represent key interaction states (loading options, form ready, submitting, submission success, submission error) so that components can render appropriate controls and messages.
user_workflows:
1) New subscription from homepage banner:
- User clicks a newsletter link on index.html that navigates to newsletter.html?source=home_banner.
- On page load, the options loader component calls getNewsletterSignupOptions and enters a loading state.
- When options are returned, the signup form is populated with interestOptions and frequencyOptions.
- The page captures the URL parameter source=home_banner in state. If frequencyOptions includes an option whose id is "weekly", the form may preselect that option to align with banner expectations; otherwise it leaves frequency unselected.
- User enters email, optionally first name and postal code, and selects one or more interests and a frequency (which may already be preselected).
- User clicks the primary submit control (e.g., "Sign Up" or "Subscribe").
- Form validation runs; if any required data is missing or invalid, errors are shown and submission is blocked until corrected.
- On passing validation, the form calls createNewsletterSubscription with {email, firstName, postalCode, interests, frequency} collected from the form.
- While waiting for the API response, the page enters a submitting state (e.g., disabling the submit control).
- If the response.success is true, the subscription-status handler switches the view to a success confirmation state, optionally updates the URL to include subscriptionStatus=success (without reloading), and may display details from response.subscription.
- User clicks "Return to Home" from the confirmation state, navigating to index.html (without additional required parameters).

2) New subscription from footer link without source parameter:
- User clicks the "Holiday Newsletter" footer link, navigating to newsletter.html with no additional query parameters.
- On page load, the options loader calls getNewsletterSignupOptions and waits for a response.
- When data is loaded, the form appears with dynamic interestOptions and frequencyOptions but no source-specific preconfiguration.
- User completes the form fields, selects at least one interest and a frequency, and submits.
- createNewsletterSubscription is called; on success, a confirmation state is shown; on error, error messaging is displayed and the form remains visible for corrections or retry.

3) Error and retry flow:
- User accesses newsletter.html (with or without a source parameter) and completes the form but omits a required field or selects no interests.
- On submit, client-side validation fails; the form-error-summary component displays a list of issues and focuses the first invalid field.
- User corrects the issues and resubmits; createNewsletterSubscription is called.
- If the API returns success=false, the form-error-summary shows the API error message returned in response.message, and the form remains editable.
- User can adjust their entries and submit again until a success response is returned or they abandon the form.

4) Page reload with subscription status in URL:
- After a successful subscription, the subscription-status handler optionally updates the URL using the History API to add subscriptionStatus=success.
- If the user reloads newsletter.html?subscriptionStatus=success (e.g., via refresh or bookmarked URL), the state logic detects subscriptionStatus=success on load.
- When subscriptionStatus=success is present, the page initializes directly into the confirmation state instead of showing the empty signup form. The user can then click "Return to Home" or navigate elsewhere without resubmitting the form.
interactions: On page load of newsletter.html, the newsletter-signup-options-loader component triggers getNewsletterSignupOptions (onLoad) and populates shared state with interestOptions and frequencyOptions or exposes an error state if the call fails.

If getNewsletterSignupOptions fails, the user is shown an error state with a control (e.g., "Try again") that, when clicked, retriggers getNewsletterSignupOptions via the loader’s onErrorRetry handler.

As the user types into the email, firstName, or postalCode inputs, or toggles interest checkboxes and frequency selection controls, the newsletter-signup-form component’s onChange handler updates local form state and clears field-level validation errors for those fields.

When the user clicks the form’s submit control, the newsletter-signup-form onSubmit handler performs client-side validation based on the current form state and only proceeds to call createNewsletterSubscription if all validation rules pass.

The createNewsletterSubscription call sends only dynamically captured values: email, firstName, postalCode, interests (array of selected interest IDs from interestOptions), and frequency (ID of the selected frequency from frequencyOptions).
While createNewsletterSubscription is in progress, the subscription-status-handler sets a submitting flag, which the form uses to disable the submit control and prevent duplicate submissions.

Upon receive of the createNewsletterSubscription response, the subscription-status-handler invokes onSubscriptionCreated: if success is true, it stores the returned NewsletterSubscription object, updates page state to success, and may update the URL to include subscriptionStatus=success; if success is false, it passes the error message to the form-error-summary and keeps the form visible.
When the confirmation state is visible, the return-home-link component is active. Clicking it triggers a standard navigation to index.html as described in the outgoing navigation configuration.

If a user dismisses the confirmation state (for example, via a "Back to form" or close control, if implemented), the subscription-status-handler onDismissConfirmation can clear the subscriptionStatus=success URL parameter (if present) and return the page to an idle form state while leaving any existing form values as needed.

If a source URL parameter is present (e.g., newsletter.html?source=home_banner or newsletter.html?source=footer_link), the state manager reads it on load and stores it as contextual state. Other components may use this context for behavior such as default frequency selection (when a matching option exists) or for internal tracking; it is not sent to createNewsletterSubscription since that interface does not accept a source parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s state is driven by a combination of URL parameters and in-memory component state, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page parses the URL for supported parameters: (1) source: if present, its string value is stored as a page-level context (e.g., 'home_banner', 'footer_link', or other tags used by linking pages). This context can influence default form behavior, such as preselecting a particular frequency option when a matching option id is available in frequencyOptions, but it is purely behavioral and not persisted to any backend via the assigned interfaces. (2) subscriptionStatus: when equal to 'success', the subscription-status-handler initializes the page in a success/confirmation state rather than the blank form state. This parameter can be set or cleared using the browser History API after a successful createNewsletterSubscription call, enabling the confirmation state to survive page refresh or bookmarking without resubmitting the form. In-memory state tracks phases within a single visit: an optionsLoading flag (true while waiting for getNewsletterSignupOptions), an optionsError flag/message (if loading fails), a formData object (email, firstName, postalCode, selectedInterests, selectedFrequency), a formValidationErrors structure, a submitting flag (true while createNewsletterSubscription is in flight), and a subscriptionResult object (holding the latest NewsletterSubscription and API message). Components read and update this shared state in response to events (load, change, submit, API responses), but persistent behavior across page reloads relies solely on URL parameters, keeping the implementation stateless with respect to client storage.

components.functionality: On page load, this component calls getNewsletterSignupOptions to retrieve the dynamic lists of interestOptions and frequencyOptions used to construct the newsletter preferences UI. It manages loading and error states and exposes the loaded options to other components.

This component renders the newsletter signup form fields, binds them to internal form state, and coordinates validation and submission. It presents inputs for email, first name, postal/ZIP code, and dynamically generated controls for choosing interests (based on interestOptions) and a frequency (based on frequencyOptions). On submit, it validates the entries and, if valid, calls createNewsletterSubscription with the current form values.

This component centralizes the subscription lifecycle state, including tracking whether the page is idle, loading options, ready, submitting, or in a success/error state. It triggers createNewsletterSubscription when instructed by the form, processes the API response, stores the resulting NewsletterSubscription object, updates view state to show confirmation or errors, and manages the subscriptionStatus URL parameter so the success state can persist across reloads.

Aggregates and displays validation and API error messages related to the signup form. It receives error details from the newsletter-signup-form and subscription-status-handler, presents them in a consolidated list, and can coordinate focus management (e.g., focusing the first invalid field) to improve usability.

Visible in the success/confirmation state, this component provides a control that navigates the user back to the site’s home page (index.html) after a successful subscription, as defined in the outgoing navigation configuration.

Responsible for parsing and interpreting URL parameters on initial load (such as source and subscriptionStatus), storing them in page-level state, and exposing this context to other components. It does not persist anything to localStorage and relies solely on URL parameters and in-memory state for behavior control.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s pattern: a full-width, red bokeh hero at the top with the site logo and navigation overlaid along the top edge, and a large, centered page title such as “Holiday Newsletter.” Directly beneath the hero, a white content section spans the center of the viewport within a max-width container (~1100–1200px) using a two-column layout. The left column (about 60–65% width) carries the functional, text-heavy content: the newsletter signup form, its error summary, and the subscription state messages. The right column (about 35–40% width) hosts a tall, festive illustration and a brief descriptive blurb about the newsletter, echoing the historical image from the mockup. Utility logic components (loader, URL/state handlers) affect what shows in the left column but have no standalone visual presence.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s pattern: a full-width, red bokeh hero at the top with the site logo and navigation overlaid along the top edge, and a large, centered page title such as “Holiday Newsletter.” Directly beneath the hero, a white content section spans the center of the viewport within a max-width container (~1100–1200px) using a two-column layout. The left column (about 60–65% width) carries the functional, text-heavy content: the newsletter signup form, its error summary, and the subscription state messages. The right column (about 35–40% width) hosts a tall, festive illustration and a brief descriptive blurb about the newsletter, echoing the historical image from the mockup. Utility logic components (loader, URL/state handlers) affect what shows in the left column but have no standalone visual presence.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) around the centered hero heading, keeping the navigation compact along the top. Below, the main content section sits on a white background with ~64px of vertical separation from the hero. Within the centered container, the two columns sit side by side with a 32–40px gutter. The left column form and messages use roomy internal spacing: 24–32px padding from column edges and 16–24px vertical spacing between headings, error summary, fields, and actions. The right column image stretches vertically to align with the top of the left column’s heading and may extend beyond the form’s height, preserving a similar visual rhythm to the tall historical image in the mockup. A comfortable 48–64px margin separates the bottom of the main content from the page footer.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a low-to-medium density, optimized for ease of reading and calm interaction. Text blocks (headings, short intros, help text) are well spaced, and form fields are grouped logically with 16–24px vertical gaps. There is ample whitespace to the left and right of the entire container, and between the two columns, preventing any feeling of crowding. The error summary, when visible, occupies its own clear block with sufficient padding so it feels important but not overwhelming. Decorative and explanatory elements in the right column have generous breathing room, staying secondary to the main task: completing the signup form.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the logo at the top-left and tracks across the navigation, then settles on the large, centered “Holiday Newsletter” hero heading against the red festive background. From there, the user’s gaze flows down into the white content area, landing on the left-aligned section heading above the signup form, then moving downward through the form, field by field. If visible, the error summary sits early in this path, just below the form heading, so it is naturally encountered. The right column’s tall festive illustration and descriptive text act as a secondary attractor, pulling the eye across after the user has oriented themselves on the form, echoing the mild Z-pattern of the mockup: top-left logo → top-right navigation → center hero title → down-left form heading → across to the right-side imagery.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a dramatic, full-width hero: a deep red bokeh background with subtle sparkles, the attraction’s logo and navigation floating elegantly along the top edge. Centered in this space, a large, warm title such as “Holiday Newsletter” sits with ample breathing room above and below, setting a festive but refined tone. As you scroll slightly or let your eyes drop, the background transitions cleanly to a white content section framed within a centered, max-width container. Here, a two-column layout emerges. On the left, a broad column anchored to the top of this white section introduces the newsletter with a concise heading and a short lead-in, immediately followed by the signup form. The form is vertically stacked, calm, and well spaced, with any error summary appearing in a clear band near the top. On the right, a narrower column begins at the same vertical starting line as the form heading, featuring a tall, softly framed holiday illustration—perhaps Santa’s Castle in the snow—and a brief editorial-style description of what subscribers can expect. The right column remains visually lighter and more decorative, while the left column retains functional primacy. At the bottom of the left column, beneath the form’s primary actions and any confirmation message, a subtle “Return home” link provides an easy escape back to the main site. The overall feeling is editorial and spacious: festive color in the hero, restrained typography and generous whitespace in the body, and a clear visual path that leads the visitor gently but firmly toward completing the signup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data loader has no fixed visual presence, but when loading is required (for example, fetching available interest categories), it affects the left column of the main content section where the form lives. In its active visual state, it appears as a subtle overlay within the left column only: a translucent white veil covers the area where the form will appear, starting directly beneath the section heading and extending down to approximately where the bottom of the form normally sits. Centered horizontally and vertically within this veiled area, a small, circular loading indicator (or a simple “Loading your holiday options...” text line) is shown. The overlay respects the left column’s padding, staying 24–32px away from the container edges, and does not extend into the right column. On smaller screens where the layout becomes single-column, this loading state spans the full width of the content container, still centered on the screen and occupying roughly the top half of the visible form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader is confined to the left content column and scrolls with the page. When active, it temporarily covers the form area without shifting the overall layout, then fades out to reveal the form once data is ready. It does not fix to the viewport; instead, it is anchored in place relative to the form’s position within the white content section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter signup form is the primary interactive element and sits in the left column of the main white content section, directly beneath a short section heading and optional introductory sentence. This left column occupies about 60–65% of the central container’s width and aligns to the left edge of the container with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. The form itself spans the full width of this left column, starting roughly 48–64px below the bottom of the hero. Within the form, elements are arranged in a simple vertical stack: heading/intro text at the top, followed by the form-error-summary when present, then the individual field groups (email, interests, frequency, consent), each separated by 16–24px of vertical space. The primary action buttons (e.g., “Subscribe”) sit toward the bottom of the form, left-aligned with the input fields, with a comfortable 24–32px gap from the last input. The overall width of the text in this column is constrained to a comfortable reading line length (~55–65 characters) and ensures labels and helper text don’t feel cramped. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, the two columns stack: the form shifts to full-width at the top of the white section, maintaining similar vertical padding and spacing, with the decorative right-column content moving below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form is static and scrolls naturally with the page. It remains in the left column on larger screens but expands to full width when the layout collapses to a single column. Within its area, states managed by other components (loading, success, error) transition in place without repositioning the form container itself, preserving a stable layout as the user interacts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager controls what appears in the same space as the newsletter-signup-form within the left column of the main content section. In the default state, the full form is visible as described. After a successful subscription, the form is replaced in situ by a confirmation block that uses the same horizontal footprint: a centered success heading and short message appear where the form fields were, aligned to the left column’s padding and starting at roughly the same top position as the form’s first field group. The success message might include a brief summary of the user’s chosen interests and an indication of their email frequency, all staying within the left column’s width and maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing between lines. If the handler needs to display a non-validation error (e.g., server issue or already-subscribed notice), it appears in a message band positioned above the form or confirmation content, spanning the full width of the left column and separated from the main content by about 16px. At no point does this state manager occupy space outside the left column; it simply swaps or augments content within that reserved area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Spatially, this component is static within the left column and does not create separate fixed or floating elements. It swaps content (form vs. confirmation) in place, preserving the same column position and approximate height to avoid jarring jumps. Transitions should be smooth (e.g., fade or gentle slide), but the overall container’s position relative to the hero and the right column remains unchanged.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error summary appears inside the newsletter-signup-form at the top of the left column content block. When validation errors occur, it is positioned directly below the form’s title and any short introductory copy and directly above the first field group. It spans the full width of the left column’s inner content area (i.e., from left padding to right padding), forming a horizontal band of about 56–72px in height for a short list of errors, expanding vertically as needed. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space above and below this band so it feels distinct but integrated with the form. The summary is visually differentiated with a subtle tinted background and a thin border, but it remains aligned to the column’s grid so the eye still follows a clean vertical path: section heading → intro text → error summary → first field. On narrow screens, it continues to sit at the top of the now full-width form, maintaining full-width presentation and similar spacing from the edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The error summary is a static block element inside the form; it appears and disappears in-place without overlaying other content. When it appears, it pushes subsequent form fields downward, preserving all existing column alignment. It does not stick to the viewport or follow the user; its visibility is tied directly to the presence of validation errors and to the scroll position of the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The return-home-link sits at the bottom-left of the primary content area, positioned within the left column beneath the form or confirmation message. After the main form’s action area (primary and any secondary buttons), there is a clear breathing space of about 24–32px, and then the return-home-link appears as a low-emphasis text link (possibly accompanied by a left-pointing arrow icon). It is left-aligned with the form’s input fields and buttons, respecting the same inner padding from the left column edge. The link does not extend into the right column; it occupies a single line with a small top margin and sits near the bottom of the visible content in that column. On tablet and mobile views where the layout is single-column, the return-home-link remains immediately below the form or confirmation content, still left-aligned within the central content width, and may appear closer to the center of the viewport because the container itself is narrower.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The link is static and scrolls with the rest of the content. It is intentionally separated from the main form actions with extra spacing so it reads as a subtle, secondary navigation option rather than a competing primary action. There is no sticky or floating behavior; its position relative to the bottom of the form remains consistent across states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state initializer has no direct visual footprint, but it influences the initial state of elements within the left column, particularly the newsletter-signup-form. Before the form renders fully, this component reads any relevant URL parameters (for example, preselecting certain interests or applying a campaign code) and ensures the form opens in the correct configuration. From a layout perspective, its impact may be seen in sections of the form being pre-expanded or specific options already highlighted, but it does not introduce additional elements or spacing of its own. Any brief delay it causes in preparing state is masked by the newsletter-signup-options-loader’s inline loading presentation, keeping the visible layout smooth and consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The initializer operates behind the scenes and does not occupy space or alter the grid directly. It runs before or alongside the loader state, after which the form appears fully configured in its normal position within the left column. There is no fixed, floating, or sticky behavior associated with this component.

name: Getting Here & Parking
title: Getting Here & Parking
description: Page for visitors to view driving directions, public transit options, parking details, and other transport information, with filterable transit routes and route-specific details.

core_features: Display structured information for Driving, Public Transit, Parking, and Other Transport using content from getGettingHereContent.
Provide tabbed navigation so users can switch between Driving, Public Transit, Parking, and Other Transport sections; the active tab is controlled by the URL parameter `tab` when present.

Within the Public Transit tab, provide a mode filter (e.g., bus, train) that calls searchTransitRoutes with the selected mode and only displays routes for that mode (e.g., only bus routes when `mode=bus`).
Use searchTransitRoutes to list matching TransitRoute records, including walking time and service hours, with each route name clickable to open a details panel.
Use getTransitRouteDetails to populate a transit route details panel with additional information for the selected route.

Within the transit route details panel, provide links that navigate to contact.html with query parameters for transportation and parking questions, including `topic=transportation_parking` and `context` based on the selected route name.
Expose additional links from other sections (e.g., Parking, Driving) to contact.html for general transportation and parking questions, pre-selecting the `transportation_parking` topic.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View driving directions and parking information
1. User opens getting_here.html directly or via navigation.
2. Page reads the `tab` URL parameter; if it is absent or not set to `public_transit` or `parking`, the Driving tab is used as the default active tab.
3. Page calls getGettingHereContent once and stores the returned drivingInfo, parkingInfo, publicTransitInfo, and otherTransportInfo in page state.
4. With the Driving tab active, the drivingInfo content is rendered in the Driving section.
5. User can switch to the Parking tab via the tab navigation; the URL is updated to include `tab=parking`.
6. The Parking tab renders parkingInfo from getGettingHereContent and shows a contextual link to contact.html for additional transportation and parking questions (with `topic=transportation_parking`).

Workflow: Discover public transit options and filter to bus routes
1. User navigates to getting_here.html, possibly via a link that includes `tab=public_transit` or both `tab=public_transit&mode=bus` from index.html or faq.html.
2. Page initializes and reads URL parameters:
   - Sets the active tab to `public_transit` if `tab=public_transit`.
   - Sets the initial transit mode filter based on the `mode` parameter if present.
3. Page calls getGettingHereContent and displays publicTransitInfo as overview text in the Public Transit section.
4. Public Transit mode filter initializes to the `mode` query value (if provided) or to a neutral state (no mode filter). The component calls searchTransitRoutes with:
   - mode equal to the selected mode (or undefined/null if no mode filter),
   - maxWalkingTimeMinutes, minServiceEndTime, and dayOfWeek unset unless the user adjusts advanced filters.
5. The list of TransitRoute results returned by searchTransitRoutes is rendered (route name, walking time, service hours, and any other available fields).
6. If user selects the bus mode filter, the page updates the URL to `tab=public_transit&mode=bus` and re-calls searchTransitRoutes with `mode='bus'`. Only bus routes from the response are displayed, satisfying the requirement to show only bus routes when the bus filter is selected.

Workflow: View detailed information for a specific transit route and ask a question
1. With the Public Transit tab active and a mode filter set (or not), user sees the list of TransitRoute results.
2. User clicks on a route name in the list.
3. The page stores the selected route ID in component state and calls getTransitRouteDetails with that transitRouteId.
4. The route details panel opens, rendering the returned transitRoute object (including walkingTimeMinutes, serviceStartTime, serviceEndTime, daysOfWeek, description, detailsUrl, and notes if available).
5. Within the details panel, user can click an "Ask about this route" action.
6. This click triggers navigation to contact.html with URL parameters:
   - topic=transportation_parking
   - context equal to the selected route's name (from transitRoute.name).
7. On contact.html, the form can pre-select the transportation and parking topic and display or record the route name as context.

Workflow: General transportation question from any section
1. User is viewing information in any tab (Driving, Public Transit, Parking, or Other Transport).
2. User clicks a general link such as "More transportation questions?" displayed within the section.
3. The page navigates to contact.html with `topic=transportation_parking` and may omit the `context` parameter (or use a generic context string if the implementation supports it).
4. User completes the contact form on contact.html with their transportation or parking question.
interactions: Tab selection: Clicking a tab label triggers a change of the active section (Driving, Public Transit, Parking, Other Transport) and updates the `tab` URL parameter to the corresponding value. The interaction does not re-fetch getGettingHereContent; it reuses already loaded content.

Initial load: On page initialization, the component reads the `tab` and `mode` URL parameters and sets the active tab and initial transit mode filter accordingly, then calls getGettingHereContent and, if applicable, searchTransitRoutes.

Transit mode filter change: When the user selects a transit mode (e.g., bus, train) in the Public Transit section, the component updates the `mode` URL parameter, calls searchTransitRoutes with the new mode and any additional filter values, and re-renders the TransitRoute list. When the mode is set to `bus`, only bus routes from the response are shown.

Optional advanced transit filter changes: When the user adjusts optional filters (like maximum walking time, minimum service end time, or day of week), the component calls searchTransitRoutes with these parameters and re-renders the TransitRoute list.
Route selection: Clicking on a transit route list item triggers fetching additional details via getTransitRouteDetails and opens or focuses the route details panel.
Route details close: Clicking a close or back action in the route details panel hides the panel and returns focus to the route list without altering URL parameters.

Ask about this route: Within the route details panel, clicking the route-specific contact action navigates the user to contact.html with `topic=transportation_parking` and `context` set to the selected route's name.

General transportation questions: Clicking a general transportation contact link in any section navigates to contact.html with `topic=transportation_parking` set, allowing the contact page to pre-select the relevant topic.

Internal deep links: Links from other pages (index.html, faq.html, footer) may include `tab` and/or `mode` parameters; the page respects these on load to bring users directly to the relevant tab and pre-filtered Public Transit view.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is controlled via URL query parameters and the results of the assigned interfaces. On load, the page reads the `tab` and `mode` parameters from the query string. If `tab` is one of `driving`, `public_transit`, or `parking`, that section becomes the active tab; otherwise a default tab is applied. If `mode` is provided and the active tab is `public_transit`, the mode filter in the Public Transit section is initialized to this value. The page then calls getGettingHereContent once and caches the returned content for all tabs.

When the active tab becomes `public_transit` (either on first load or via user click), the page calls searchTransitRoutes using:
- mode: the current mode filter (possibly derived from the `mode` URL parameter).
- maxWalkingTimeMinutes, minServiceEndTime, dayOfWeek: values optionally set by user input; if the user has not set these, they are omitted or passed as null/undefined to searchTransitRoutes so that the backend applies defaults.
The returned array of TransitRoute objects becomes the state for the route list.

User interactions update URL parameters rather than localStorage. Changing tabs updates the `tab` parameter. Changing transit mode updates the `mode` parameter (and keeps `tab=public_transit` in the URL). The page reacts to these changes immediately by updating visible sections and re-calling searchTransitRoutes as necessary. The state of which route is currently selected for details is managed in component state and does not need to appear in the URL unless a deeper linking pattern is later defined.

Navigation to contact.html is performed by building URLs that include `topic=transportation_parking` and, for route-specific actions, `context` derived from the selected TransitRoute.name. This allows the contact page to understand the context of the user's question without storing any local state on the Getting Here & Parking page.

components.functionality: Controls which main section (Driving, Public Transit, Parking, Other Transport) is visible and synchronizes the active tab with the `tab` URL parameter.
data_binding: getGettingHereContent (for labels and availability if needed)
event_handlers: onInit: Read `tab` from URL parameters, defaulting to a defined tab if not provided.

onTabChange: Update internal activeTab state, push updated `tab` value into the URL query string without a full page reload, and trigger dependent behaviors (e.g., load transit routes when switching to Public Transit).

components.functionality: Displays driving directions and related information when the Driving tab is active.
event_handlers: onInit: Wait for getGettingHereContent to resolve and bind drivingInfo to the section.
onTabChange: Render or hide this panel based on activeTab === 'driving'.

components.functionality: Displays parking information (locations, guidelines, etc.) when the Parking tab is active, and provides a general link to the Contact page for additional parking questions.
event_handlers: onInit: Wait for getGettingHereContent to resolve and bind parkingInfo to the section.
onTabChange: Render or hide this panel based on activeTab === 'parking'.
onMoreParkingQuestionsClick: Navigate to contact.html with query parameter topic=transportation_parking.

components.functionality: Shows general information about using public transit to reach the attraction at the top of the Public Transit tab.
event_handlers: onInit: Bind publicTransitInfo once getGettingHereContent returns.
onTabChange: Render or hide this panel based on activeTab === 'public_transit'.

components.functionality: Displays information about other transportation modes (e.g., rideshare, cycling) and provides a general link to contact.html for further questions.
event_handlers: onTabChange: Render or hide this panel based on activeTab value for the Other Transport tab.
onMoreTransportQuestionsClick: Navigate to contact.html with query parameter topic=transportation_parking.

components.functionality: Allows users to filter public transit routes by transport mode (e.g., bus, train). Updates the `mode` URL parameter and triggers route search. Ensures that only routes for the selected mode are shown (for example, only bus routes when the bus mode is selected).
event_handlers: onInit: If activeTab === 'public_transit', read `mode` from URL and set the initial mode filter state; then call searchTransitRoutes with that mode.

onModeChange: Update internal mode state, write the selected mode to the `mode` URL parameter (ensuring `tab=public_transit` remains set), and call searchTransitRoutes with the new mode and any advanced filter values.

components.functionality: Optional controls for refining the list of public transit routes by maximum walking time, minimum service end time, and day of week. These values are passed into searchTransitRoutes when set.
event_handlers: onFilterChange: When user adjusts any advanced filter, call searchTransitRoutes with the currently selected mode (from public-transit-mode-filter) plus updated maxWalkingTimeMinutes, minServiceEndTime, and/or dayOfWeek; re-bind the returned routes to the route list.

components.functionality: Displays the list of TransitRoute results returned by searchTransitRoutes, including route name, walking time, and service hours. Each route name is clickable to open a details panel for that route.
event_handlers: onRoutesLoaded: Render route items based on the latest searchTransitRoutes result.

onRouteClick: Capture the clicked route's id, store it as the selected route in shared state, and trigger getTransitRouteDetails with transitRouteId equal to the selected id; then open the route details panel.

components.functionality: Shows detailed information for a single selected transit route, using getTransitRouteDetails. Provides actions to contact the attraction about this specific route or about transportation questions in general.
event_handlers: onRouteSelected: When a route is selected from the route list, call getTransitRouteDetails with transitRouteId and bind the returned transitRoute object to the panel fields.
onClose: Hide the details panel and clear the selected route state.
onAskAboutThisRouteClick: Navigate to contact.html with query parameters topic=transportation_parking and context set to getTransitRouteDetails.transitRoute.name.
onMoreTransportationQuestionsClick: Navigate to contact.html with query parameter topic=transportation_parking and, optionally, context set based on current tab or left unset.

components.functionality: Provides context-aware links to the Contact page from multiple sections (Driving, Public Transit, Parking, Other Transport) for follow-up questions about transportation and parking.
event_handlers: onDrivingContactClick: Navigate to contact.html with topic=transportation_parking and optionally context indicating that the question relates to driving.
onParkingContactClick: Navigate to contact.html with topic=transportation_parking and optionally context indicating that the question relates to parking.
onPublicTransitContactClick: Navigate to contact.html with topic=transportation_parking and optionally context indicating that the question relates to public transit in general.
onOtherTransportContactClick: Navigate to contact.html with topic=transportation_parking and optionally context indicating that the question relates to other transport modes.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and wiring between components for the Getting Here & Parking page.
event_handlers:
onInit: 
- Parse URL parameters `tab` and `mode`.
- Set activeTab in getting-here-tab-navigation.
- Call getGettingHereContent and pass results to the content-section components.
- If activeTab === 'public_transit', call searchTransitRoutes with mode from URL (if any) and default or unset values for other parameters, then pass results to public-transit-route-list.

onUrlChange: When the URL query string changes within the page (e.g., by tab or mode selection), re-parse `tab` and `mode` and update child components' state accordingly; call searchTransitRoutes again if the Public Transit tab remains active and the mode has changed.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width, fixed-height Christmas-themed hero at the very top (with global navigation overlaid) and a centered white content area beneath it. Inside the white area, content is organized in a linear top-to-bottom flow. The 'Getting Here & Parking' content starts with a tabbed navigation just below the hero, then conditionally reveals one primary section at a time (Driving, Parking, Public Transit, Other Transport). Within the Public Transit tab, the layout switches to a clear two-column structure: filters and route list on the left, route details on the right, echoing the mockup’s text + image split.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s structure: a full-width, fixed-height Christmas-themed hero at the very top (with global navigation overlaid) and a centered white content area beneath it. Inside the white area, content is organized in a linear top-to-bottom flow. The 'Getting Here & Parking' content starts with a tabbed navigation just below the hero, then conditionally reveals one primary section at a time (Driving, Parking, Public Transit, Other Transport). Within the Public Transit tab, the layout switches to a clear two-column structure: filters and route list on the left, route details on the right, echoing the mockup’s text + image split.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height with generous vertical padding around the page title. Below, the main content is constrained to a centered container of about 1100–1200px width with substantial left/right margins. Text-oriented panels (Driving, Parking, Other Transport, Transit Overview) use a single, left-aligned column that occupies roughly 60–65% of the container width, leaving airy whitespace on the right. For the transit interaction area, the left column (filters + route list) takes about 35–40% of the container width, while the right column (route details) takes the remaining 60–65%, both aligned to the same top edge. Vertical spacing between major sections is 48–64px, and internal spacing within sections follows 16–32px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low to medium, optimized for clarity and easy reading. Paragraph widths are kept to roughly 55–65 characters per line. Within each panel, headings, subheadings, labels, and body text are separated by 8–16px of vertical space, while larger blocks (e.g., between tab navigation and panel content, or between filter groups) use 24–32px spacing. The transit area, even though more information-dense, still preserves breathing room via clear grouping and sufficient padding, avoiding visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s mild Z-pattern. The eye first hits the top-left logo and scans right across the global navigation in the hero. The large centered ‘Getting Here & Parking’ hero title then pulls focus into the middle of the screen. As the user moves downward into the white content area, attention is drawn to the left-aligned tab navigation, then to the currently active section heading (e.g., Driving Directions) directly beneath it. In the Public Transit tab, the eye starts at the left with the transit overview text and mode filters, then naturally moves down through the route list while frequently referencing the more detailed information in the right-hand column, creating a left-to-right rhythm similar to the mockup’s text + image relationship.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a dramatic full-width red bokeh Christmas hero at the top, with the site logo and navigation sitting lightly across the top edge. Centered within this hero is a large, elegant ‘Getting Here & Parking’ title with generous vertical padding so the text feels anchored and prominent against the festive backdrop. As the user scrolls down, the background shifts to a clean white, and a centered content container appears, framed by comfortable side margins. At the top of this white area is a horizontal tab navigation bar that lets visitors switch between Driving, Parking, Public Transit, and Other Transport. Below the tabs, each section is revealed in turn. Driving and Parking panels use a left-aligned editorial column of text occupying about two-thirds of the content width, leaving subtle whitespace to the right to maintain a calm, article-like feel. When the Public Transit tab is active, the layout shifts into a structured two-column arrangement: on the left, concise overview copy, mode filter chips, advanced filter controls, and a vertically stacked route list; on the right, a larger details panel that presents the selected route’s schedule and instructions, visually echoing the mockup’s tall image column. The Other Transport section then returns to the single-column editorial pattern near the bottom. Finally, a compact transport contact link group closes the page, centered and clearly separated from the content above. Throughout, the spacing is spacious and consistent, and the eye is gently led from hero to tabs, then down into the active content, with the transit area providing the main interactive focal point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab navigation is positioned at the very top of the white content area, directly below the hero section. It is centered horizontally within the main content container, spanning roughly 80–90% of the container width while keeping at least one base unit (8px) of padding from the container edges. The tabs themselves are laid out in a single horizontal row, left-aligned within this band, with equal spacing between them to preserve a clean editorial feel. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48px between the bottom of the hero and the top of the tabs, and about 24px between the tabs and whichever content panel is currently active. The entire tab strip has vertical padding of 12–16px to give it a defined yet unobtrusive presence, without feeling like a bulky toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow, anchoring all subsequent panels. On taller screens, it remains near the top of the content area and scrolls away naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Driving info panel appears immediately below the tab navigation when the Driving tab is selected. It is contained within the main centered content area, using a single, left-aligned text column that occupies approximately 60–65% of the container width, starting from the left margin of the container. The right 35–40% of the container remains mostly open whitespace, echoing the mockup’s broader text column with a lighter secondary column. The panel starts with a section heading (e.g., ‘Driving Directions’), followed by a small gap of about 8–12px to any subheading or label (such as ‘From the North’, ‘From Downtown’), and 16px gaps between paragraphs or grouped instruction blocks. Top and bottom padding inside the panel are about 32–40px, clearly separating it from the tabs above and subsequent sections below. This produces an editorial, easy-to-scan block of content with plenty of breathing room around the text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal vertical flow directly beneath the tabs; completely hidden or replaced when other tabs (Parking, Public Transit, Other Transport) are active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Parking info panel occupies the same spatial slot and width as the Driving panel, appearing directly beneath the tab navigation when the Parking tab is selected. It uses the identical left-aligned editorial column taking up about 60–65% of the container width, leaving the right side open for whitespace and maintaining the visual rhythm across sections. Within the panel, a clear section heading (e.g., ‘Parking Information’) sits at the top, followed by a short 8–12px gap to any brief intro line, and then 16–24px spacing between lines about parking lots, pricing, time limits, or restrictions. Lists of parking options can be presented as vertically stacked items with 8–12px gaps, ensuring the panel feels structured but uncrowded. Vertical padding at the top and bottom mirrors the Driving section (around 32–40px), ensuring the two panels feel interchangeable in the same spatial container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same position in the content flow as the Driving panel; it replaces the Driving panel when the Parking tab is selected and is otherwise hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the Public Transit tab is active, the public transit overview panel is the first block directly under the tab navigation, acting as an introduction to the more complex transit area. It is positioned at the top of the main container, with about 24px of space below the tabs and about 24–32px of space before the filters begin. The content is left-aligned and limited to a text column occupying around 60% of the container width, matching the editorial layout of the Driving and Parking panels, while the remaining 40% on the right side remains clean whitespace.
