This panel typically includes a concise heading (e.g., ‘Public Transit Options’) and one or two brief paragraphs describing the available transit types and how to use the filters. The internal spacing between heading and body text is around 8–16px, maintaining a light, readable cadence above the denser, more interactive filter and list area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the top of the Public Transit tab’s content, in normal flow, and scrolls upward as the user moves down into the filters and route list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The transit mode filter sits directly below the public transit overview panel and serves as the topmost element in the left column of the two-column transit area. Horizontally, it is aligned to the left edge of the main content container and spans approximately 35–40% of the container width, defining the left column. It has a distinct horizontal row of mode options (e.g., Bus, Train, Shuttle) styled as pill or tab-like buttons, arranged from left to right with consistent 8–12px gaps. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the overview text above and the filter row, and another 16px between this filter and the advanced filter controls below. The filter group itself has 12–16px of internal padding, so it feels like a cohesive control band without overwhelming the surrounding whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchors the top of the left transit column. It scrolls with the main content but always remains above the route list within its column as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advanced filters sit immediately below the mode filter within the same left column, sharing its width (about 35–40% of the container) and left alignment. They are arranged as a vertical stack of controls—such as dropdowns for time of day or accessibility options—grouped within a lightly framed or subtly shaded block to distinguish them from the simple mode filters above. There is a vertical gap of around 16px between the mode filter and this block, and a slightly larger 24px gap between the bottom of this block and the start of the route list. Inside the advanced filters block, individual controls are spaced 8–16px apart, keeping the density moderate and visually organized. The block’s padding (about 16–24px) ensures the controls do not press against the edges, maintaining the clean, airy feel of the overall layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the left column’s vertical flow; it scrolls naturally with the page as users move between filters and the route list below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route list occupies the remainder of the left column directly below the advanced filters. It aligns with the same left edge and shares the same width (35–40% of the container). Each route appears as a horizontally full-width card or row within this column, with 8–12px vertical spacing between routes to keep the list legible. There is a 24px gap between the advanced filters above and the first route item, creating a clear separation between filter controls and results. The entire list extends vertically and can be taller than the viewport; as the user scrolls, the list continues downward while the route details panel on the right remains visually adjacent. On taller screens, this creates the sense of a browsing column on the left feeding details to the right column. Top alignment of the first list item matches the top of the route details panel for a balanced two-column look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the main page flow. On longer lists, the left column can extend significantly below the initial viewport, while the right column’s details panel may remain partially or fully in view depending on scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route details panel forms the right column of the transit area and visually mirrors the tall image column in the mockup. It starts at the same vertical level as the top of the route list and mode filter (just below the transit overview panel) but on the right side of the container. It spans approximately 60–65% of the container width, with its left edge aligned to the inner edge of the right column (leaving a comfortable 24–32px gutter between the left and right columns). Inside, there is a generous 24–32px padding on all sides to frame route-specific information—such as route name, timings, stops, and step-by-step instructions—in a structured, card-like block. Headings, subheadings, and text within are arranged vertically with 8–16px spacing, matching the editorial style of the text sections. Overall, it appears as a prominent, vertically oriented panel that feels substantial but not cramped, acting as the main focal area when exploring transit options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a fixed position in the right column within the document flow; on larger screens it can be implemented as sticky within its column so that it remains in view as the user scrolls through the route list on the left, while on smaller screens it stacks below the route list and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Other Transport info panel appears below the entire Public Transit area when the Other Transport tab is selected, occupying the same general region as the other main panels (Driving, Parking, Transit). In the white content area, it returns to the single-column editorial layout: a left-aligned text column that spans about 60–65% of the container width, with the right side left mostly open for whitespace. There is a clear vertical separation of 48–64px between the transit section (or whichever panel precedes it in the tab context) and this panel, reinforcing that it is a distinct topic. Inside the panel, a heading (e.g., ‘Other Ways to Get Here’) sits at the top with 8–12px spacing before any brief intro copy, followed by 16–24px gaps between descriptions of options like rideshares, bicycles, or walking routes. The top and bottom padding of roughly 32–40px ensure this section feels equally substantial and easy to read as the other informational panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the other panels when its tab is active and scrolls in the standard vertical flow of the page, with no special fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The transport contact links block is positioned near the bottom of the main content area, after the currently active panel’s content (e.g., at the end of the Driving panel, or as a common footer block when appropriate). It is centered within the main content container, with a width of about 60–70% to keep it visually compact. The block may begin with a small, subtle label such as ‘Need Help?’ followed by horizontally arranged contact options (e.g., ‘Email Us’, ‘Call for Directions’, ‘Transit Website’) with 16–24px spacing between each link. On wider screens, links sit on a single row; on narrower screens, they wrap into two rows while staying center-aligned. There is a healthy 32–48px of vertical space between this block and the content above it, and 48–64px of space below it before the site footer, clearly marking it as the page’s closing interaction element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears at the end of the main content in normal document flow, serving as a lightweight call-to-action area that scrolls off-screen at the bottom like other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page initializer is not visually represented on the page. It functions behind the scenes to set the default active tab (e.g., Driving), initialize filter states for the Public Transit section, and manage interactions such as updating the route details panel when a route is selected from the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; it has no spatial footprint in the layout and does not affect the physical arrangement of on-screen components.

name: Contact Us
title: Contact Us
description: Page where visitors can select a topic, enter their contact details, submit a question or request, and see confirmation with response information, with optional pre-filled context from other pages.

core_features: Load the list of contact topics dynamically using the getContactTopics interface and populate the Subject/Topic dropdown.
Read optional URL parameters for topic and context on page load to pre-select a contact topic and pre-fill the message field.
Provide a contact form capturing topic, name, email, and message, with client-side validation for required fields and email format.
Include an internal relatedContext value derived from the optional context URL parameter to be sent with the message via submitContactMessage.
Submit the visitor’s inquiry using submitContactMessage, handling loading state, success, and error responses.

Display a confirmation area showing the result returned by submitContactMessage, including any response text and reference ID, and present an option to navigate back to the home page after successful submission.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: General contact from navigation
1. Visitor selects the 'Contact Us' link in the header or footer navigation, opening contact.html with no URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page-init logic calls getContactTopics to retrieve the dynamic list of topics.
3. Once topics are loaded, the Subject/Topic dropdown is enabled and displays the options returned by the interface. No topic is pre-selected unless the implementation chooses a default from the returned list.
4. The visitor fills in the form: selects a topic, enters their name, email, and message.
5. On clicking the form submit button, client-side validation runs. If any required field is missing or the email format is invalid, inline validation errors are shown and submission is blocked.
6. If validation passes, the form enters a 'submitting' state and calls submitContactMessage with the selected topic, name, email, message, and a relatedContext value (empty in this workflow because there is no context URL parameter).
7. When the interface responds with success, the form is locked to prevent duplicate submissions, the confirmation panel displays the success message and reference ID from the response, and a 'Back to Home' navigation option becomes available.
8. If the interface responds with an error or the request fails, the page shows an error state in the status panel, keeps the user’s form input intact, and allows the visitor to retry submission.

Workflow 2: Topic pre-selected from another page
1. Visitor is on a contextual page such as getting_here.html or tickets.html and clicks a contextual link to contact.html that includes a topic URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?topic=some_topic_id).
2. On page load, the page-init logic reads the topic URL parameter and stores it in temporary state.
3. The page calls getContactTopics and waits for the topic list.
4. After topics load, the logic searches for a topic whose ID matches the topic URL parameter. If a match is found, that topic is pre-selected in the Subject/Topic dropdown. If there is no match, the dropdown is left unselected and the visitor must choose manually.
5. The visitor reviews the pre-selected topic, adjusts it if necessary, and enters their name, email, and message.
6. On submit, validation runs and, if valid, submitContactMessage is called with the currently selected topic and the other form values. The relatedContext parameter in this workflow is still empty unless a context URL parameter is also present.
7. The visitor sees success or error feedback in the status/confirmation panel and can use the 'Back to Home' navigation after success.

Workflow 3: Contextual contact with pre-filled message
1. Visitor is on a page such as getting_here.html or accessibility.html and clicks a link to contact.html that includes both topic and context URL parameters (e.g., contact.html?topic=some_topic_id&context=some_description).
2. On page load, the page-init logic reads both URL parameters. The context value is stored for use both as an initial message prefill and as relatedContext.
3. The page calls getContactTopics and, once the topics are loaded, pre-selects the topic that matches the topic URL parameter, if any.
4. The message textarea is pre-populated with the context text in a way that the visitor can see and edit it (for example, appended at the start or end of the message area). The visitor can modify or extend this text as desired.
5. Internally, the relatedContext value remains the original context URL parameter, independent of any edits the visitor makes to the main message field. If there is no context parameter, relatedContext is empty.
6. The visitor reviews the pre-selected topic and pre-filled message, enters name and email, adjusts any content, and submits the form.
7. On submit, validation runs. If valid, submitContactMessage is called with topic, name, email, the final message text from the form, and relatedContext taken from the original context URL parameter.
8. On success, the confirmation panel displays the response from submitContactMessage, including any timing/response information contained in the response message field, and a 'Back to Home' link that navigates to index.html. On failure, an error is shown and the visitor can retry without losing their input.

Workflow 4: Accessing Contact Us from ticket or group-related pages with no context
1. Visitor is on tickets.html or groups.html and clicks a generic 'Contact Us' link pointing to contact.html without additional parameters.
2. The Contact Us page loads, calls getContactTopics, and presents the full topic list.
3. The visitor selects the most appropriate topic, fills in the form, and submits.
4. The remaining steps follow Workflow 1 (validation, submitContactMessage call, feedback, and optional navigation back to home).
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: contact.html is opened.
- Behavior: Read topic and context URL parameters if present. Call getContactTopics. While topics are loading, disable or show a loading state for the Subject/Topic dropdown. When topics are loaded, populate the dropdown and pre-select a topic if the topic URL parameter matches one of the returned topic IDs.

Interaction: Topic selection change
- Trigger: User selects a different option in the Subject/Topic dropdown.
- Behavior: Update the in-memory ContactMessage draft object's topic field to match the selected topic ID. Clear any validation error associated with the topic field. This change does not alter the URL parameters.

Interaction: Name, email, and message input
- Trigger: User types in the name, email, or message fields.
- Behavior: Update the corresponding fields in the ContactMessage draft object. On blur for each field, run field-level validity checks (e.g., required, email format) and show or clear validation messages accordingly.

Interaction: Form submission
- Trigger: User clicks the main submit button on the Contact Us form.
- Behavior: Run full-form validation. If any required field is empty or invalid, prevent submission and display validation messages next to the fields and/or in a summary list. If validation passes, set submission state to 'submitting', disable the form controls or the submit button to prevent duplicate submissions, and call submitContactMessage with the current draft values for topic, name, email, message, and relatedContext (derived from context URL parameter if available, otherwise empty).

Interaction: Handling submitContactMessage success
- Trigger: submitContactMessage returns a successful response.
- Behavior: Set submission state to 'success'. Store the returned messageId and message in state. Display a confirmation panel that surfaces the returned message string and includes the messageId as the reference. Keep the original form values visible or optionally collapse them, but prevent resubmission unless explicitly allowed by the design. Reveal a 'Back to Home' control that navigates to index.html when clicked.

Interaction: Handling submitContactMessage error
- Trigger: submitContactMessage returns a non-success response or fails.
- Behavior: Set submission state to 'error'. Display an error notice in the status panel describing that the message could not be sent, optionally augmented with information from the error response if available. Keep the user’s form input intact so they can correct and re-submit. Re-enable the submit button.

Interaction: Back to Home navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Back to Home' control shown after successful form submission.
- Behavior: Navigate the browser to index.html with no additional state required. The existing Contact Us page state is discarded upon navigation.

Interaction: Reloading the page with pre-filled parameters
- Trigger: User refreshes the page while on a pre-filled Contact Us URL such as contact.html?topic=...&context=....
- Behavior: The page re-runs the same initialization logic: reads topic and context from the URL, calls getContactTopics, re-applies the pre-selection and message pre-fill, and reconstructs the ContactMessage draft object accordingly. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact Us page relies on URL parameters and in-memory state rather than localStorage. On every page load, the page reads the topic and context URL parameters from contact.html, if present. The topic parameter is stored in a temporary initialization variable and used only after getContactTopics completes: the logic searches the returned list for a topic whose id matches this parameter and, if found, pre-selects it in the dropdown. If the parameter does not match any returned topic, the dropdown remains unselected and the visitor must choose a topic. The context parameter is copied into two places at initialization time: (1) the initial value of the message textarea, so the visitor can see and edit it; and (2) a separate relatedContext field in the in-memory ContactMessage draft object that preserves the original context string for submission, regardless of edits to the visible message. Both topic and context URL parameters are read-only from the perspective of the Contact Us page; user interactions do not modify the URL. The page maintains transient in-memory state for: (a) the list of topics loaded from getContactTopics; (b) a ContactMessage draft object reflecting current topic, name, email, message, and relatedContext values; (c) field-level validation states and error messages; and (d) a submission state flag (idle, submitting, success, error), plus the last submitContactMessage response (including success flag, messageId, and message). On navigation away from contact.html or reload without the same URL parameters, this state is reset. All navigation, including returning to index.html, is done via standard URL-based navigation without storing additional state in localStorage or other persistent client-side storage.

components.functionality: On page load, reads the topic and context URL parameters, calls getContactTopics to retrieve the list of contact topics, and initializes the ContactMessage draft object and the topic dropdown’s selected value based on these inputs.

Displays the list of topics returned by getContactTopics as selectable options, applies pre-selection based on the topic URL parameter if it matches a returned topic ID, and updates the ContactMessage draft object’s topic field when the visitor changes the selection.

Hosts the input fields for name, email, and message, together with the topic-dropdown component and the submit button. Maintains the in-memory ContactMessage draft object, validates required fields and email format, and triggers submission via submitContactMessage when the form is submitted successfully.

Displays and edits the ContactMessage draft object’s message field. On initialization, pre-fills the textarea with the context URL parameter if present, allowing the visitor to edit or extend it. Updates the ContactMessage.message field as the visitor types.
Stores the original context URL parameter value separately from the visible message text and ensures that this value is included in the relatedContext argument when calling submitContactMessage.

Aggregates and displays form validation errors derived from the ContactMessage draft object (e.g., missing topic, name, email, or message) so that visitors can see which fields need attention before a successful submit.

Reflects the current submission state of the form (idle, submitting, success, error). Shows a loading state while submitContactMessage is in progress, displays the response message and messageId from submitContactMessage on success, and shows an error notice if submission fails.

Triggers full form validation and, if validation passes, calls submitContactMessage with the topic, name, email, message, and relatedContext from the ContactMessage draft object. It is disabled while submission is in progress to prevent duplicate submissions.

Appears only when the submission-status-panel is in a success state and the last submitContactMessage call returned success. When clicked, navigates the visitor to index.html as specified by the outgoing navigation requirements.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the hero + content pattern from the mockup. A full-width, fixed-height Christmas-themed hero with red bokeh background sits at the top with the global navigation overlaid. Centered within the hero, a large 'Contact Us' title introduces the page. Below the hero, the page transitions into a white content section constrained to a centered max-width container (~1120px). Inside this container, a two-column layout is used: the left column (around 60–65% width) holds the entire contact flow (status messages, topic dropdown, form fields, submit button, back link), while the right column (around 35–40% width) is visually lighter and reserved for decorative seasonal imagery or brief informational copy (not part of the component list). On narrow screens, the layout collapses to a single column with all components stacked vertically and centered.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the hero + content pattern from the mockup. A full-width, fixed-height Christmas-themed hero with red bokeh background sits at the top with the global navigation overlaid. Centered within the hero, a large 'Contact Us' title introduces the page. Below the hero, the page transitions into a white content section constrained to a centered max-width container (~1120px). Inside this container, a two-column layout is used: the left column (around 60–65% width) holds the entire contact flow (status messages, topic dropdown, form fields, submit button, back link), while the right column (around 35–40% width) is visually lighter and reserved for decorative seasonal imagery or brief informational copy (not part of the component list). On narrow screens, the layout collapses to a single column with all components stacked vertically and centered.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero takes roughly the top 35–40% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding around the 'Contact Us' title. The white content section starts with a clear vertical gap (about 64px) below the hero. Inside the content section, there is a comfortable top padding (~48px) before the form begins. Components within the left column follow a consistent vertical rhythm: tight but readable spacing between label + input (8–16px), 16–24px between grouped elements (e.g., between topic dropdown and next field), and a larger 32–40px gap before major transitions (e.g., from the form body to the back-to-home link). Horizontal padding inside the container is about 24–32px on each side, with ample whitespace framing the content. Both status panels occupy full width of the left column but maintain generous inner padding to feel like distinct, breathable blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout prioritizes reading and clarity over packing in elements. Each field and panel has room to breathe: the text column is kept within an optimal reading width, and inputs are full-width within that column. Paragraph-style explanations (within panels or hints adjacent to fields) use a comfortable line length and slightly increased line-height. Error and success panels are compact but not cramped, with clear separation from the form fields. Overall density is low to medium: enough information is visible to understand the form and its status, but wide margins, consistent spacing, and the two-column structure prevent visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the logo and navigation at the top, then drops to the large centered 'Contact Us' heading in the hero. As the user scrolls into the white content section, attention lands on the left column, where the form heading and any active status panel sit at the top of the column. From there, the eye moves downward through the validation-error list or submission-status panel (if present), then to the topic dropdown, then through the remaining form fields down to the message textarea and finally the submit button. The right column acts as a secondary visual anchor, creating a mild Z-pattern echo: nav → hero title → down-left form area → across to the lighter decorative right column. After submission, the submission-status-panel becomes a strong focal point at the top of the left column, before the eye moves back down to the rest of the form or the back-to-home link.

layout.overall_description: The Contact Us page opens with a full-width, red bokeh Christmas hero with the global navigation overlaid along the very top edge. Centered in this hero, a large, editorial-style 'Contact Us' title sits with generous vertical padding, surrounded by festive but soft-focus background sparkles. As the user scrolls slightly, the hero gives way to a clean white content section framed within a centered, max-width container. This section is split into two columns: on the left, a broad column contains the entire interactive contact experience – a compact heading, a stacked status area for errors or submission messages, and a vertically flowing form with a topic dropdown, contact fields, a large message textarea, and a left-aligned primary submit button, all spaced with a comfortable, editorial rhythm. On the right, a narrower column provides visual balance with seasonal imagery or light supporting copy, leaving plenty of whitespace so the form remains dominant. At the very bottom of the content column, a subtle 'Back to Home' link sits left-aligned, giving a gentle exit path without challenging the prominence of the form. The overall effect mirrors the mockup’s seasonal editorial feel: a dramatic festive hero leading into a quiet, well-structured, reading-friendly form area with ample breathing room.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-init-controller is a non-visual, logical page-level component. It sits conceptually at the very top of the main content layer, just below the hero section and above the visible form layout, initializing any pre-filled context or query parameters. It occupies no visual space and does not affect the spacing or alignment of other elements; the white content section and its two-column layout begin immediately as if this controller were not present. On all screen sizes, it remains hidden and does not alter the visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered controller; no box model footprint, static in the page logic hierarchy but invisible in the visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-dropdown is positioned near the top of the left content column, inside the contact form. Within the white content section, it appears after any form heading and status panels, sitting roughly 64–80px below the start of the column content. It spans the full width of the left column, leaving a small inner horizontal padding on both sides consistent with other inputs. Vertically, it has about 8–12px of space between its label and the select box, and 16–24px below it before the next field begins. In the overall composition, it feels like the first actionable decision point in the form, anchored just under the messaging area, aligning with the mockup’s pattern of a prominent intro followed by primary content on the left. On mobile, it remains one of the topmost visible form controls, spanning nearly 100% of the content width with consistent spacing above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left form column; it participates in normal document flow, shifting down only when status panels expand above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form serves as the main structural wrapper for the interactive elements in the left column of the white content section. It starts just below any section heading and is anchored at the top-left of the left column, occupying approximately 60–65% of the container width. The form’s visible top edge aligns with the left column’s grid, sitting about 48px below the section’s top padding and around 64px below the bottom edge of the hero. The form extends vertically down the page, with its fields stacked in a single column inside this left region, leaving a generous right gutter between the form’s edge and the right decorative column. Internally, the form maintains consistent vertical spacing: small gaps (8–16px) between labels and fields, moderate gaps (16–24px) between related fields, and slightly larger gaps (24–32px) before major elements like the message textarea and the submit button. On mobile, the form becomes a full-width column centered in the viewport, with its left and right padding maintaining the same feeling of spacious margins as in the desktop mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container; defines the left column’s interactive stack and grows vertically with its contents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message-textarea is positioned in the lower half of the contact form’s left column stack, following the primary contact details and topic dropdown. It spans the full width of the left column (minus standard inner padding), visually forming the largest single input area and echoing the tall imagery used on the right side of the mockup’s two-column layout. There is a larger vertical gap (about 24px) above it to signal a transition from shorter fields to a long-form message area, and about 24–32px of space below before the submit button. The textarea height is generous, roughly equivalent to 4–6 lines of text by default, giving it a substantial vertical presence that balances the right column’s decorative content. On mobile, it continues to span the full width of the content area and becomes one of the most visually dominant form elements, reinforcing its importance as the main place to write a question or request.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; expands vertically with content if configured, but generally maintains a defined minimum height to preserve the intended vertical weight.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-context-hidden-field sits inside the contact form structure, typically near the bottom of the form markup so it can carry pre-filled context from other pages. Visually, it is completely hidden: it has no visible label, no border, and no reserved space, so the layout appears seamless to the user. It does not alter the spacing between the message-textarea and the submit button; those two elements appear to sit directly adjacent in the vertical flow. On all devices, the field remains invisible and does not affect alignment or overall form height beyond any minimal technical footprint that is not perceptible to the user.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visible form control; occupies no intentional visual space and remains inline in the DOM inside the contact-form without affecting visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-error-list appears within the left column, at the top of the contact form area, just below any section heading and above the first visible form field such as the topic-dropdown. When errors exist, it presents as a horizontally full-width panel relative to the left column, with a subtle but clear boundary (e.g., soft red tint and border) and generous inner padding (16–24px) that matches the editorial, spacious feel of the mockup. There is about 16–24px of vertical space above it (from the section heading) and about 24px below before the first field, ensuring it is visually distinct yet integrated. It aligns with the left column grid so that its left edge lines up with the input fields below. When no errors are present, the list collapses entirely, freeing that vertical space so the first field moves up. On smaller screens, it remains at the top of the stacked form content, spanning the full width of the content area and pushing other elements downward when expanded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered panel; appears and disappears in the normal vertical flow, pushing subsequent form elements down when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-panel is located in the same general position as the validation-error-list, near the top of the form in the left column of the white content section. When active (after a successful submission or key status change), it appears directly above the validation-error-list (if both are shown) or in its place when no errors are present. It spans the full width of the left column and uses a calm, success-oriented styling with ample padding (16–24px) and clear typography, mirroring the spacious editorial blocks used in the mockup’s textual sections. There is a modest vertical gap (16–24px) above it from any form heading and around 24px below it before the first field, creating a clear yet integrated status zone at the top of the interactive area. When inactive, it collapses completely, allowing the form fields or error list to shift up. On mobile, it also appears at the very top of the stacked form content, pushing other elements down and remaining highly visible within the single-column flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered, collapsible block; participates in normal document flow and vertically repositions subsequent components when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit-button is positioned at the bottom of the contact form’s left column stack, directly below the message-textarea (and any other concluding fields) and above the back-to-home link. It is left-aligned with the other inputs in the left column, creating a clean vertical line of alignment that echoes the typographic column in the mockup. Horizontally, it occupies only the width necessary for its label, plus generous horizontal padding, leaving whitespace to its right that reinforces the editorial look. Vertically, there is a clear gap of about 24–32px above it from the last field and about 24–32px below it before the back-to-home link, giving the button room to stand out as the primary action. Its size and color ensure it reads as the main call-to-action without overwhelming the calm, spacious layout. On mobile, it remains left-aligned relative to the content column (not stretched full-width unless the design system dictates), preserving the feeling of an editorial page with a clear action anchor.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; acts as the final element in the interactive stack before navigation links, shifting downward as preceding elements expand.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is placed immediately below the submit-button within the left column of the white content section, acting as a soft secondary escape route. It is left-aligned with the button and other form fields, maintaining the vertical alignment of the text column. There is a modest vertical gap (about 24–32px) between the bottom of the submit button and the link, giving the link a distinct but understated presence. The link uses smaller, lighter-weight typography than the main form labels and does not have a large hit-area, making it visually secondary to the primary action. It sits near the bottom of the main content section, with additional bottom padding (about 48–64px) before the end of the page, which frames it with generous whitespace in line with the mockup’s low-density layout. On mobile, it remains directly under the button, left-aligned within the single-column stack and easy to discover after scanning past the primary call-to-action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element within the left column; it stays at the bottom of the form content and moves down if the form height increases.

name: About the Attraction
title: About the Attraction
description: Provides visitors with background information about the Christmas attraction, including its history, mission, event overview, key experiences, and links to planning pages.

core_features: Fetch and display about-page content (intro, history, mission, event dates, daily schedule summary, charitable partnerships, key experiences, and related links) using getAboutAttractionContent.
Present structured sections for History, Mission, Event Dates & Daily Schedule, Charitable Partnerships, and Key Experiences, populated entirely from the API response.
Render a list of key experiences from the keyExperiences array, allowing users to expand or collapse individual experience descriptions.
Display planning/navigation links based on relatedPageLinks, mapping logical targetPage values to actual site URLs such as tickets.html, schedule.html, and accessibility.html.
Support direct navigation to specific sections of the page via URL parameters (for example, highlighting and scrolling to the requested section) without using localStorage.
Expose in-page navigation controls that update URL parameters when users jump between sections, keeping the page state shareable/bookmarkable.
user_workflows: Workflow: Read general overview of the attraction 1) User navigates to about.html (optionally with URL parameters). 2) The page initializes and the about-content-loader component calls getAboutAttractionContent with no parameters. 3) Once data is returned, the intro-section component renders introText. 4) The user scrolls or uses in-page navigation to read additional sections such as historySection and missionSection.

Workflow: Understand event dates and typical daily schedule 1) User navigates to about.html with or without URL parameters. 2) The page loads and getAboutAttractionContent returns eventDatesSummary and dailyScheduleSummary. 3) The schedule-summary-section component displays these summaries. 4) The user may click the 'See Daily Activities' planning link from relatedPageLinks, triggering navigation to schedule.html.

Workflow: Learn about charitable partnerships 1) User opens about.html. 2) The content loader fetches data via getAboutAttractionContent, including charitablePartnerships. 3) The charitable-partnerships-section renders charitablePartnerships as provided. 4) If the URL parameter section is set to the charitable partnerships section identifier, the page automatically scrolls to and highlights this section.

Workflow: Explore key experiences 1) User opens about.html or jumps to it from another page using a section URL parameter that targets the key experiences section. 2) getAboutAttractionContent returns the keyExperiences array. 3) The key-experiences-list component renders each experience title and its description in a collapsible row. 4) The user clicks on an experience row to toggle an expanded/collapsed state for that experience’s description while other sections remain unchanged.

Workflow: Navigate to planning pages from About 1) User scrolls to the planning/navigation area (e.g., related links panel). 2) The related-links-panel component uses relatedPageLinks from getAboutAttractionContent to render links. 3) When the user clicks the link corresponding to Tickets & Pricing, the browser navigates to tickets.html according to the defined mapping for that targetPage value. 4) Similarly, clicking links corresponding to daily activities or accessibility navigates to schedule.html or accessibility.html.

Workflow: Deep link to a specific About section 1) Another page or an external site links to about.html with a URL parameter indicating a specific section (for example, about.html?section=<section-id>). 2) On initialization, the state-sync-controller component reads URL parameters and determines the initial active section. 3) After getAboutAttractionContent resolves and all content is rendered, the controller scrolls to the referenced section and marks it as active in the in-page-section-navigation component. 4) If the user later clicks another section in the in-page navigation, the URL parameter is updated to reflect the newly active section.
interactions: On page load, the about-content-loader component calls getAboutAttractionContent and updates the page with loading, success, or error states based on the response.

Users can click items in the in-page-section-navigation component to jump to specific sections (e.g., history, mission, key experiences); the click both scrolls the page and updates the section URL parameter.

Users can scroll manually through the content; as sections enter the viewport, the state-sync-controller can optionally update the active section in in-page-section-navigation and synchronize the URL parameter without a full reload.
Within the key-experiences-list component, users can click on a key experience header or a dedicated toggle control to expand or collapse that experience’s description.

In the related-links-panel component, users can click on planning links (e.g., to view tickets, schedules, or accessibility information). Each click triggers navigation to the appropriate page (tickets.html, schedule.html, or accessibility.html) using standard link navigation.
If an error occurs while calling getAboutAttractionContent, the about-content-loader displays an error state and a retry control; clicking the retry control triggers getAboutAttractionContent again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page derives its state solely from the URL and the response of getAboutAttractionContent. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. The base URL is about.html. Optional query parameters control in-page focus and behavior: 1) section=<section-id> selects which major content section should be considered active and scrolled into view after content is loaded (for example, section identifiers corresponding to intro, history, mission, schedule, partnerships, or key experiences). The state-sync-controller reads this parameter on initial load and when the URL changes; after getAboutAttractionContent data has been rendered, it scrolls to the matching section if it exists and informs in-page-section-navigation which section is active. When the user clicks a section link in the in-page navigation, the in-page-section-navigation updates the section parameter in the URL using the History API (or a full navigation, depending on implementation) and notifies the state-sync-controller of the change. 2) Any additional URL parameters not explicitly recognized are ignored by this page’s logic and do not affect functionality. Section identifiers themselves are defined within the client logic (for example, stable IDs for each section) and do not represent static content values; they are only used to map between URL parameters and DOM sections. All textual content (including labels of links to other pages) is sourced from getAboutAttractionContent, and no hardcoded textual content is used for these areas.

components.functionality: Responsible for fetching all content for the About page using getAboutAttractionContent, managing loading and error states, and distributing the returned data to child components (intro, history, mission, schedule summary, charitable partnerships, key experiences, and related links).
event_handlers: onInit: triggered when about.html is loaded; calls getAboutAttractionContent with no parameters and sets internal state to loading until a response or error is received.

onLoadSuccess: triggered after a successful API response; passes the data to content sections (intro-section, history-section, mission-section, schedule-summary-section, charitable-partnerships-section, key-experiences-list, related-links-panel) and notifies state-sync-controller that content is ready for section-based scrolling.
onLoadError: triggered if getAboutAttractionContent fails; displays an error message and a retry control instead of content sections.
onRetryClick: re-invokes getAboutAttractionContent when the user chooses to retry loading content after an error.

components.functionality: Displays the introductory text for the attraction, giving users a concise overview of what the attraction is, sourced from introText.
event_handlers: onRender: updates the intro section content when introText changes or when about-content-loader transitions from loading to loaded.
onSectionActivated: triggered by state-sync-controller when the intro section is set as the active section; ensures it can be scrolled into view and marked as active in in-page-section-navigation.

components.functionality: Shows the historySection content, allowing visitors to read about the origin and evolution of the attraction.
event_handlers: onRender: fills the section with historySection when data is available from about-content-loader.
onSectionActivated: called by state-sync-controller when the URL parameter indicates this section; scrolls into view and sets any accessibility attributes indicating active state.

components.functionality: Renders the missionSection content, explaining the purpose and goals of the attraction as provided by the API.
event_handlers: onRender: renders missionSection text when data is available.
onSectionActivated: triggered when this section is selected via the section URL parameter or via in-page navigation, causing the page to focus/scroll to this section.

components.functionality: Displays a high-level overview of event timing, combining eventDatesSummary and dailyScheduleSummary to inform visitors about when the attraction runs and typical daily patterns.
event_handlers: onRender: updates the section when eventDatesSummary or dailyScheduleSummary is received.
onSectionActivated: invoked when the section is targeted via URL parameter; scrolls into view and sets the active state for navigation highlighting.
onSeeActivitiesLinkClick: optional handler if the section includes a control that programmatically triggers the same navigation behavior as clicking the related link to schedule.html.

components.functionality: Shows information about the attraction’s charitablePartnerships, giving users context about any community or charitable aspects.
event_handlers: onRender: populates the section with charitablePartnerships content when available.
onSectionActivated: called when this section is indicated by the current section URL parameter, focusing and highlighting it as needed.

components.functionality: Renders the keyExperiences array as a list of experiences. Each item shows a title by default and allows the user to expand or collapse the associated description. This orients new visitors to major experiences available at the attraction.
event_handlers: onRender: constructs the list of key experiences once keyExperiences is available from about-content-loader.
onExperienceToggleClick: triggered when the user clicks on an experience item or toggle control; changes that item’s expanded/collapsed state and updates ARIA attributes for accessibility.

onSectionActivated: called by state-sync-controller when the key experiences section is selected via URL parameter; scrolls the list into view and signals in-page-section-navigation to highlight this section.

components.functionality: Displays navigation links derived from relatedPageLinks, guiding users to planning pages such as Tickets & Pricing, Schedule & Activities, and Accessibility. It maps logical targetPage values to concrete page URLs, allowing the labels to be fully controlled by the API.
event_handlers: onRender: iterates over relatedPageLinks and creates navigation entries. For each item, it maps the targetPage identifier to its corresponding URL (for example, mapping a tickets-related targetPage to tickets.html, a schedule-related targetPage to schedule.html, and an accessibility-related targetPage to accessibility.html). The mapping logic itself does not hardcode human-readable text; it only associates identifiers to route paths.

onLinkClick: when a user clicks a navigation item, the handler performs a standard navigation to the mapped URL (e.g., tickets.html, schedule.html, accessibility.html) in accordance with the outgoing_connections definition.
onSectionActivated: optional; if the panel is treated as a section, this event can scroll it into view when referenced by the section URL parameter.

components.functionality: Provides a compact navigation control listing major sections of the About page (intro, history, mission, schedule summary, charitable partnerships, key experiences). Users can click section items to jump to specific parts of the page; the component also highlights the currently active section based on scroll position or URL parameters.
data_binding: getAboutAttractionContent (used indirectly to determine which sections are present and should appear in navigation)
event_handlers: onRender: initializes the navigation items corresponding to available sections after about-content-loader has successfully fetched data.
onSectionNavClick: triggered when the user selects a section from the navigation; scrolls to the target section and invokes state-sync-controller to update the section URL parameter.

onActiveSectionChange: invoked by state-sync-controller when the active section is changed by scroll or URL change; updates the visual and ARIA state of navigation items to reflect which section is currently active.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameters and in-page section state. It reads the URL on load, determines the initial section to focus, waits until getAboutAttractionContent has loaded and sections are rendered, then scrolls to the requested section. It also listens for section changes initiated by user interaction and updates the URL parameter to keep state shareable.
event_handlers: onInit: executed on page load; parses URL parameters, especially section, and stores the desired initial active section.

onContentReady: triggered by about-content-loader after data has been loaded and major sections have been rendered; if a valid section parameter is present, scrolls to that section and informs in-page-section-navigation of the active section.

onSectionChangeFromNav: called by in-page-section-navigation when the user selects a new section; uses the History API or a full reload to update the section query parameter without modifying other parameters, then scrolls to the chosen section.

onScroll: optional handler that observes which section is in view; when it detects a new active section, it updates the in-page-section-navigation and may update the section URL parameter to reflect the current position.
onUrlChange: listens for changes to the URL (e.g., via browser navigation); when the section parameter changes, updates the active section and scrolls accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). The hero spans full-width, but inner content appears constrained within a central column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. A full-width, red bokeh hero-style intro section sits beneath the global navigation, acting as the visual anchor for the About page. Immediately below the hero, the layout transitions onto a white background with a centered max-width content container. Within this container, sections are stacked vertically in a single column, with occasional two-column splits: (1) a strong two-column history section (text-dominant left, image right), and (2) a later two-column band pairing the key experiences list on the left with a smaller related links panel on the right. The in-page section navigation sits just below the hero, above the main content sections, functioning as a horizontal anchor bar into the rest of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. A full-width, red bokeh hero-style intro section sits beneath the global navigation, acting as the visual anchor for the About page. Immediately below the hero, the layout transitions onto a white background with a centered max-width content container. Within this container, sections are stacked vertically in a single column, with occasional two-column splits: (1) a strong two-column history section (text-dominant left, image right), and (2) a later two-column band pairing the key experiences list on the left with a smaller related links panel on the right. The in-page section navigation sits just below the hero, above the main content sections, functioning as a horizontal anchor bar into the rest of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero intro occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding around the main page title. Below the hero, all content is contained within a centered max-width wrapper (~1100–1200px) with substantial left/right whitespace. Vertical spacing between major sections (history, mission, schedule, charitable, experiences) uses 48–64px of separation. Within sections, headings, sublines, and paragraphs maintain 8–16px vertical spacing, preserving a clear, breathable reading rhythm. In two-column sections, the primary content column typically occupies about 60–65% of the container width (7–8 of 12 grid columns), and the secondary column occupies about 35–40% (4–5 grid columns).
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is low-to-medium, optimized for reading and reflection rather than packing in information. Paragraphs have comfortable line lengths (around 55–65 characters) and are well spaced. The history and mission sections read like editorial features, with no text crowding images. Interactive elements, like the key experiences list, are still visually calm, with generous padding around list items and consistent vertical rhythm. The related links panel is compact but not cramped, using white space to visually group links without overwhelming the page.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey starts at the top-left logo and main site navigation (outside this layout), then moves across to any global nav links, before being pulled down into the bold, centered hero title in the intro section. From there, the eye drops to the in-page section navigation bar, which hints at scannable anchors. As users scroll, the eye is drawn first to left-aligned section headings (e.g., 'Origins of Santa's Castle'), then naturally down through author lines and intros, before noticing the supporting imagery or secondary columns on the right. This produces a soft Z-pattern: top navigation → central hero title → down-left to section headings → across to right-side imagery or panels, reinforcing the editorial feel while gently surfacing secondary content.

layout.overall_description: The About the Attraction page unfolds as a warm, editorial-style Christmas story. A full-width hero intro floats at the top with a rich red bokeh background and a large, centered page title framed by generous vertical space. Directly beneath it, the white content area begins, with a slim, centered in-page section navigation bar that lets visitors jump between History, Mission, Schedule, Experiences, and Charitable Partnerships. The main content lives inside a wide but contained central column, leaving comfortable margins on both sides. The first major section, History, uses a two-column layout: a broad left text column with an article-style heading, author line, and narrative paragraphs, and a slightly narrower right column holding a tall, historical holiday image aligned with the top of the text. As users scroll, sections like Mission and Schedule appear as spacious blocks of text, separated by generous vertical breathing room. Further down, the layout shifts again into a two-column band with key experiences on the left and a compact, card-like related links panel on the right, echoing the text-priority pattern from the mockup. The whole page feels cohesive and calm—festive at the top, then clean and readable throughout—with a consistent rhythm of whitespace, clear hierarchy, and left-weighted primary content balanced by lighter right-hand supporting elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader is a non-visual, structural component positioned logically at the very top of the About page’s content stack, just below the global site navigation layer. It does not occupy any visible width or height in the layout; instead, it initializes and manages the data for the intro, history, mission, schedule, charitable partnerships, key experiences, and related links sections. From a spatial perspective, all visible sections (starting with the hero-style intro-section) appear as if they render seamlessly starting at the top of the viewport, with no gap where the loader resides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; logically fixed at the top of the About page content tree but does not render any physical space or affect the visual flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro-section functions as the hero area for the About page. It spans the full browser width edge-to-edge, with a dramatic red bokeh/sparkle background image. Within this full-width band is a centered content container capped at approximately 1100–1200px in width, aligned centrally in the viewport. The hero’s vertical height on initial load is about 35–45% of the viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding (roughly 64–96px total) so the main heading breathes. The hero title 'About the Attraction' (or similar) is centered horizontally within the inner container and vertically positioned slightly above center to mirror the mockup’s 'Our History' emphasis. Supporting text (a short subtitle or tagline) sits directly beneath it, with 8–16px of vertical spacing. Above this hero, the global navigation and logo float as an overlay; the intro-section begins immediately below that nav with no visible gap. The overall effect is a bold, festive top area that anchors the page and visually separates the white content that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section spanning full width; its inner content remains centered and responsive. Background image scales with viewport width while maintaining a minimum height to preserve the hero’s dramatic feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section is the first major white-background content block immediately below the in-page section navigation. It sits within the same centered max-width container as the rest of the content, framed by wide left and right margins. The section begins with approximately 64px of top padding from the element above, ensuring a clear break from the navigation bar. The layout uses a two-column grid: the left column occupies around 60–65% of the container width (roughly 7–8 of 12 columns) and is dedicated to text, while the right column occupies about 35–40% (4–5 columns) and holds a tall, vertically oriented historical image. In the left column, a left-aligned section heading such as 'Origins of Santa's Castle' appears at the top, followed by an author line directly beneath it with about 8px of vertical space. Below the author line, an italic lead-in paragraph is separated by another 8–12px, leading into the main body text, which is broken into multiple paragraphs with 12–16px spacing between them. The text column maintains a comfortable reading width (~55–65 characters per line). The right column’s image is aligned to the top of the text block so that the top edges of the heading and the image line up horizontally. The image extends downward, typically taller than the block of text, reinforcing the vertical, editorial feel. There is at least 32px of horizontal gutter space between the two columns. The bottom of the section has 48–64px of padding before the next section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive two-column section. On narrower viewports, the layout collapses to a single column with the text appearing first and the image stacking below, preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section sits directly beneath the history-section, separated by approximately 56–64px of vertical white space to maintain a clear section break. It remains inside the same centered max-width container. Unlike the history section, the mission uses a single-column layout to emphasize uninterrupted reading. The content column is centered horizontally, with the main text area constrained to about 70–75% of the container width (roughly 8 of 12 columns) to maintain comfortable line lengths and preserve substantial left/right margins. At the top of the mission section, a left-aligned heading such as 'Our Mission & Values' appears with ample top margin from the preceding section. Directly beneath it, an optional short intro line or subheading may sit with 8–12px spacing. The main mission copy is organized into 2–4 paragraphs, each separated by 12–16px, with an overall feeling of openness. The section’s top and bottom padding combined are roughly 64–80px, ensuring the mission reads as its own calm, editorial block within the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, single-column section that stretches the full container width but visually emphasizes a centered text block. On mobile, the section simply narrows while preserving vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-summary-section appears below the mission-section with a vertical gap of about 56px. It also resides within the central max-width container on a white background. This section lightly echoes the two-column idea from the history layout but in a more compact, utilitarian way. The left side, occupying roughly 60% of the container width, holds a left-aligned heading such as 'Season Schedule at a Glance' followed by a short explanatory paragraph or two describing the seasonal dates and general hours, with standard 8–16px spacing between text elements. To the right, taking up roughly 40% of the container width, a visually distinct schedule summary panel is displayed as part of this component—a bordered or softly shaded box containing key dates or a simplified timetable. The right panel is aligned to the top of the left text block, with a horizontal gutter of about 24–32px between columns. Overall vertical padding above the heading and below the lowest content line is around 48–64px. The section feels structured yet still airy, making the schedule easy to scan without appearing dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static two-column section that collapses to a single column on smaller screens, with the descriptive text appearing first and the summary panel stacking beneath, maintaining comfortable padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The charitable-partnerships-section is placed immediately after the schedule-summary-section, separated by about 64px of vertical space to mark a thematic shift. It remains inside the same centered content container but may subtly differentiate itself with a very light tint or soft divider line at the top while retaining an overall white, clean feel consistent with the mockup. The layout returns to a single-column, editorial style: a left-aligned heading such as 'Charitable Partnerships' sits at the top of the section, followed by a short introductory paragraph and then either bullet-style highlights or brief paragraphs describing partner organizations and cause-related initiatives. Vertical spacing between the heading, intro, and subsequent paragraphs is around 8–16px. Logos or small partner marks, if used, line up horizontally or in a simple grid beneath the text but do not crowd it, with at least 16–24px padding around them. The total vertical padding of this section is slightly more generous (64–80px) to lend a sense of importance and reflection, while still fitting the spacious editorial rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, single-column block that flows naturally with the rest of the content. On smaller screens, any partner logos or highlights stack vertically with consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The key-experiences-list appears in the lower portion of the page, beneath the charitable-partnerships-section, and introduces a more interactive band of content. It shares a horizontal row with the related-links-panel, creating a two-column layout within the central container. This experiences list occupies the left, primary column, taking up about 60–65% of the container width (7–8 of 12 columns). At the top of this left column sits a left-aligned heading such as 'Key Experiences at Santa's Castle', followed by a short descriptive intro with 8–12px spacing. Below this, the interactive list items (such as expandable cards or hoverable rows) stack vertically with generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between each, so the list feels substantial but not crowded. Each list item maintains internal padding (16–24px) and aligns its content flush with the main text column. The column itself is aligned to the same left edge as previous sections, reinforcing the editorial rhythm. A horizontal gutter of about 32px separates this list from the right-hand related-links-panel. The bottom of the experiences column aligns visually with or slightly exceeds the bottom of the related links column, preserving a balanced band across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static primary column in a two-column row. On smaller screens, this component stacks above the related-links-panel and expands to nearly full container width, preserving its generous item spacing and interactive affordances.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-links-panel is positioned to the right of the key-experiences-list within the same horizontal band. It serves as a secondary, supportive column taking up about 35–40% of the container width (4–5 of 12 columns). The top of the panel lines up horizontally with the heading of the key experiences list. The panel is styled as a compact, card-like module: a heading such as 'Plan Your Visit' or 'Related Pages' appears at the top, left-aligned, followed by a vertical list of navigation links (to tickets, detailed schedule, FAQs, etc.). Each link item has consistent vertical spacing (8–12px) and sufficient padding (12–16px) so the panel reads as a tidy, breathable block rather than a dense menu. The panel’s background may be subtly differentiated (e.g., a very light tint or a border) to signal its navigational role while keeping the white, minimal aesthetic. The panel’s bottom edge generally aligns with or sits slightly above the bottom of the key experiences column to maintain a visually coherent row. There is a clear 24–32px gutter between this panel and the experiences list to prevent visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static secondary column in a two-column layout. On smaller screens, it stacks below the key-experiences-list and expands to near full width but retains its card-like appearance and internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page-section-navigation sits directly below the hero-like intro-section and just above the history-section, marking the transition from the festive hero into the editorial content area. It spans the full width of the central content container but not the full browser width, leaving generous side margins consistent with the main grid. Visually, it appears as a slim horizontal bar or row of pill-style links with labels like 'History', 'Mission', 'Schedule', 'Experiences', and 'Charitable Partnerships'. The navigation is left-aligned within the container, starting at the same left edge as the main text in subsequent sections, with about 24–32px of vertical padding above and below the nav items so it does not feel crowded. There is approximately 32–40px of white space between the bottom of this navigation and the top of the history-section heading. This bar subtly echoes the top global nav but is lighter and more integrated into the content, providing quick in-page anchors without overpowering the hero or sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky behavior: after the user scrolls past the hero, this navigation bar pins to the top of the viewport within the white content area, maintaining its width and alignment with the content container. On mobile, the nav condenses (e.g., into a horizontally scrollable row or compact dropdown) but remains sticky at the top for easy section jumping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state-sync-controller is a non-visual orchestration component that underpins interactive and navigational behaviors on the page. Logically, it sits alongside the about-content-loader near the top of the page hierarchy, managing state for the in-page section navigation, the key-experiences-list interactions, and any dynamic highlighting of sections as the user scrolls. It occupies no visible area in the layout and does not alter spacing; all visible components appear to flow naturally from the hero intro down through each section without any sign of this controller’s presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and layered behind the UI; it is effectively fixed in the application structure rather than the layout and has no physical dimensions or scroll behavior.

name: Frequently Asked Questions
title: Frequently Asked Questions
description: Page for browsing, filtering, and searching frequently asked questions, with links to related detail pages.

core_features: Load FAQ sections dynamically using getFaqSections and expose them as selectable filters (e.g., tabs or dropdown).
Load FAQ items dynamically using getFaqItems, filtered by the active sectionId and query URL parameters when present.
Provide a search form that updates the query URL parameter and refreshes FAQ results via getFaqItems.
Allow filtering by FAQ section, updating the sectionId URL parameter and reloading items via getFaqItems.
Keep FAQ state (active section and search query) fully synchronized with the URL so it can be shared, bookmarked, and navigated with browser back/forward.
Display FAQ items in an expandable list (e.g., accordions), showing questions and answers returned by getFaqItems.
Handle empty-result states when getFaqItems returns no items for the current filters, with options to clear or adjust filters.
Provide context-specific navigation links from FAQ answers to related pages (tickets.html, getting_here.html, accessibility.html, groups.html) based on the FAQ section.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from another page with a preselected section
1) User clicks an FAQ link on another page that includes a sectionId URL parameter.
2) FAQ page reads sectionId from the URL on load.
3) Page calls getFaqSections() to obtain available sections.
4) Page sets the active section to the section whose id matches the sectionId parameter (if any).
5) Page calls getFaqItems({ sectionId: activeSectionId, query: undefined }) to load relevant items.
6) User sees only FAQs from that section and can expand items to view answers.
7) User may change the section filter or perform a search, which updates the URL parameters and reloads data via getFaqItems.

Workflow: Arrive with a pre-filled search query
1) User submits a search from index.html that navigates to faq.html with a query URL parameter.
2) FAQ page reads query (and sectionId if present) from the URL on load.
3) Page calls getFaqSections() to build the section filter UI.
4) Page calls getFaqItems({ query: queryFromUrl, sectionId: sectionIdFromUrlOrUndefined }).
5) Search input is pre-filled with the query value from the URL, and results show only matching FAQs.
6) User can refine the search or add a section filter; each change updates the query/sectionId parameters and triggers a new getFaqItems call.

Workflow: Browse FAQs and refine results on-page
1) User opens faq.html without specific URL parameters.
2) Page initializes with empty filters, calls getFaqSections(), then calls getFaqItems({ query: undefined, sectionId: undefined }).
3) User selects a section from the sections filter component.
4) Page updates internal activeSectionId state, updates the sectionId parameter in the URL, and calls getFaqItems() with the selected sectionId.
5) User types a keyword into the search input and submits the search.
6) Page updates internal searchQuery state, sets the query parameter in the URL, and calls getFaqItems() with both sectionId and query.
7) User can clear filters (reset search and/or section), which removes the corresponding parameters from the URL and reloads all FAQs via getFaqItems().

Workflow: Open an FAQ answer and navigate to a related detail page
1) User sees a list of FAQs returned by getFaqItems for the current section and query.
2) User clicks on a question row to expand it; the answer text from the item is revealed.
3) The component checks the item’s sectionId or sectionName to determine if a related detail page is available (e.g., tickets, accessibility, getting here, groups) using a pre-defined mapping to outgoing page URLs.
4) If applicable, the component renders an action link or button such as a generic 'View related details' linked to the appropriate target page (e.g., tickets.html).
5) User clicks this action; the browser navigates to the configured target page (e.g., tickets.html) without modifying FAQ URL parameters.

Workflow: Use browser back/forward to navigate FAQ states
1) User performs a series of actions on faq.html: selects sections, runs searches, and clears filters; each action updates the URL parameters (query and/or sectionId) and triggers getFaqItems.
2) User clicks the browser Back button.
3) A popstate handler reads the current URL parameters (query and sectionId).
4) Page updates internal state to match the URL and calls getFaqItems() with the restored parameters.
5) FAQ list updates to reflect the previous state.
6) This behavior repeats as the user uses Back/Forward, ensuring that what they see always corresponds to the URL.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Browser loads faq.html.
- Behavior: Read sectionId and query from the URL (if present). Call getFaqSections(). After sections are loaded, call getFaqItems() with the URL-derived query and sectionId (or undefined if not set). Render the sections filter, search input (pre-filled with query if present), and FAQ list.

Interaction: Change active section filter
- Trigger: User clicks on a section in the sections filter component or selects it from a dropdown.
- Behavior: Update internal activeSectionId to the selected section’s id. Update the sectionId parameter in the query string (or remove it if user chooses an 'all sections' option). Call getFaqItems({ query: currentSearchQueryOrUndefined, sectionId: activeSectionIdOrUndefined }). Re-render FAQ list based on new results. Maintain focus and scroll behavior as appropriate.

Interaction: Submit search
- Trigger: User types a text query into the search input and either presses Enter or clicks a search button.
- Behavior: Update internal searchQuery state. Update the query parameter in the URL (or remove it if input is cleared). Call getFaqItems({ query: searchQueryOrUndefined, sectionId: activeSectionIdOrUndefined }). Update FAQ list with the returned items.

Interaction: Clear search
- Trigger: User clicks a 'clear search' control within the search component or manually deletes the query text and submits.
- Behavior: Set internal searchQuery to an empty value. Remove query from the URL parameters. Call getFaqItems({ query: undefined, sectionId: activeSectionIdOrUndefined }). FAQ list refreshes to show all items for the current section.

Interaction: Reset all filters
- Trigger: User clicks a 'reset filters' control in the filters/summary area.
- Behavior: Set internal searchQuery and activeSectionId to undefined or their default states. Remove both query and sectionId from the URL parameters. Call getFaqItems({ query: undefined, sectionId: undefined }). Render FAQ list for unfiltered results and clear any filter indicators.

Interaction: Expand/collapse FAQ item
- Trigger: User clicks on a question row or an expand/collapse control next to a question in the FAQ list.
- Behavior: Toggle an internal expanded/collapsed state for that FAQ item id. The corresponding answer text (from getFaqItems result) is shown or hidden. This action does not change URL parameters or cause additional API calls.

Interaction: Navigate to related detail page from an FAQ
- Trigger: Within an expanded FAQ answer, user clicks a context-specific link or button labeled generically (e.g., a 'More details' style action) that the component renders based on the item’s section context.
- Behavior: Client resolves the target URL using a mapping between the FAQ section and the outgoing target page (e.g., tickets.html, getting_here.html, accessibility.html, groups.html). Browser navigates to that URL. No API calls occur from this interaction.

Interaction: Respond to browser back/forward navigation
- Trigger: User presses the browser Back or Forward button while on faq.html.
- Behavior: A popstate listener reads the updated URL parameters (query and sectionId). Internal state is updated to match these parameters, and getFaqItems() is called with the restored filters. The FAQ list, search input, and selected section filter all update to remain consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The FAQ page state is entirely controlled via URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page reads two optional URL parameters: sectionId and query. These are used to initialize internal state variables activeSectionId and searchQuery. The page then calls getFaqSections() once to obtain the list of available FAQ sections; this result is stored in memory and used to render the section filter UI. After sections are available (or in parallel), the page calls getFaqItems(), passing sectionId and query only if they are currently defined and non-empty in state. Whenever the user updates the search query or section filter, the corresponding parameter (query and/or sectionId) in the URL is updated via the browser's history API or by constructing a new query string. If a filter is cleared, that parameter is removed from the URL. Each URL change that reflects a user action triggers a new getFaqItems() call with parameter values derived from the updated URL. A popstate listener ensures that using browser back/forward restores activeSectionId and searchQuery from the URL, which again drives getFaqItems() to refresh the list.
